topics.title: The importance of hydrogen as a clean energy carrier
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT832 - HYDROGEN AND FUEL CELL VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** The importance of hydrogen as a clean energy carrier

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hydrogen as a Clean Energy Carrier (KTU Syllabus Definition):**
> Hydrogen (H₂) is the **lightest and most abundant chemical element** in the universe. As an *energy carrier* (NOT a primary energy source), it stores and transports energy produced from other sources. When used in a **fuel cell** or combusted with oxygen, its only by-product is **water (H₂O)** and/or negligible amounts of **NOₓ**, making it a *zero-carbon* energy vector critical for the global energy transition.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (Frequently Asked in KTU Exams):**
> - **Energy Source:** Naturally available form (coal, crude oil, sunlight, wind, uranium).
> - **Energy Carrier:** Stores/releases energy produced elsewhere (electricity, hydrogen, gasoline).
> Hydrogen is to the future what electricity is today — a clean, versatile *carrier*.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine electricity flowing through wires from a power plant to your home. The plant generates the energy, but **electricity itself is the carrier** that delivers it. Hydrogen plays an identical role in the emerging clean economy:
- **Sun/Wind (Source)** → produces electricity → splits water into H₂ via electrolysis.
- The H₂ molecule **locks away** that renewable energy inside its chemical bonds.
- Later, a fuel cell or combustion engine **unlocks** that energy on demand, releasing only water vapor.

Think of hydrogen as a **"rechargeable chemical battery"** — except it refills in minutes, weighs almost nothing, and emits no CO₂.

### 1.3 Critical Physical Constants & Metrics

| Property | Value | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Molecular weight | **2.016 g/mol** | Lightest molecule → highest mass energy density |
| Density (STP, gas) | **0.0899 kg/m³** | Very low → requires compression or liquefaction |
| Density (liquid, –253 °C) | **70.8 kg/m³** | 1/10th of water density |
| Higher Heating Value (HHV) | **142 MJ/kg** | ~3× gasoline on mass basis |
| Lower Heating Value (LHV) | **120 MJ/kg** | Used in fuel cell/ICE practical calculations |
| Auto-ignition temperature | **500 °C** | Higher than gasoline (≈ 250 °C) → safer in some respects |
| Flame speed | **2.5–3.5 m/s** | 8× faster than gasoline → better combustion |
| Flammability range in air | **4–75 % by volume** | Wider than any other fuel |
| Energy in 1 gallon gasoline-equivalent | ≈ **1 kg H₂** | Critical metric for vehicle range parity |

> [!NOTE]
> The **Higher Heating Value (HHV) of 142 MJ/kg** is a board-favorite constant. Remember it because KTU often asks comparative energy density questions using this value.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Hydrogen is Called a "Clean" Energy Carrier

The "clean" status stems from three structural advantages analyzed step-by-step:

**Step 1 — The Combustion Chemistry (Zero Carbon)**

When hydrogen burns, the reaction is:

$$\text{2H}_2 + \text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{2H}_2\text{O} + \text{Energy}$$

Notice: there is **no carbon atom** in either reactant. Therefore, **CO₂ cannot be produced**.

**Step 2 — The Fuel Cell Chemistry (Higher Efficiency, Zero Emissions)**

In a Proton Exchange Membrane (PEM) fuel cell, the electrochemical equivalent is:

$$\text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{Electrical Energy} + \text{Heat}$$

This directly converts chemical energy to electrical energy, **bypassing the Carnot limit** (efficiency 50–60 % vs. ICE at 25–35 %).

**Step 3 — Energy Density Superiority**

Per unit mass, hydrogen stores **~3× more energy than gasoline**. This is decisive for aviation, heavy transport, and long-range EVs.

**Step 4 — Versatility of End Use**

Hydrogen can power:
- Fuel Cell Electric Vehicles (FCEVs) — electricity
- Hydrogen Internal Combustion Engines (H₂-ICE) — mechanical
- Gas turbines — electricity grid balancing
- Industrial furnaces — process heat
- Power-to-gas — synthetic methane production

**Step 5 — Decarbonization of "Hard-to-Abate" Sectors**

Batteries struggle with aviation, shipping, steel-making, and long-haul trucks due to **weight and charging time**. Hydrogen uniquely solves these because it stores energy **as a fluid**, not as solid-state chemistry.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Energy Content & Emissions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize the values in **bold** for Part A (3-mark) questions. The derivation examples are tested in Part B.

| Equation / Parameter | Formula | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| HHV of H₂ | $Q_{HHV} = 142$ | MJ/kg | Maximum thermal energy recoverable |
| LHV of H₂ | $Q_{LHV} = 120$ | MJ/kg | Practical fuel cell / ICE energy |
| Mass Energy Density Ratio | $R = \dfrac{E_{H_2}}{E_{fuel}}$ | Dimensionless | Compare H₂ vs fossil fuels |
| Volume Energy Density (gas, 700 bar) | $E_v = \rho_{gas} \cdot LHV$ | MJ/L | Vehicle range estimation |
| Volume Energy Density (liquid) | $E_v = 70.8 \times 120$ | MJ/L | Cryogenic tank design |
| CO₂ Avoided | $\Delta m_{CO_2} = m_{fuel} \cdot f_{C} \cdot \dfrac{44}{12}$ | kg | Emission savings calculation |
| Stoichiometric Air Requirement | $A/F = \dfrac{\text{mass of air}}{\text{mass of fuel}}$ | kg/kg | Combustion balance |

**Real-World Engineering Utility:**
The hydrogen economy is not science fiction. The **Toyota Mirai (2014–)**, **Hyundai Nexo**, and **Hyundai XCIENT Fuel Cell Truck** are production FCEVs using exactly these formulas to size their tanks. A typical FCEV stores **5–6 kg of H₂ at 700 bar**, giving a range of **500–650 km** — proving the energy density advantage is commercially exploitable.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Mass Energy Density of Hydrogen vs Gasoline

**Problem:** Prove that hydrogen contains ~3× more energy per kg than gasoline.

**Given (standard reference values):**
- LHV of hydrogen = $120$ MJ/kg
- LHV of gasoline ≈ $44$ MJ/kg

**Step-by-step logical evaluation:**

The mass energy density ratio between hydrogen and gasoline is defined as:

$$\begin{aligned}
R_{m} &= \frac{LHV_{H_2}}{LHV_{gasoline}} \\[4pt]
R_{m} &= \frac{120 \text{ MJ/kg}}{44 \text{ MJ/kg}} \\[4pt]
R_{m} &= 2.727 \approx 2.73
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore, **1 kg of hydrogen delivers ~2.73× more energy than 1 kg of gasoline**. Using HHV, this becomes:

$$\begin{aligned}
R_{m,HHV} &= \frac{142}{44} = 3.227 \approx 3.23
\end{aligned}$$

> This is why rockets (e.g., the NASA Space Shuttle) used liquid hydrogen — unmatched mass-energy performance.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Volume Energy Density of Liquid Hydrogen

**Problem:** Calculate the volumetric energy density of liquid hydrogen and compare with gasoline.

**Given:**
- Density of liquid H₂ = $\rho_{LH_2} = 70.8$ kg/m³
- LHV of H₂ = $120$ MJ/kg
- Density of gasoline = $\rho_{g} \approx 740$ kg/m³
- LHV of gasoline = $44$ MJ/kg

**Step 1 — Volume energy density of liquid hydrogen:**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{v,LH_2} &= \rho_{LH_2} \times LHV_{H_2} \\[4pt]
E_{v,LH_2} &= 70.8 \text{ kg/m}^3 \times 120 \text{ MJ/kg} \\[4pt]
E_{v,LH_2} &= 8496 \text{ MJ/m}^3
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Volume energy density of gasoline:**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{v,gas} &= \rho_{g} \times LHV_{gas} \\[4pt]
E_{v,gas} &= 740 \text{ kg/m}^3 \times 44 \text{ MJ/kg} \\[4pt]
E_{v,gas} &= 32560 \text{ MJ/m}^3
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — The critical insight — the volume ratio:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{E_{v,gas}}{E_{v,LH_2}} &= \frac{32560}{8496} = 3.83
\end{aligned}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Trap:** Students often quote the mass-energy density advantage (3×) without addressing the **volume penalty (3.83× worse)**. A vehicle tank of equal *energy content* must be **~4× larger** for liquid hydrogen than gasoline. This is why **700 bar compressed gas tanks** are standard in FCEVs.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — CO₂ Avoidance Using Hydrogen Instead of Gasoline

**Problem:** A car travels 100 km burning 8 L of gasoline. Calculate the CO₂ saved if it instead uses hydrogen.

**Given:**
- Density of gasoline = $0.74$ kg/L
- Carbon fraction in gasoline by mass, $f_C \approx 0.85$
- Molar mass C = 12, molar mass CO₂ = 44

**Step 1 — Mass of gasoline burned:**

$$\begin{aligned}
m_{gas} &= 8 \text{ L} \times 0.74 \text{ kg/L} = 5.92 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Mass of carbon in that gasoline:**

$$\begin{aligned}
m_C &= m_{gas} \times f_C = 5.92 \times 0.85 = 5.032 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Mass of CO₂ produced (using stoichiometry C + O₂ → CO₂):**

$$\begin{aligned}
m_{CO_2} &= m_C \times \frac{44}{12} \\[4pt]
m_{CO_2} &= 5.032 \times 3.667 \\[4pt]
m_{CO_2} &= 18.45 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Net CO₂ saved using hydrogen:**

A hydrogen FCEV emits **0 kg CO₂** from the tailpipe (only water). Hence:

$$\Delta m_{CO_2} = 18.45 \text{ kg per 100 km}$$

> Annualized for a 15,000 km/year vehicle: ≈ **2.77 tonnes of CO₂ avoided per car per year**.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Energy Stored in a Typical FCEV Tank

**Problem:** A Toyota Mirai stores 5 kg of H₂ at 700 bar. Compute the chemical energy equivalent.

**Step 1 — Total chemical energy:**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{total} &= m_{H_2} \times LHV \\[4pt]
E_{total} &= 5 \text{ kg} \times 120 \text{ MJ/kg} \\[4pt]
E_{total} &= 600 \text{ MJ}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Equivalent in kWh (1 kWh = 3.6 MJ):**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{kWh} &= \frac{600}{3.6} = 166.7 \text{ kWh}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Equivalent in liters of gasoline:**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{gas} &= \frac{600}{44} \approx 13.6 \text{ L of gasoline}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Engineering takeaway:** 5 kg H₂ ≈ 13.6 L of gasoline → supports a 500+ km drive in an efficient FCEV.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hydrogen as an Energy Carrier — System-Level Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcA[Renewable Energy Sources] --> elec[Electricity Generation]
    srcB[Natural Gas] --> reform[Steam Methane Reforming]
    srcC[Biomass] --> gasif[Gasification]
    elec --> elecStep[Electrolysis of Water]
    reform --> compH2[Hydrogen Stream]
    gasif --> compH2
    elecStep --> compH2
    compH2 --> storeComp[Compression 700 bar]
    storeComp --> lique[Liquefaction minus 253 C]
    lique --> transp[Transport via Pipeline Truck Ship]
    transp --> endApp
    storeComp --> endApp
    endApp{Final Application}
    endApp --> fcevApp[FCEV Mobility]
    endApp --> indApp[Industrial Heat]
    endApp --> gridApp[Grid Power Generation]
    endApp --> synApp[Synthetic Fuel Synthesis]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Hydrogen is *not* a source — it is manufactured (left side), transported (middle), and released (right side). The arrow into "Final Application" represents energy delivery, just like electricity through a wire.

---

### 4.2 Hydrogen Economy Value Chain — Block-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    p1[Production Stage] --> p2[Storage and Conditioning]
    p2 --> p3[Distribution and Transportation]
    p3 --> p4[End Use Applications]
    p4 --> p5[Only Water Vapor Emission]
    p5 -.-> env[Net Zero Carbon Environment]

    subgraph blockA [Production Methods]
    pA1[Green Electrolysis] -.-> p1
    pA2[Blue SMR with CCS] -.-> p1
    pA3[Gray SMR without CCS] -.-> p1
    end

    subgraph blockB [Storage Modes]
    pB1[Compressed Gas 350 700 bar] -.-> p2
    pB2[Liquid Cryogenic] -.-> p2
    pB3[Metal Hydrides] -.-> p2
    pB4[Underground Caverns] -.-> p2
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **color codes** of hydrogen are a high-frequency KTU topic:
> - **Green H₂:** Electrolysis powered by renewables → zero carbon.
> - **Blue H₂:** SMR + Carbon Capture & Storage → low carbon.
> - **Gray H₂:** SMR without CCS → high carbon (current industry standard).
> - **Pink/Purple H₂:** Nuclear-powered electrolysis.

---

### 4.3 Comparison Block Diagram — Energy Carrier Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    a1[Hydrogen H2] --> k1[Best mass energy density]
    a2[Electricity Li ion] --> k2[Best round trip efficiency]
    a3[Natural Gas CH4] --> k3[Mature infrastructure]
    a4[Gasoline C8H18] --> k4[High volume energy density]

    a1 --> attr1[Zero CO2 at point of use]
    a1 --> attr2[Renewable storage medium]
    a1 --> attr3[Versatile molecule]
    a1 --> attr4[Long duration energy buffer]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** *[KTU University Exam — June 2024]* Define an **energy carrier**. Why is hydrogen classified as an energy carrier and not as an energy source? Give two examples of primary energy sources used to produce hydrogen.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An **energy carrier** is a substance or medium that stores energy produced by a primary source and delivers it for end-use applications. Examples: electricity, hydrogen.
Hydrogen is not naturally available as a usable energy reserve (unlike coal, oil, or wind); it must be **extracted from compounds** like water (H₂O) or hydrocarbons (CH₄) using energy input. Hence it is an energy carrier, not a source.
Two primary sources: (1) **Renewable electricity** (solar/wind → electrolysis), (2) **Natural gas** (steam methane reforming). *(1 mark for definition + 1 mark for hydrogen classification + 1 mark for examples.)*

---

**Q2.** *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]* State the **Higher Heating Value (HHV)** and **Lower Heating Value (LHV)** of hydrogen and explain why they differ.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **HHV of H₂ = 142 MJ/kg**, **LHV of H₂ = 120 MJ/kg**.
- HHV assumes the water vapor produced is **condensed back to liquid**, releasing latent heat of vaporization (~22 MJ/kg).
- LHV assumes water remains as **vapor**, so this latent heat is not recovered.
- Difference = 142 − 120 = **22 MJ/kg**, which is exactly the enthalpy of condensation of the water produced per kg of H₂ burned.
- Fuel cells operate near 80 °C → only LHV is practically recoverable. *(1 mark HHV + 1 mark LHV + 1 mark reasoning.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Part B questions on this topic always test (i) properties and (ii) numerical comparison. Practice both below.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Energy Content & Emissions Analysis**

**(a)** *[7 marks]* Explain in detail the **key properties of hydrogen** that make it suitable as a clean energy carrier for transportation applications. Compare its mass-energy density with that of gasoline.

**Model Answer Outline (7 marks):**

1. **Zero-carbon emission:** Combustion/FC reaction produces only H₂O. (1 mark)
2. **Highest mass energy density:** 120 MJ/kg (LHV) vs gasoline 44 MJ/kg → **~2.73× advantage**. (1 mark)
3. **Lightest molecule:** M = 2.016 g/mol → excellent for aerospace. (1 mark)
4. **Wide flammability range (4–75 %):** Enables lean-burn engines. (0.5 mark)
5. **High flame speed (≈ 3 m/s):** Better combustion efficiency. (0.5 mark)
6. **Renewable feedstock:** Produced from water via electrolysis using solar/wind. (1 mark)
7. **Versatile end-use:** FCEV, ICE, turbine, industrial process. (1 mark)
8. **Conclusion + comparison table with gasoline.** (1 mark)

**(b)** *[7 marks]* A vehicle travels 500 km using 35 L of gasoline (density 0.74 kg/L, LHV 44 MJ/kg). Calculate:
(i) Total chemical energy used.
(ii) Mass of CO₂ emitted (assume carbon fraction 85 %).
(iii) The mass of hydrogen required to deliver the *same energy* and the resulting CO₂ saving.

**Step-by-step Model Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Total chemical energy of gasoline:**

$$\begin{aligned}
m_{gas} &= 35 \times 0.74 = 25.9 \text{ kg} \\[4pt]
E_{gas} &= 25.9 \times 44 = 1139.6 \text{ MJ}
\end{aligned}$$

**(2 marks for correct mass and energy)**

**(ii) Mass of CO₂ emitted:**

$$\begin{aligned}
m_C &= 25.9 \times 0.85 = 22.015 \text{ kg} \\[4pt]
m_{CO_2} &= 22.015 \times \frac{44}{12} \\[4pt]
m_{CO_2} &= 22.015 \times 3.667 = 80.73 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}$$

**(2 marks for stoichiometric conversion)**

**(iii) Equivalent mass of hydrogen and CO₂ saving:**

$$\begin{aligned}
m_{H_2} &= \frac{1139.6}{120} = 9.50 \text{ kg} \\[4pt]
\Delta m_{CO_2} &= 80.73 - 0 = 80.73 \text{ kg per 500 km}
\end{aligned}$$

**(2 marks for hydrogen mass and 1 mark for CO₂ saving interpretation)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Hydrogen Economy & Decarbonization**

**(a)** *[7 marks]* Discuss the concept of the **"Hydrogen Economy"**. Explain its key pillars — production, storage, distribution, and end-use — with reference to the **colour codes** of hydrogen.

**Model Answer Outline (7 marks):**

1. **Definition:** A hydrogen economy is an energy system in which hydrogen serves as the primary clean energy carrier replacing fossil fuels. (1 mark)
2. **Pillar 1 — Production:** Green (electrolysis + renewables), Blue (SMR + CCS), Gray (SMR, current industry standard), Pink (nuclear-powered electrolysis). (2 marks)
3. **Pillar 2 — Storage:** Compressed gas (350/700 bar), liquid (cryogenic, –253 °C), metal hydrides, underground caverns. (1.5 marks)
4. **Pillar 3 — Distribution:** Pipelines, tube trailers, liquid tankers, ammonia/methanol carriers for long-distance shipping. (1 mark)
5. **Pillar 4 — End-Use:** Mobility (FCEV, H₂-ICE), industrial heat/feedstock, power generation, synthetic fuels. (1 mark)
6. **Conclusion on net-zero alignment.** (0.5 mark)

**(b)** *[7 marks]* With neat sketches/flow diagrams, describe the **role of hydrogen in decarbonizing the transport sector**. Justify why batteries alone cannot achieve full decarbonization.

**Model Answer Outline (7 marks):**

1. **Decarbonization challenge:** Transport contributes ≈ 25 % of global CO₂ emissions. (0.5 mark)
2. **Hydrogen pathway:** Green H₂ → FCEV → only water emitted. (1 mark)
3. **Sector-wise suitability:**
   - Light-duty cars → BEV competitive.
   - **Long-haul trucks, buses, trains, ships, aircraft** → H₂/FCEV essential due to weight and refueling time. (2 marks)
4. **Why batteries are insufficient:**
   - Mass-energy density: Li-ion ≈ 0.5–1 MJ/kg vs H₂ 120 MJ/kg. (1 mark)
   - Long charging time vs 5-minute H₂ refuel. (0.5 mark)
   - Battery degradation at extreme temperatures. (0.5 mark)
5. **Hydrogen-based synthetic fuels (e-SAF, e-methanol) for aviation and shipping.** (1 mark)
6. **Diagrammatic block representation of green H₂ → FCEV ecosystem.** (0.5 mark)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Carefully:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse "source" vs "carrier"** in definitions — it costs 1 full mark.
> 2. **Always cite the LHV (120 MJ/kg) for fuel-cell problems** and HHV (142 MJ/kg) only when the question specifies condensation recovery.
> 3. **Volume penalty must be acknowledged** when comparing H₂ with gasoline — omitting this loses 2 marks in (b) type questions.
> 4. **In CO₂ saving calculations**, always multiply by 44/12 (not 44/14 or any other ratio) — this is the single most common arithmetic error.
> 5. **Color codes (green/blue/gray)** must appear in any hydrogen economy answer — even one missing will cost 1 mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this checklist before entering the exam hall.**

- ✅ Hydrogen is an **energy carrier**, not an energy source — *frequently asked definition*.
- ✅ **HHV = 142 MJ/kg**, **LHV = 120 MJ/kg** — memorize both.
- ✅ **Mass energy advantage over gasoline ≈ 2.73× (LHV) or 3.23× (HHV).**
- ✅ **Volume energy penalty ≈ 3.8× worse** than gasoline — central to FCEV tank design.
- ✅ Combustion: 2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O + heat (zero CO₂).
- ✅ Fuel cell: H₂ + ½O₂ → H₂O + electricity + heat (~50–60 % efficient).
- ✅ Hydrogen colour codes: **Green (best), Blue, Gray (worst), Pink/Purple.**
- ✅ Stoichiometric ratio: C → CO₂ multiplier = **44/12 = 3.667**.
- ✅ Typical FCEV tank: **5 kg H₂ @ 700 bar → 500–650 km range**.
- ✅ Hard-to-abate sectors (aviation, shipping, steel) → **hydrogen is essential**, batteries alone insufficient.
- ✅ Hydrogen enables **seasonal renewable storage** (electricity cannot be cheaply stored for months).
- ✅ Auto-ignition temp 500 °C, flammability range 4–75 % — safety-relevant numbers.
- ✅ One gallon gasoline equivalent ≈ **1 kg of hydrogen**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic properties of hydrogen
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEMUT832 - HYDROGEN AND FUEL CELL VEHICLES > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Basic properties of hydrogen ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Hydrogen (H)** is the **lightest**, **simplest**, and **most abundant** chemical element in the universe. It is a **colorless**, **odorless**, **tasteless**, and **non-toxic** diatomic gas at Standard Temperature and Pressure (STP), with the chemical formula **H₂** and atomic number **1**. In the context of **OEMUT832 – Hydrogen and Fuel Cell Vehicles (KTU 2024 Scheme)**, hydrogen is fundamentally classified as an **energy carrier** rather than a primary energy source, because it does not exist in its free molecular form on Earth in significant quantities and must be **produced** from compounds (water, hydrocarbons, biomass) using an energy input. ### 1.2 Atomic & Molecular Identity - **Symbol:** H - **Atomic Number (Z):** 1 (one proton, one electron) - **Standard Atomic Weight:** **1.008 u** - **Electronic Configuration:** 1s¹ - **Group:** 1 (Alkali metals position in periodic table, though physically a non-metal) - **Period:** 1 - **Molecular Form (under STP):** **H₂** (dihydrogen) — bonded by a single covalent σ-bond - **Bond Dissociation Energy of H–H:** **436 kJ/mol** ### 1.3 Isotopes of Hydrogen | Isotope | Symbol | Nucleus | Stability | Natural Abundance | |---|---|---|---|---| | Protium | ¹H | 1 proton | Stable | **99.985 %** | | Deuterium | ²H (D) | 1p + 1n | Stable | 0.015 % | | Tritium | ³H (T) | 1p + 2n | Radioactive (t₁/₂ = 12.32 y) | Trace | > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Of the three isotopes, **Protium** is the relevant isotope for vehicular fuel-cell and combustion applications because of its overwhelmingly high natural abundance and lowest atomic mass (highest specific energy). ### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine hydrogen as the *universal currency* of the chemical world. Just as money does not grow on trees but is *minted* from raw materials through energy expenditure, hydrogen does not exist as a free gas in usable pockets on Earth. You must **manufacture** it (e.g., by splitting water using electricity), **store** it (in high-pressure tanks or cryogenic vessels), and **spend** it (in a fuel cell or internal combustion engine) to extract the energy originally invested during its production. Its "bank balance" — the **energy released per kilogram** — is extraordinarily high, which is why engineers consider it a premium future fuel. ### 1.5 Why Hydrogen? — Context for Automotive Use The four pillars that make H₂ attractive for vehicles are summarized below: 1. **Zero Tailpipe CO₂ Emissions:** Combustion or fuel-cell oxidation produces only **H₂O** (water vapor). 2. **Highest Gravimetric Energy Density** among practical fuels. 3. **Renewable Production Pathway:** Can be generated from water electrolysis using solar/wind electricity (**Green Hydrogen**). 4. **Fast Refueling** analogous to gasoline vehicles, unlike battery-electric charging limitations. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Fundamental Physical Properties of Hydrogen (H₂) The following structured analysis breaks down the physics behind each property, the **"why"** it matters for a fuel-cell/combustion vehicle, and the engineering consequence. #### 2.1.1 Density & Specific Volume - **Density at STP (0 °C, 101.325 kPa):** **ρ = 0.0899 kg/m³** - **Density at NTP (20 °C, 101.325 kPa):** ρ ≈ **0.0838 kg/m³** - **Specific Volume (v):** **v = 1/ρ ≈ 11.13 m³/kg at STP** - **Engineering Consequence:** Hydrogen is **~14× lighter than air** and **~8× lighter than gasoline vapor**. This mandates **large-volume storage tanks** for any meaningful mass of fuel. #### 2.1.2 Boiling & Melting Points (Critical for Cryogenic Storage) - **Boiling Point (1 atm):** **T_b = 20.28 K (–252.87 °C)** — the lowest boiling point of any fuel except helium. - **Melting Point (1 atm):** **T_m = 13.99 K (–259.16 °C)** - **Engineering Consequence:** Liquefied hydrogen (LH₂) requires **cryogenic storage at ~20 K**, demanding heavy vacuum-insulated tanks — about **30–40 % of total system mass** in typical LH₂ vehicles. #### 2.1.3 Energy Content (The Heart of Any Fuel Question) - **Higher Heating Value (HHV):** **142 MJ/kg** - **Lower Heating Value (LHV):** **120 MJ/kg** (water leaves as vapor) - **Volumetric Energy Density (gas, STP):** **10.8 MJ/m³** - **Volumetric Energy Density (liquid, 20 K):** **8.5 GJ/m³** - **Engineering Consequence:** Although **gravimetrically excellent**, hydrogen's **volumetric energy density is poor** in gaseous form — about **1/3000th that of gasoline** at atmospheric pressure. Hence the necessity of **700 bar compressed tanks** or **LH₂** or **cryo-compressed** storage. #### 2.1.4 Flammability & Ignition Characteristics - **Flammability Limit in Air:** **4 % to 75 % by volume** (widest range of any common fuel). - **Detonability Limit in Air:** **18.3 % to 59 % by volume.** - **Auto-Ignition Temperature:** **500 – 585 °C** - **Minimum Ignition Energy:** **0.017 mJ** (about **1/10 of gasoline's**). - **Flame Speed in Air:** **2.65 – 3.25 m/s** - **Diffusion Coefficient in Air:** **0.61 cm²/s** (≈ 4× that of gasoline vapor, hence leaks disperse quickly). - **Engineering Consequence:** Hydrogen's **wide flammability range** and **extremely low ignition energy** demand rigorous **leak detection, ventilation, and flame-arrestor designs** in vehicle fuel systems. #### 2.1.5 Diffusion & Permeation - Hydrogen is the **smallest molecule** (covalent radius ≈ 31 pm; kinetic diameter ≈ 289 pm). - It **leaks easily** through micro-gaps and even permeates through some metals. - **Engineering Consequence:** Mandates **specialized seals, gaskets, and metal liners** (e.g., aluminum or carbon-fiber composites with polymer inner liners) for high-pressure storage tanks. #### 2.1.6 Hydrogen Embrittlement - Hydrogen atoms diffuse into metal lattices, causing **loss of ductility, cracking, and reduced fatigue life**. - Most severe in **high-strength steels**. - **Mitigation:** Use of **austenitic stainless steels, aluminum alloys, or polymer-lined composite tanks**. ### 2.2 Comparison of Hydrogen with Conventional Automotive Fuels > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Expected Comparison:** Students are routinely asked to tabulate H₂ properties against gasoline, diesel, and methane. The following high-yield values are extracted from standard thermodynamic data (NIST & engineering handbooks). | Property | H₂ (H₂) | Gasoline | Diesel | Methane (CH₄) | LNG (propane equiv.) | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | Density at STP (kg/m³) | **0.0899** | ~750 (liq) | ~830 (liq) | 0.717 | ~493 (liq) | | Boiling Point (°C) | **–252.87** | 30–210 | 150–380 | –161.5 | –42 | | LHV (MJ/kg) | **120.0** | ~43.5 | ~42.5 | 50.0 | 46.3 | | HHV (MJ/kg) | **142.0** | ~47.5 | ~45.5 | 55.5 | 50.2 | | Flammability in Air (% vol) | **4 – 75** | 1.4 – 7.6 | 0.6 – 7.5 | 5 – 15 | 2.1 – 9.5 | | Auto-Ignition (°C) | **500 – 585** | ~280 | ~210 | 540 | 470 | | Min. Ignition Energy (mJ) | **0.017** | 0.25 | — | 0.28 | 0.25 | | Flame Speed (m/s) | **2.65 – 3.25** | 0.4 | — | 0.4 | 0.45 | | Octane Number | **130+ (RON)** | 92–98 | — | 120 | — | ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following compact table consolidates **all essential formulas** required for Module 1 calculation problems. Memorize the constants in **bold**. | # | Formula | Description | Typical Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\rho = \dfrac{m}{V}$ | Density definition | kg/m³ | | 2 | $v = \dfrac{1}{\rho}$ | Specific volume | m³/kg | | 3 | $\rho_{STP} = \dfrac{P \cdot M}{R \cdot T}$ | Ideal-gas density at STP | kg/m³ | | 4 | $E_{stored} = m \times \text{LHV}$ | Available chemical energy | J | | 5 | $\text{Stoichiometric A/F} = \dfrac{m_{air}}{m_{fuel}}$ | Air-to-Fuel mass ratio | dimensionless | | 6 | $\text{Energy per volume} = \rho \times \text{LHV}$ | Volumetric energy density | J/m³ | | 7 | $\phi = \dfrac{(A/F)_{actual}}{(A/F)_{stoich}}$ | Equivalence ratio | dimensionless | | 8 | $Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Sensible heat (storage) | J | | 9 | $W = P \cdot \Delta V$ | Compression work input | J | | 10 | $\eta_{th} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}}$ | Otto-cycle efficiency | dimensionless | | 11 | $T_{ad} = T_1 \cdot \dfrac{P_2}{P_1}^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ | Adiabatic compression temperature | K | | 12 | $h = \dfrac{k_B T}{m} \cdot t$ | Fick's diffusion estimate | m | **Critical Constants to Memorize:** - **Universal Gas Constant:** $R = 8.314 \, \text{J/(mol·K)}$ - **Molar Mass of H₂:** $M = 2.016 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{kg/mol}$ - **LHV of H₂:** **120 MJ/kg** ; **HHV: 142 MJ/kg** - **Adiabatic Index (γ) for H₂:** **1.405** - **Specific Heat c_p (H₂, 300 K):** **14.32 kJ/(kg·K)** (very high — heat-sink advantage in IC engines). --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of Hydrogen's Density at STP Using the Ideal Gas Equation The ideal gas equation relates pressure, volume, and temperature for one mole of an ideal gas: $$PV = nRT$$ For $n$ moles of hydrogen, substituting $n = \dfrac{m}{M}$ (where $m$ = mass in kg, $M$ = molar mass in kg/mol) yields: $$PV = \dfrac{m}{M}RT$$ Dividing both sides by $V$ and rearranging: $$\rho = \dfrac{m}{V} = \dfrac{P \cdot M}{R \cdot T}$$ Now substitute STP values: $P = 101325 \, \text{Pa}$, $M = 2.016 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{kg/mol}$, $R = 8.314 \, \text{J/(mol·K)}$, $T = 273.15 \, \text{K}$. $$\rho = \dfrac{101325 \times 2.016 \times 10^{-3}}{8.314 \times 273.15}$$ Step-by-step calculation: $$\rho = \dfrac{204.27}{2271.07}$$ $$\boxed{\rho_{H_2, STP} = 0.0899 \, \text{kg/m}^3}$$ **Engineering interpretation:** Because $\rho$ is directly proportional to $M$, and $M_{H_2}$ is the smallest of any gas, hydrogen's density is the lowest — explaining the need for **compression** (typically 350 bar or 700 bar) to achieve practical on-board storage. ### 3.2 Derivation of Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio for Hydrogen Combustion The complete combustion reaction in air is: $$H_2 + \frac{1}{2}(O_2 + 3.76\,N_2) \longrightarrow H_2O + 1.88\,N_2$$ In mole terms: - 1 mole of H₂ requires **0.5 mole of O₂** (pure oxygen basis) - In air, each mole of O₂ is accompanied by **3.76 moles of N₂** - Air required per mole of H₂ = $0.5 \times (1 + 3.76) = 2.38$ moles of air **Mass-basis calculation:** - Mass of 1 mole H₂ = $2.016 \times 10^{-3}$ kg - Mass of 2.38 moles air = $2.38 \times 28.97 \times 10^{-3}$ kg = $0.06895$ kg $$\text{Stoichiometric A/F} = \dfrac{m_{air}}{m_{fuel}} = \dfrac{0.06895}{0.002016}$$ $$\boxed{(A/F)_{stoich,\,H_2} \approx 34.3}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Critical Comparison:** Gasoline has a stoichiometric A/F of **~14.7**, diesel **~14.5**, methane **~17.2**. Hydrogen's A/F of **~34.3** is the **highest of all common fuels** — meaning that for the same energy released, an H₂ engine ingests ~2.3× more mass of mixture, requiring **larger throttle bodies and intake manifolds**. ### 3.3 Derivation of Heating Value from Combustion Enthalpy The LHV corresponds to the enthalpy of reaction at constant pressure producing **gaseous** water (298 K, 1 atm): $$H_2(g) + \frac{1}{2}O_2(g) \longrightarrow H_2O(g), \quad \Delta H = -241.8 \, \text{kJ/mol}$$ To convert to per-kg basis, divide by the molar mass: $$\text{LHV} = \dfrac{241.8 \, \text{kJ/mol}}{2.016 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{kg/mol}}$$ $$\text{LHV} = \dfrac{241.8}{0.002016} \, \text{kJ/kg}$$ $$\text{LHV} = 119{,}940 \, \text{kJ/kg} \approx 120 \, \text{MJ/kg}$$ $$\boxed{\text{LHV}_{H_2} \approx 120 \, \text{MJ/kg}}$$ For HHV, water condenses to liquid, releasing latent heat of vaporization ($h_{fg} = 44 \, \text{kJ/mol}$ at 1 atm): $$\text{HHV} = \text{LHV} + \dfrac{h_{fg}}{M_{H_2}} = 120 + \dfrac{44}{0.002016} \times 10^{-3} \, \text{MJ/kg}$$ $$\boxed{\text{HHV}_{H_2} \approx 142 \, \text{MJ/kg}}$$ ### 3.4 Derivation of Compressed-Hydrogen Tank Stored Mass Given a tank of volume $V$, storage pressure $P$, and ambient conditions $T$, $P_{amb}$, the ideal-gas mass stored is: $$m = \dfrac{(P - P_{amb}) V M}{R T_{amb}}$$ **Numerical Example:** A 700 bar (70 MPa) tank of $V = 0.122 \, \text{m}^3$ (≈ 122 L) at $T = 298 \, \text{K}$: $$m = \dfrac{70 \times 10^6 \times 0.122 \times 2.016 \times 10^{-3}}{8.314 \times 298}$$ Step-by-step: $$m = \dfrac{70 \times 10^6 \times 2.459 \times 10^{-4}}{2477.6}$$ $$m = \dfrac{17{,}215}{2477.6} \approx 6.95 \, \text{kg}$$ Energy stored: $E = m \times \text{LHV} = 6.95 \times 120 = 834 \, \text{MJ}$. At a typical fuel-cell vehicle consumption of ~1 kg H₂ per 100 km, this gives a range of approximately **695 km**. ### 3.5 Derivation of Adiabatic Compression Temperature of Hydrogen For reversible adiabatic compression: $$T_2 = T_1 \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$$ For hydrogen, $\gamma = c_p/c_v = 1.405$, so $(\gamma-1)/\gamma = 0.2884$. Compressing from $P_1 = 1$ bar to $P_2 = 700$ bar at $T_1 = 298 \, \text{K}$: $$T_2 = 298 \times (700)^{0.2884}$$ Compute stepwise: $$\ln(700) = 6.551, \quad 0.2884 \times 6.551 = 1.889, \quad e^{1.889} \approx 6.61$$ $$T_2 = 298 \times 6.61 \approx 1970 \, \text{K}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Note:** Real-gas effects and tank heat losses reduce this peak; nevertheless, **filling a 700 bar H₂ tank generates significant heat** (~10–15 °C rise per 10 MPa fill), which must be managed with **pre-cooling** at dispensing stations (–40 °C protocols used by Toyota Mirai, Hyundai Nexo). ### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Property Trade-offs in Vehicle Design The following matrix highlights how each property translates into engineering design decisions. | Property | Numerical Value | Engineering Implication | Vehicle-System Response | |---|---|---|---| | Low density | 0.0899 kg/m³ | Large volume needed | High-pressure or cryogenic tanks | | Wide flammability | 4 – 75 % | Leak hazard | Leak sensors, ventilation, flame arrestors | | Low ignition energy | 0.017 mJ | Easy ignition | Flame-arrestor inlines, safe filling | | High LHV | 120 MJ/kg | Light fuel mass | Range comparable to gasoline | | Low boiling point | 20.28 K | LH₂ boil-off | Vacuum-insulated cryo tanks | | Small molecule | d ≈ 0.289 nm | Permeation through seals | Polymer liners, metal gaskets | | High c_p | 14.32 kJ/(kg·K) | Suction-stroke cooling | Higher volumetric efficiency in H₂ICE | | High flame speed | 3.25 m/s | Knock resistance | High RON ≈ 130 allows high CR | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Diagram 1: Classification of Hydrogen by Production Pathway (Color Code) This diagram illustrates the internationally adopted **"hydrogen color code"** classification, which is frequently examined in Module 1. ```mermaid graph TD A[Energy Source] --> B[Hydrogen Production Pathway] B --> C[Brown Hydrogen] B --> D[Grey Hydrogen] B --> E[Blue Hydrogen] B --> F[Green Hydrogen] B --> G[Pink Hydrogen] C --> C1[Coal Gasification
No CCS] D --> D1[Steam Methane Reforming
No CCS] E --> E1[SMR with Carbon
Capture and Storage] F --> F1[Electrolysis using
Renewable Electricity] G --> G1[Electrolysis using
Nuclear Electricity] style A fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#000,color:#fff style B fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#000,color:#fff style C fill:#8c564b,stroke:#000,color:#fff style D fill:#7f7f7f,stroke:#000,color:#fff style E fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#000,color:#fff style F fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#000,color:#fff style G fill:#e377c2,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` ### 4.2 Diagram 2: Block-Level Functional Architecture of an H₂ Fuel-Cell Vehicle (Property-Driven) This block diagram maps how the **physical properties of hydrogen** dictate each subsystem's design. ```mermaid graph LR P1[Property 1
Low Density] --> S1[Storage Tank
700 bar CH2
or LH2 Cryogenic] P2[Property 2
Wide Flammability 4 to 75 pct] --> S2[Safety Subsystem
Leak Sensors
Flame Arrestors] P3[Property 3
Low Ignition Energy] --> S2 P4[Property 4
Small Molecule Size] --> S3[Sealing Subsystem
Polymer Liner
Composite Shell] P5[Property 5
High LHV 120 MJ per kg] --> S4[PEM Fuel Cell Stack
or H2 ICE] P6[Property 6
High Flame Speed 3.25 m per s] --> S5[Knock Resistant
H2 ICE Compression Ratio 12 to 15] S1 --> S6[Power Train
Electric Motor Drive] S2 --> S6 S3 --> S6 S4 --> S6 S5 --> S6 S6 --> O1[Vehicle Wheels
Zero CO2 Emission
Water Vapour Only] style P1 fill:#d62728,stroke:#000,color:#fff style P2 fill:#d62728,stroke:#000,color:#fff style P3 fill:#d62728,stroke:#000,color:#fff style P4 fill:#d62728,stroke:#000,color:#fff style P5 fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#000,color:#fff style P6 fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#000,color:#fff style O1 fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` ### 4.3 Diagram 3: Process Flow of H₂ Combustion & Energy Release ```mermaid graph LR A[H2 Gas
LHV 120 MJ per kg] --> B[Mixing with Air
A by F equals 34.3] B --> C[Combustion Chamber
Adiabatic Flame Temp 2400 K] C --> D[Energy Release
Mechanical Shaft Work] C --> E[Exhaust H2O Vapor
0 percent CO2] style A fill:#17becf,stroke:#000,color:#fff style B fill:#bcbd22,stroke:#000,color:#000 style C fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#000,color:#fff style D fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#000,color:#fff style E fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- #### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **State any six physical properties of hydrogen relevant to its use as a vehicular fuel.** **Model Answer (6 × 0.5 = 3 marks):** 1. **Lowest density** of any gas at STP: **ρ = 0.0899 kg/m³** — requires high-pressure storage. 2. **Colorless, odorless, tasteless** — leaks cannot be detected by human senses; odorants (e.g., ethyl mercaptan) are sometimes added. 3. **Non-toxic** but acts as a simple asphyxiant in confined spaces. 4. **Boiling point 20.28 K (–252.87 °C)** — lowest of all fuels; cryogenic storage needed for liquid H₂. 5. **Widest flammability range in air (4–75 %)** — poses leak-hazard risk. 6. **Auto-ignition temperature 500–585 °C** — high enough to be safely handled in engines. 7. **Highest flame speed (~3.25 m/s)** — supports efficient combustion in IC engines. 8. **Energy density LHV = 120 MJ/kg** — about 2.8 × gasoline. *(Any six with correct numerical values ⇒ 3 marks.)* --- #### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Compare the Lower Heating Value and stoichiometric air-fuel ratio of hydrogen with gasoline. State two engineering consequences for H₂ vehicles.** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Hydrogen (H₂) | Gasoline | |---|---|---| | LHV (MJ/kg) | **120** | ~43.5 | | Stoichiometric A/F | **34.3** | ~14.7 | **Engineering Consequences (2 marks):** 1. **Higher gravimetric energy but low volumetric density** ⇒ need **700 bar compressed tanks** to obtain practical driving range. 2. **Very high A/F ratio** ⇒ H₂ engines require **larger intake manifolds, throttle bodies, and injectors** to admit the larger air mass required per unit of fuel. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]** **(a) Derive the density of hydrogen at STP from the ideal gas equation and explain why this low density mandates high-pressure storage in fuel-cell vehicles.** **(7 marks)** **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Start with the ideal gas equation for $n$ moles:** $$PV = nRT, \quad n = \dfrac{m}{M}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute to get density:** $$\rho = \dfrac{m}{V} = \dfrac{PM}{RT}$$ **Step 3 — Insert STP values:** $P = 101325 \, \text{Pa}$, $M_{H_2} = 2.016 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{kg/mol}$, $R = 8.314 \, \text{J/(mol·K)}$, $T = 273.15 \, \text{K}$. $$\rho = \dfrac{101325 \times 2.016 \times 10^{-3}}{8.314 \times 273.15} = 0.0899 \, \text{kg/m}^3$$ **Step 4 — Engineering interpretation (3 marks):** - At 1 atm, 1 kg of H₂ occupies **11.13 m³** — impractically large for a vehicle tank. - For a typical FCEV range of 500 km, ~5 kg H₂ is needed → tank volume at 1 atm would be **55.6 m³** (impossible). - **Compression to 700 bar** reduces the volume by a factor of 700, giving **~0.08 m³ (~80 L)** — feasible using carbon-fiber-reinforced polymer (CFRP) tanks. - Therefore, hydrogen's inherently low density is the **primary physical driver** behind the design of high-pressure composite storage systems in FCEVs. **Conclusion:** $\boxed{\rho_{H_2, STP} = 0.0899 \, \text{kg/m}^3}$ — the lowest of any fuel gas, dictating the choice of **700 bar CFRP tanks** in production FCEVs (Toyota Mirai, Hyundai Nexo, Honda Clarity). --- **(b) The combustion of hydrogen in air produces water. Write the stoichiometric reaction, compute the stoichiometric air-fuel ratio on a mass basis, and discuss its effect on engine design.** **(7 marks)** **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Write stoichiometric equation:** $$H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}(O_2 + 3.76\,N_2) \to H_2O + 1.88\,N_2$$ **Step 2 — Compute molar A/F:** - Air moles per mole H₂ = $0.5 \times 4.76 = 2.38$ **Step 3 — Convert to mass basis:** - Mass of 2.38 mol air = $2.38 \times 28.97 \times 10^{-3} = 0.0689 \, \text{kg}$ - Mass of 1 mol H₂ = $2.016 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{kg}$ $$\text{A/F} = \dfrac{0.0689}{0.002016} \approx 34.2$$ $$\boxed{(A/F)_{stoich,\,H_2} \approx 34.2}$$ **Step 4 — Engine design implications (3 marks):** 1. **Higher air mass per unit fuel energy** requires **larger displacement intake manifolds** and wider throttle bores for the same power output. 2. Because H₂ has **very high specific heat $c_p$**, the inducted mixture **absorbs heat** during the intake stroke → **higher volumetric efficiency** in H₂ ICE. 3. The **high flame speed** and **wide flammability range** of H₂ mean combustion can be **lean-burn (φ < 1)** without misfire, improving thermal efficiency. 4. **Reduced NOx formation** is possible because peak flame temperatures in lean burn are lower. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]** **(a) With a neat table, compare the following properties of hydrogen, gasoline, and methane: density (STP), boiling point, LHV, flammability range in air, and minimum ignition energy. Comment on the safety implications.** **(7 marks)** **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** | Property | Hydrogen (H₂) | Gasoline | Methane (CH₄) | |---|---|---|---| | Density at STP (kg/m³) | **0.0899** | ~4.5 (vapor) | 0.717 | | Boiling Point (°C) | **–252.87** | 30 – 210 | –161.5 | | LHV (MJ/kg) | **120** | 43.5 | 50 | | Flammability Range (% vol in air) | **4 – 75** | 1.4 – 7.6 | 5 – 15 | | Min. Ignition Energy (mJ) | **0.017** | 0.25 | 0.28 | **Safety Implications (4 marks):** 1. **Hydrogen has the widest flammability range** (4–75 %) — a leak anywhere within this window can ignite → **need for rigorous leak detection and ventilation**. 2. **Minimum ignition energy (0.017 mJ) is ~15× lower than gasoline** — even static-electricity sparks can ignite → mandates **flame arrestors, anti-static materials, and grounded refueling systems**. 3. However, hydrogen's **high diffusivity in air** causes rapid dispersion of leaked gas, **reducing explosion hazard** in open or ventilated spaces. 4. Hydrogen's **high auto-ignition temperature (~570 °C)** reduces accidental ignition risk in hot-engine environments. --- **(b) An H₂-fueled bus consumes 8 kg of hydrogen per 100 km. Calculate the total energy released (using LHV), the equivalent gasoline mass (LHV_gasoline = 43.5 MJ/kg) required to release the same energy, and the volume occupied by 8 kg of H₂ at 700 bar and 298 K. Take R = 8.314 J/(mol·K) and M = 2.016 × 10⁻³ kg/mol.** **(7 marks)** **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Energy released by 8 kg H₂:** $$E = m \times \text{LHV} = 8 \times 120 = 960 \, \text{MJ}$$ **Step 2 — Equivalent gasoline mass:** $$m_{gas} = \dfrac{E}{\text{LHV}_{gas}} = \dfrac{960}{43.5} \approx 22.07 \, \text{kg}$$ **Step 3 — Volume of 8 kg H₂ at 700 bar, 298 K:** Using the ideal-gas form: $$V = \dfrac{m R T}{M P}$$ $$V = \dfrac{8 \times 8.314 \times 298}{2.016 \times 10^{-3} \times 70 \times 10^6}$$ **Step-by-step numerator:** $$8 \times 8.314 \times 298 = 19{,}820 \, \text{J}$$ **Step-by-step denominator:** $$2.016 \times 10^{-3} \times 70 \times 10^6 = 141{,}120 \, \text{Pa·m}^3/\text{kg} \times \text{kg/mol} \times \text{mol/m³} = 141{,}120$$ Wait, dimensions check: $M \cdot P$ has units of $\text{kg/mol} \times \text{Pa}$, but in numerator we have $m R T$ with units $\text{kg} \cdot \text{J/(mol·K)} \cdot \text{K} = \text{J·kg/mol}$. Dividing yields m³. Numerically: $$V = \dfrac{19{,}820}{141{,}120} \approx 0.1404 \, \text{m}^3 \approx 140.4 \, \text{L}$$ **Final Answers:** $$\boxed{E = 960 \, \text{MJ}, \quad m_{gas} \approx 22.07 \, \text{kg}, \quad V_{H_2} \approx 0.140 \, \text{m}^3}$$ **Engineering Insight:** 8 kg of H₂ at 700 bar occupies only ~140 L — a compact, practical volume — yet releases energy equivalent to over 22 kg of gasoline. This is the fundamental **gravimetric advantage** that makes H₂ attractive for heavy-duty transport. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Forgetting the N₂ factor in air (3.76):** Many students compute A/F using only O₂ instead of air, getting A/F = 8 instead of 34.2. Always remember: air ≈ **21 % O₂ + 79 % N₂**, hence the **4.76 multiplier**. > 2. **Mixing HHV and LHV:** Board questions often specify which to use. **Default to LHV** for engine/fuel-cell calculations (water leaves as vapor). Use **HHV** only when condensation energy is captured. > 3. **Unit mistakes in tank volume problems:** The unit of $P$ in the ideal-gas equation must be **Pascals (Pa)**, not bar. Convert: 1 bar = $10^5$ Pa. > 4. **Ignoring fuel mass in "equivalent" energy problems:** When asked for "equivalent gasoline," always state **mass, not volume**, unless density is explicitly given. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Basic Properties of Hydrogen (Module 1)** - ✅ **Atomic number 1; molecular form H₂; molar mass 2.016 g/mol.** - ✅ **Density at STP = 0.0899 kg/m³** — lowest of any gas. - ✅ **LHV = 120 MJ/kg; HHV = 142 MJ/kg** — highest gravimetric energy of all fuels. - ✅ **Boiling point = 20.28 K** — lowest among fuels; dictates cryogenic storage. - ✅ **Flammability range in air = 4 to 75 %** — widest of all common fuels. - ✅ **Auto-ignition temperature ≈ 500–585 °C** — high → safer in engines. - ✅ **Minimum ignition energy = 0.017 mJ** — ~15× lower than gasoline. - ✅ **Flame speed ≈ 2.65–3.25 m/s** — supports lean burn. - ✅ **Specific heat c_p ≈ 14.32 kJ/(kg·K)** — improves volumetric efficiency. - ✅ **Stoichiometric A/F = 34.3** — ~2.3× that of gasoline. - ✅ **Three isotopes: Protium (¹H, 99.985 %), Deuterium (²H, 0.015 %), Tritium (³H, trace, radioactive).** - ✅ **Hydrogen embrittlement** affects high-strength steels → mitigated by austenitic SS or polymer liners. - ✅ **700 bar CFRP tanks** are standard for FCEVs because of low volumetric density at ambient pressure. - ✅ **Green H₂** (renewable electrolysis) is the only sustainable pathway for automotive applications. ---

topics.title: Introduction to fuel cells and how they work
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT832 - HYDROGEN AND FUEL CELL VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to fuel cells and how they work

# Module 1 — Introduction to Fuel Cells and How They Work

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fuel Cell (KTU 2024 Definition):** An electrochemical energy conversion device that converts the chemical energy of a fuel (most commonly **hydrogen**) and an oxidant (typically **oxygen from air**) directly into **electrical energy**, with **water** and **heat** as the primary by-products, through a controlled redox (oxidation–reduction) reaction at the electrodes. Unlike a battery, a fuel cell is an **open thermodynamic system** that can continuously produce electricity as long as fuel and oxidant are supplied externally.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Silent Tea Stall"

Imagine a roadside tea stall that never shuts down. As long as the vendor keeps adding **water, tea leaves, sugar, and milk** from outside, the stall keeps producing tea. The kettle is always on, but it is *refuelled* rather than *recharged*. A **fuel cell works exactly like this tea stall**:

- The **kettle** = the **electrochemical cell** (anode + cathode + electrolyte)
- The **tea leaves + milk** = the **fuel** (hydrogen)
- The **sugar (oxidiser of taste)** = the **oxidant** (oxygen from air)
- The **chai (output product)** = **electricity + water + heat**
- The **shopkeeper** = the **catalyst** (usually Platinum)

In contrast, a **battery** is like a pre-packed sealed water bottle — once the water (charge) is finished, you must recharge the entire bottle. A **fuel cell**, however, only needs a fresh refill of hydrogen and oxygen.

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Milestone — Examiner's Favourite:** Sir **William Robert Grove** (a Welsh judge and physicist) built the first fuel cell in **1839**, which he famously called the *"gas battery"*. It was only in **1959** that **Francis Thomas Bacon** demonstrated the first practical, commercially usable **alkaline fuel cell (AFC)**. NASA subsequently adopted AFCs in the **Apollo space missions (1960s)** to provide onboard power and drinking water.

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants Used in Fuel Cell Analysis

| Constant | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Faraday's Constant | $F$ | **96,485** | C mol⁻¹ |
| Universal Gas Constant | $R$ | **8.314** | J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹ |
| Standard Temperature | $T_{std}$ | **298.15** | K |
| Standard Pressure | $P_{std}$ | **101.325** | kPa |
| Gibbs Free Energy of H₂O (l) | $\Delta G^0$ | **–237.13** | kJ mol⁻¹ |
| Enthalpy of H₂O (l) | $\Delta H^0$ | **–285.83** | kJ mol⁻¹ |
| Standard Cell EMF for H₂/O₂ | $E^0$ | **1.229** | V |

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Operating Principle

A fuel cell operates on the principle of **reverse electrolysis**. While electrolysis uses electricity to split water into hydrogen and oxygen, a fuel cell *re-combines* hydrogen and oxygen to *recover* that electricity.

The operating logic of any fuel cell follows this structured sequence:

1. **Fuel Supply:** Pressurised hydrogen gas (or a hydrogen-rich reformer output) is fed into the **anode** side of the cell.
2. **Oxidant Supply:** Air (or pure oxygen) is fed into the **cathode** side.
3. **Anode Half-Reaction (Oxidation of Hydrogen):** A platinum-based catalyst splits the H₂ molecule into protons (H⁺) and electrons (e⁻).
4. **Ion Migration:** The protons migrate through the **electrolyte** toward the cathode. The electrolyte is *selectively permeable* — only ions pass, electrons are forced through an external circuit.
5. **External Current:** Electrons, blocked by the electrolyte, travel through the **external load** (motor, light, grid), producing usable electric current.
6. **Cathode Half-Reaction (Reduction of Oxygen):** At the cathode, oxygen combines with the arriving protons and electrons to form water and heat.
7. **By-product Removal:** Water and unreacted gases are exhausted from the cathode outlet.

### 2.2 Core Electrochemistry — The Heart of the Fuel Cell

For the most fundamental case — a **Proton Exchange Membrane (PEM) Hydrogen Fuel Cell** — the three governing reactions are:

**Step 1 — Anode (Oxidation Reaction):**

$$H_{2} \rightarrow 2H^{+} + 2e^{-}$$

**Step 2 — Cathode (Reduction Reaction):**

$$\tfrac{1}{2}O_{2} + 2H^{+} + 2e^{-} \rightarrow H_{2}O$$

**Step 3 — Net Cell Reaction (Sum of Steps 1 and 2):**

$$H_{2} + \tfrac{1}{2}O_{2} \rightarrow H_{2}O + \text{Electrical Energy} + \text{Heat}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for vehicles:** The *only* tailpipe emission of a hydrogen fuel cell vehicle (FCEV) is **pure water vapour**. There is no CO₂, NOx, SOx, soot, or unburnt hydrocarbon — which is why FCEVs are classified as **Zero Emission Vehicles (ZEVs)** under KTU's EV and alternative-propulsion syllabus.

### 2.3 Main Components of a Generic Fuel Cell

| Component | Function | Common Material |
|---|---|---|
| **Anode** (Fuel Electrode) | Site of hydrogen oxidation; releases electrons | Porous graphite / nickel / carbon with Pt catalyst |
| **Cathode** (Air Electrode) | Site of oxygen reduction; consumes electrons | Same as anode, often Pt-loaded |
| **Electrolyte** | Conducts ions only; blocks electrons | Polymer membrane (Nafion) / KOH liquid / Ceramic (YSZ) / Phosphoric acid |
| **Catalyst Layer** | Lowers activation energy of half-reactions | **Platinum (Pt)** — most common; Pt-Ni, Pt-Co for cost reduction |
| **Gas Diffusion Layer (GDL)** | Uniform gas distribution and water management | Carbon paper / carbon cloth |
| **Bipolar Plates** | Distribute gases, collect current, separate cells | Graphite / stainless steel coated |
| **Seals & Gaskets** | Prevent gas crossover | Silicone / elastomeric rubber |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield for Board Exams)

| # | Formula | Meaning of Symbols | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E^0 = -\dfrac{\Delta G^0}{nF}$ | $E^0$=standard EMF, $\Delta G^0$=Gibbs free energy, $n$=electrons transferred, $F$=96485 C/mol | Theoretical open-circuit voltage |
| 2 | $\Delta G^0 = -nFE^0$ | Same as above | Reverse — compute EMF from free energy |
| 3 | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{\Delta G^0}{\Delta H^0}$ | $\eta_{th}$=thermodynamic efficiency | Max theoretical efficiency of fuel cell |
| 4 | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{-nFE^0}{-\Delta H^0}$ | Equivalent form | Practical calculation |
| 5 | $E = E^0 + \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_{H_2}\cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}}{P_{H_2O}}\right)$ | Nernst form for H₂/O₂ cell | Voltage under non-standard conditions |
| 6 | $W_{elec} = nFE$ | Electrical work output | Energy delivered per mole of fuel |
| 7 | $\eta_{voltage} = \dfrac{V_{cell}}{E_{rev}}$ | Voltage efficiency | Real vs theoretical cell voltage |
| 8 | $P = V \times I$ | Power output | Stack design |
| 9 | $\Delta H = \Delta G + T\Delta S$ | Enthalpy–Gibbs relation | Heat vs work split |
| 10 | $\eta_{HHV} = 1.482\,V / E^0$ | HHV reference for H₂ | Quick efficiency estimate |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Tip:** Always quote $\eta_{th}$ for the **H₂/O₂** cell as **83% (using $\Delta H$ at HHV)** or **94.5% (using $\Delta G$)**. The 100% Carnot ceiling does NOT apply to fuel cells — they are *not* heat engines!

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Theoretical Thermodynamic Efficiency of a Hydrogen Fuel Cell

**Problem Statement:** Prove that the maximum theoretical (thermodynamic) efficiency of a hydrogen–oxygen fuel cell producing liquid water is **83%** when referenced to the **Higher Heating Value (HHV)** of hydrogen.

**Given Data:**

- $\Delta G^0_{H_2O(l)} = -237.13$ kJ mol⁻¹ (Gibbs free energy of formation of liquid water at 298.15 K)
- $\Delta H^0_{H_2O(l)} = -285.83$ kJ mol⁻¹ (Higher Heating Value of hydrogen)
- Number of electrons transferred per mole H₂: $n = 2$
- Faraday's constant: $F = 96485$ C mol⁻¹

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

**Step 1:** Define thermodynamic efficiency as the ratio of *useful electrical work extractable* to the *total chemical energy released*.

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{electrical}}{Q_{chemical}} = \frac{-\Delta G^0}{-\Delta H^0}$$

*Explanation:* The Gibbs free energy $\Delta G^0$ represents the maximum reversible electrical work obtainable, while the enthalpy change $\Delta H^0$ represents the total chemical energy liberated if all the heat were captured (HHV basis).

**Step 2:** Substitute the standard values for the hydrogen–oxygen cell reaction.

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{-(-237.13)\ \text{kJ/mol}}{-(-285.83)\ \text{kJ/mol}}$$

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{237.13}{285.83}$$

**Step 3:** Carry out the numerical division.

$$\eta_{th} = 0.8296$$

**Step 4:** Convert to percentage.

$$\boxed{\eta_{th} \approx 82.96\% \approx 83\%}$$

This is the **maximum reversible efficiency** of an ideal hydrogen fuel cell. The remaining **17%** of the chemical energy is inevitably released as **low-grade heat (TΔS)** that must be removed by the cooling system — a direct consequence of the **Second Law of Thermodynamics**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** If the product is **steam** (gaseous water) instead of liquid water, use $\Delta H_{HHV}^{steam} = -241.82$ kJ/mol and $\Delta G^0_{steam} = -228.57$ kJ/mol, giving $\eta_{th}^{LHV} = 94.5\%$. Be precise about HHV vs LHV in board answers.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Deriving the Standard Cell EMF (Open-Circuit Voltage) of the H₂/O₂ Cell

**Problem Statement:** Using Gibbs free energy, derive the theoretical open-circuit EMF of a standard hydrogen–oxygen fuel cell.

**Given Data:**

- $\Delta G^0 = -237.13 \times 10^3$ J mol⁻¹
- $n = 2$
- $F = 96,485$ C mol⁻¹

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

**Step 1:** State the relationship between Gibbs free energy and reversible electrical work. The maximum electrical work extracted per mole of fuel is the product of charge transferred and cell voltage.

$$W_{elec,max} = -\Delta G^0 = nFE^0$$

**Step 2:** Solve for the standard EMF $E^0$.

$$E^0 = \frac{-\Delta G^0}{nF}$$

**Step 3:** Insert the values (note the negative signs — both numerator and denominator carry sign).

$$E^0 = \frac{-(-237130\ \text{J/mol})}{(2)(96485\ \text{C/mol})}$$

**Step 4:** Simplify the numerator.

$$E^0 = \frac{237130}{192970}$$

**Step 5:** Evaluate.

$$E^0 = 1.229\ \text{V}$$

**Step 6:** Final result.

$$\boxed{E^0_{H_2/O_2} = 1.229\ \text{V per cell}}$$

This is the **thermoneutral voltage** and represents the *ideal* open-circuit voltage of one cell. Real PEM fuel cells operate between **0.6 V and 0.8 V** per cell at rated current due to **activation, ohmic, and concentration overpotentials**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Nernst Equation for a Fuel Cell Under Load

**Problem Statement:** Show how the operating cell voltage $E$ varies with reactant partial pressures.

For the overall reaction $H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O$, the general Nernst equation gives:

$$E = E^0 + \frac{RT}{nF} \ln\!\left(\frac{P_{H_2}\cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}}{P_{H_2O}}\right)$$

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

**Step 1:** Start from the general Nernst equation form:

$$E = E^0 - \frac{RT}{nF}\ln Q$$

where $Q$ is the reaction quotient.

**Step 2:** Identify the activity of each species. For gases, activity = partial pressure (in atm); for pure liquid water, activity = 1.

$$Q = \frac{a_{H_2O}}{a_{H_2}\cdot a_{O_2}^{1/2}} = \frac{1}{P_{H_2}\cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}}$$

*Explanation:* Liquid water is the reference state and has activity 1.

**Step 3:** Substitute into the Nernst equation.

$$E = E^0 - \frac{RT}{nF}\ln\!\left(\frac{1}{P_{H_2}\cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}}\right)$$

**Step 4:** Apply the logarithmic identity $-\ln(1/x) = \ln(x)$.

$$\boxed{E = E^0 + \frac{RT}{nF}\ln\!\left(P_{H_2}\cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}\right)}$$

**Step 5:** Plug in typical values for a PEMFC running on air (P_{O₂}=0.21 atm) and pure H₂ at 1 atm at 80 °C (353 K).

$$E = 1.229 + \frac{(8.314)(353)}{(2)(96485)}\ln\!\left((1)\cdot(0.21)^{1/2}\right)$$

**Step 6:** Compute the temperature coefficient.

$$\frac{RT}{nF} = \frac{8.314 \times 353}{192970} = 0.01521\ \text{V}$$

**Step 7:** Compute the logarithm term.

$$\ln\!\left(\sqrt{0.21}\right) = \ln(0.4583) = -0.7803$$

**Step 8:** Evaluate.

$$E = 1.229 + (0.01521)(-0.7803)$$

$$E = 1.229 - 0.01187$$

$$\boxed{E \approx 1.217\ \text{V}}$$

This represents the **thermodynamic open-circuit voltage under practical air-breathing conditions**, about **12 mV lower** than the pure-O₂ theoretical value.

---

|---|---|---|---|---|---|

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Single PEM Fuel Cell

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the *flow of reactants, ions, electrons, and by-products* through the heart of a hydrogen fuel cell. Each functional block represents a distinct layer or interface inside a single membrane-electrode assembly (MEA).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H2tank["Hydrogen Tank Inlet"]
    AirIn["Air Blower Inlet"]
    AnodeFlow["Anode Flow Plate Bipolar Plate"]
    AnodeCat["Anode Catalyst Layer Pt on Carbon"]
    Membrane["PEM Electrolyte Nafion Membrane H plus Conductor"]
    CathodeCat["Cathode Catalyst Layer Pt on Carbon"]
    CathodeFlow["Cathode Flow Plate Bipolar Plate"]
    ExternalLoad["External Load Motor Lights Inverter"]
    WaterOut["Water and Heat Exhaust"]
    AirOut["Depleted Air Vent"]

    H2tank --> AnodeFlow
    AnodeFlow --> AnodeCat
    AirIn --> CathodeFlow
    CathodeFlow --> CathodeCat
    AnodeCat -->|H2 splits to H plus and e| Membrane
    Membrane -->|H plus migrates across| CathodeCat
    AnodeCat -->|e minus forced through external circuit| ExternalLoad
    ExternalLoad --> CathodeCat
    CathodeCat -->|O2 plus H plus plus e gives H2O| WaterOut
    CathodeFlow --> AirOut
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Architecture of a Complete Fuel Cell Vehicle Powertrain

For KTU's automotive context, the *fuel cell stack* alone is insufficient. The complete FCEV powertrain requires several **Balance-of-Plant (BoP)** components. The Mermaid block diagram below maps these subsystems and their data/energy interconnections:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    H2Storage["High Pressure H2 Tank 350 bar or 700 bar"]
    Reg["Pressure Regulator and Humidifier"]
    FCStack["Fuel Cell Stack 300 to 400 cells in series 70 to 90 kW"]
    DCConv["DC DC Converter High Voltage Bus"]
    BatPack["Lithium Ion Battery Buffer Pack 1 to 2 kWh"]
    Inv["Three Phase Inverter"]
    EMotor["Permanent Magnet Synchronous Traction Motor"]
    Trans["Single Speed Transmission and Differential"]
    Wheels["Drive Wheels of the Vehicle"]
    Rad["Radiator and Cooling Loop"]
    ECU["VCU Vehicle Control Unit Master ECU"]

    H2Storage --> Reg
    Reg --> FCStack
    FCStack --> DCConv
    DCConv --> Inv
    BatPack --> DCConv
    Inv --> EMotor
    EMotor --> Trans
    Trans --> Wheels
    FCStack -. waste heat .-> Rad
    ECU -. control signals .-> FCStack
    ECU -. control signals .-> BatPack
    ECU -. torque demand .-> EMotor
```

### 4.3 Polarisation Curve — The Performance Signature of a Fuel Cell

A real fuel cell voltage drops below $E^0 = 1.229$ V as current is drawn. This drop is captured by the **polarisation curve**, the single most important graph for fuel cell engineering. The Mermaid representation below describes its three classical loss regions:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Region 1 Activation Loss Steep initial drop Dominated by sluggish ORR kinetics"]
    B["Region 2 Ohmic Loss Linear drop Dominated by membrane ionic resistance and contact resistance"]
    C["Region 3 Concentration Loss Sharp drop at high current Reactant starvation"]
    D["Open Circuit Voltage 1.229 V ideal 0.95 to 1.0 V real"]
    E["Rated Operating Point Typically 0.6 to 0.7 V per cell"]

    A --> B
    B --> C
    D --> A
    C --> E
```

**Key insight:** Most automotive fuel cell stacks operate at **~0.65 V per cell** at rated power, giving an effective operating efficiency of:

$$\eta_{operating} = \frac{0.65}{1.229} \approx 53\%$$

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
Define a fuel cell. Mention any two advantages of fuel cells over conventional IC engines.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A fuel cell is an **electrochemical energy conversion device** that directly converts the chemical energy of a fuel (typically hydrogen) and an oxidant (typically oxygen from air) into **electrical energy**, with water and heat as by-products, through controlled redox reactions.

**Two advantages over IC engines:**

1. **Higher thermodynamic efficiency:** Fuel cells convert chemical energy *directly* to electricity, bypassing the Carnot cycle limitation. Practical PEMFC efficiency is 50–60%, vs. 30–35% for petrol IC engines.
2. **Zero tailpipe emissions:** The only by-product of a pure hydrogen fuel cell is water vapour; there is no CO₂, NOx, CO, or unburnt hydrocarbon, making FCEVs true **Zero Emission Vehicles (ZEVs)**.

*(Examiner awards 1 mark for definition, 1 mark each for two correct advantages.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
Write the anode, cathode, and overall cell reactions for a hydrogen–oxygen PEM fuel cell.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **Anode (Oxidation) Reaction:**
$$H_{2} \rightarrow 2H^{+} + 2e^{-}$$

- **Cathode (Reduction) Reaction:**
$$\tfrac{1}{2}O_{2} + 2H^{+} + 2e^{-} \rightarrow H_{2}O$$

- **Net Cell Reaction:**
$$H_{2} + \tfrac{1}{2}O_{2} \rightarrow H_{2}O + \text{Electricity} + \text{Heat}$$

*(1 mark each for correctly written half-reactions and net reaction.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 1]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a Proton Exchange Membrane (PEM) fuel cell. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Derive the theoretical thermodynamic efficiency and open-circuit voltage of a hydrogen–oxygen fuel cell. State the assumptions clearly. *(7 Marks)*

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(a) — Construction and Working of PEMFC:**

**Construction (Block Diagram):**

```
[ H₂ In ] → [ Anode Flow Plate ] → [ Anode Catalyst (Pt) ] → [ PEM (Nafion) ] → [ Cathode Catalyst (Pt) ] → [ Cathode Flow Plate ] → [ Air In ]
                                                                                              ↓
                                                                                       [ e⁻ via External Circuit ]
                                                                                              ↓
                                                                                          [ Load ]
                                                                                              ↓
                                                                                       [ H₂O + Heat Out ]
```

*(1 Mark for correct block diagram with all six labelled layers.)*

**Working — Step-by-Step Explanation:**

1. **H₂ Supply (Anode Side):** Pressurised hydrogen gas enters through the anode flow plate (bipolar plate), which distributes it uniformly across the anode catalyst layer. *(1 Mark)*

2. **Oxidation at Anode:** At the platinum catalyst surface, hydrogen molecules split into protons and electrons:
$$H_{2} \rightarrow 2H^{+} + 2e^{-} \quad \text{(Anode half-reaction)} \quad \text{(1 Mark)}$$

3. **Proton Migration Through PEM:** The protons (H⁺) migrate through the proton exchange membrane (Nafion) — a polymer electrolyte that conducts H⁺ ions but is impermeable to electrons and gases. The membrane must be kept **humidified** to maintain ionic conductivity. *(1 Mark)*

4. **External Electron Flow:** Electrons, blocked by the electrolyte, are forced to travel through an **external circuit**, delivering useful electrical current to the load (motor, lights, battery). This is the DC electricity output of the fuel cell. *(1 Mark)*

5. **Cathode Reduction:** At the cathode catalyst layer, oxygen from air combines with the protons and the arriving electrons to form water:
$$\tfrac{1}{2}O_{2} + 2H^{+} + 2e^{-} \rightarrow H_{2}O \quad \text{(1 Mark)}$$

6. **Water and Heat Removal:** The water produced (and excess heat) is removed from the cathode outlet. Proper water management is critical — too much water *floods* the catalyst; too little *dries* the membrane. *(1 Mark)*

7. **Net Reaction & By-product:** The overall reaction produces **electricity, pure water, and waste heat**, with no other emissions. *(1 Mark)*

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(b) — Derivation of $\eta_{th}$ and $E^0$:**

**Assumptions:**
- Reactants and products are at standard conditions (298.15 K, 101.325 kPa).
- Water is produced in **liquid** state (HHV basis).
- The cell operates reversibly (no losses).
- 2 moles of electrons are transferred per mole of H₂ (n = 2).

**Step 1:** Write the net cell reaction.
$$H_{2} + \tfrac{1}{2}O_{2} \rightarrow H_{2}O_{(l)}$$

**Step 2:** Note the thermodynamic data.
- $\Delta G^0 = -237.13$ kJ/mol (max reversible work)
- $\Delta H^0 = -285.83$ kJ/mol (HHV, total chemical energy)

**Step 3:** Define thermodynamic efficiency.
$$\eta_{th} = \frac{-\Delta G^0}{-\Delta H^0} \quad \text{(2 Marks for formulation)}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute values.
$$\eta_{th} = \frac{237.13}{285.83} = 0.8296 = 82.96\% \approx 83\% \quad \text{(2 Marks)}$$

**Step 5:** Derive $E^0$ from $\Delta G^0$.
$$\Delta G^0 = -nFE^0 \implies E^0 = \frac{-\Delta G^0}{nF} \quad \text{(2 Marks)}$$

**Step 6:** Substitute numerical values.
$$E^0 = \frac{237130}{2 \times 96485} = 1.229\ \text{V} \quad \text{(1 Mark)}$$

**Final Result:**
$$\boxed{\eta_{th} \approx 83\% \quad \text{and} \quad E^0 = 1.229\ \text{V per cell}}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1 — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Explain the working of a fuel cell. Compare fuel cells with batteries in terms of operating principle, energy storage, and refuelling. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A hydrogen–oxygen PEM fuel cell operates at 80 °C with $P_{H_2} = 1.0$ atm, $P_{O_2} = 0.21$ atm (air), and the product water is liquid. Calculate the reversible cell voltage using the Nernst equation. Take $\Delta G^0 = -237.13$ kJ/mol at 25 °C, but use the cell temperature for Nernst correction. *(7 Marks)*

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(a) — Fuel Cell vs Battery:**

**Working of a Fuel Cell (Brief, 3 Marks):**
A fuel cell continuously converts chemical energy of externally supplied fuel (H₂) and oxidant (O₂) into electricity via electrochemical reactions at the anode and cathode, separated by an ion-conducting electrolyte. Electrons flow through the external circuit, producing useful DC power, with water and heat as by-products.

**Comparison Table (4 Marks):**

| Feature | Fuel Cell | Battery |
|---|---|---|
| Operating Principle | Direct electrochemical conversion of externally supplied fuel | Stored chemical energy converted within a closed cell |
| Energy Storage | **External** — stored in H₂ tank; refilled in minutes | **Internal** — stored in electrodes; requires hours to recharge |
| Refuelling Time | 3–5 minutes (H₂ refuelling) | 30 min – 8 hours (depending on chemistry) |
| Energy Density (practical) | High (500–1000 Wh/kg system) | Moderate (150–250 Wh/kg for Li-ion) |
| Lifetime | Long (stack modules replaceable) | Limited by cycle ageing |
| By-products | Water + Heat | Heat only (during discharge) |

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(b) — Nernst Voltage Calculation:**

**Given:**
- $T = 80\,°C = 353$ K
- $P_{H_2} = 1.0$ atm
- $P_{O_2} = 0.21$ atm
- $n = 2$, $F = 96,485$ C/mol, $R = 8.314$ J/mol·K

**Step 1:** Compute standard EMF at 25 °C.
$$E^0 = \frac{-\Delta G^0}{nF} = \frac{237130}{2 \times 96485} = 1.229\ \text{V} \quad \text{(2 Marks)}$$

**Step 2:** Write the Nernst equation.
$$E = E^0 + \frac{RT}{nF}\ln\!\left(P_{H_2}\cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}\right) \quad \text{(2 Marks)}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the thermal voltage term.
$$\frac{RT}{nF} = \frac{8.314 \times 353}{2 \times 96485} = 0.01521\ \text{V} \quad \text{(1 Mark)}$$

**Step 4:** Compute the log term.
$$P_{H_2}\cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2} = 1.0 \times \sqrt{0.21} = 0.4583$$
$$\ln(0.4583) = -0.7803 \quad \text{(1 Mark)}$$

**Step 5:** Final calculation.
$$E = 1.229 + (0.01521)(-0.7803)$$
$$E = 1.229 - 0.01187$$
$$\boxed{E \approx 1.217\ \text{V}} \quad \text{(1 Mark)}$$

The reversible open-circuit voltage under practical air-breathing PEMFC conditions is approximately **1.22 V**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing HHV and LHV:** If a question gives $\Delta H$ for gaseous water (–241.82 kJ/mol), do NOT use –285.83 kJ/mol. Mixing up HHV and LHV is the **#1 mark-deduction error** in fuel cell efficiency problems.
> 2. **Wrong sign conventions:** When using $E^0 = -\Delta G^0 / nF$, both numerator and denominator have implicit signs. Forgetting the double negative leads to negative voltages — an immediate full-mark loss.
> 3. **Carnot Mistake:** NEVER calculate fuel cell efficiency using $1 - T_{cold}/T_{hot}$. Fuel cells are **not heat engines** — they bypass the Carnot limit, but their ceiling is set by **$\Delta G/\Delta H$**, not Carnot.
> 4. **Forgetting the Nernst log identity:** Many students write $-\ln(1/x)$ instead of $+\ln(x)$. Memorise the identity carefully.
> 5. **Cathode reaction omission:** Always specify whether water is produced as liquid or vapour in the cathode half-reaction. The choice changes $\Delta G^0$ by ~8.5 kJ/mol.
> 6. **Confusing fuel cell with battery:** Examiners frequently test whether students know that a fuel cell is an **open system** (continuous fuel supply) while a battery is a **closed system** (finite capacity).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- A **fuel cell** is an *electrochemical* (not thermodynamic) energy converter that directly turns fuel + oxidant into DC electricity.
- The **net reaction** for a H₂/O₂ cell is $H_{2} + \tfrac{1}{2}O_{2} \rightarrow H_{2}O$ — water is the *only* tailpipe product.
- Three functional layers: **Anode (oxidation) → Electrolyte (ion conduction only) → Cathode (reduction)**.
- **Standard EMF:** $E^0 = 1.229$ V per cell (theoretical, pure H₂ and O₂).
- **Theoretical efficiency:** $\eta_{th} = \Delta G^0 / \Delta H^0 \approx 83\%$ (HHV) or **94.5%** (LHV).
- **Real operating voltage per cell:** 0.6 – 0.8 V due to activation, ohmic, and concentration overpotentials.
- **Catalyst:** Platinum is the industry standard, but Pt–Ni, Pt–Co alloys and non-precious catalysts are active research areas.
- **Electrolyte types** distinguish fuel cell families: PEM (polymer), AFC (alkaline), PAFC (acid), MCFC (carbonate), SOFC (oxide).
- **PEMFC** is the dominant automotive choice (Toyota Mirai, Hyundai Nexo, Hyundai XCIENT Fuel Cell Truck) due to its low operating temperature (~80 °C) and quick start-up.
- **Nernst equation** for the H₂/O₂ cell: $E = E^0 + \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln\!\left(P_{H_2}\cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}\right)$.
- **Fuel cell ≠ Battery:** Fuel cell is *open* (refillable); battery is *closed* (rechargeable).
- **Historical firsts:** Sir William Grove (1839) — gas battery; Francis Bacon (1959) — first practical AFC; NASA Apollo (1960s) — first automotive-grade application.
- **Carnot limit does NOT apply** to fuel cells — their ceiling is dictated by Gibbs free energy, not heat-engine thermodynamics.
- **Polarisation curve** has three zones: activation (low current), ohmic (mid current, linear), concentration (high current, sharp drop).
- **Voltage efficiency** of a practical cell: $\eta_{voltage} = V_{cell}/E_{rev} \approx 0.65/1.229 \approx 53\%$.
- For FCEVs, the **balance-of-plant** includes H₂ tank, humidifier, compressor, DC-DC converter, battery buffer, inverter, and traction motor.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Historical development of hydrogen fuel cell technology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT832 - HYDROGEN AND FUEL CELL VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Historical development of hydrogen fuel cell technology

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hydrogen Fuel Cell Technology** refers to the evolution of electrochemical energy conversion devices that directly transform the chemical energy of hydrogen (and an oxidant, typically oxygen from air) into electrical energy, water, and heat, bypassing the intermediate combustion step. The **historical development** traces over 180 years of scientific discovery, engineering refinement, and commercialization milestones — from Christian Friedrich Schönbein's foundational discovery of the "fuel cell effect" (1838) to the modern commercial deployment of hydrogen-powered vehicles by Toyota, Honda, and Hyundai.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme OEMUT832 syllabus**, the historical evolution of hydrogen fuel cell technology is foundational because it contextualizes **why** hydrogen emerged as a strategic energy carrier for automotive applications, **who** the pioneering contributors were, and **how** the technology transitioned from laboratory curiosities to vehicle-grade propulsion systems.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Reverse Electrolysis" Battery**
>
> Imagine an **ordinary AA battery** sitting on your table. You insert it into a flashlight, and it powers the bulb until it dies. Now imagine the *opposite*: instead of **storing** energy and giving it out, what if the battery could **accept fuel continuously** and never run out as long as you feed it? That is exactly what a **fuel cell** is — it is essentially a **"refuelable battery"** that uses hydrogen and oxygen to make electricity on demand.

Think of it like a **kitchen gas stove that runs on hydrogen instead of LPG**. The stove doesn't store the gas as a permanent part of itself; the gas is piped in, ignited (in a fuel cell, the reaction is electrochemical, not combustion), and produces a useful output (heat, light, or — in our case — electricity to drive a car motor). The hydrogen fuel cell is therefore *not* a battery in the conventional sense; it is a **continuous-flow energy converter** whose historical development mirrors humanity's century-long effort to replace the noisy, inefficient steam and internal combustion engines with something cleaner, quieter, and more efficient.

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Constants for Hydrogen Fuel Cell History**
>
> | Metric | Value | Significance |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Avogadro's Number** | $6.022 \times 10^{23} \text{ mol}^{-1}$ | Defines molar quantity of electrons per H₂ molecule |
> | **Faraday's Constant ($F$)** | **96,485 C/mol** | Charge per mole of electrons |
> | **Standard Hydrogen Potential ($E^0$)** | **0.000 V (SHE)** | Reference electrode potential |
> | **Lower Heating Value of H₂** | **33.33 kWh/kg** | Energy density used for range calculation |
> | **Higher Heating Value of H₂** | **39.4 kWh/kg** | Includes latent heat of vaporization |
> | **Electrons per H₂ molecule** | **2** | $H_2 \rightarrow 2H^+ + 2e^-$ |

---

## 1.4 The Three Eras of Development

The historical development of hydrogen fuel cell technology can be cleanly divided into **three distinct eras**, each defined by its dominant motivation and technological capability:

1. **Discovery Era (1838–1900)** — Scientific curiosity and theoretical grounding
2. **Space Age Era (1932–1970s)** — Practical demonstration in aerospace applications
3. **Commercial/Automotive Era (1990s–Present)** — Mass-market vehicle deployment

---

## 1.5 Syllabus-Relevant Outcomes

> [!IMPORTANT]
