<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**List any three major degradation mechanisms in a PEM fuel cell and explain how each affects the cell voltage.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Pt Catalyst Dissolution / Ostwald Ripening:** At potentials above 0.85 V vs RHE, Pt oxidizes to Pt²⁺ which dissolves into the ionomer. Smaller particles dissolve and redeposit on larger ones, causing ECSA loss. Effect: ↑ **Activation overpotential** → cell voltage ↓.

2. **Carbon Support Corrosion:** During start-up with air on both sides, cathode potential can exceed 1.5 V. Carbon oxidizes to CO₂. Effect: Catalyst layer collapse → ↑ **Mass transport loss** → cell voltage ↓, especially at high current density.

3. **Membrane Chemical Degradation:** Hydroxyl (·OH) and peroxyl (·OOH) radicals from H₂O₂ (catalyzed by trace Fe²⁺) attack the C–F backbone. Effect: Membrane thinning and pinhole formation → ↑ **Hydrogen crossover** → ↓ OCV and cell voltage.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (KTU 2024):** Students often write "the membrane wears out" without specifying the *radical mechanism*. Always name the radicals (·OH, ·OOH) and the Fenton's reaction catalyst (Fe²⁺). **3 marks → 1 mark** if mechanism is missing.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is the DOE 2025 durability target for automotive PEM fuel cells? Define the End-of-Life (EOL) criterion used in this assessment.**

**Model Answer:**

- **DOE 2025 LDV target:** **5,000 hours** of operation (or **8,000 hours equivalent cycle life**) under simulated drive-cycle conditions.
- **Heavy-duty target:** 25,000–30,000 hours.
- **EOL Criterion:** The cell is considered to have reached end-of-life when the **voltage at rated current density (typically 1.0 A/cm²) drops by 20% from its initial value**, OR when voltage falls below 0.55 V/cell (whichever occurs first).
- This criterion accounts for both power loss and the safe operating window of the fuel cell system.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Pattern]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain in detail the **four primary categories of fuel cell degradation** (electrochemical, chemical, mechanical, thermal) with one specific example for each.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A PEM fuel cell operates at 0.65 V initially at 1.0 A/cm². After 2,500 hours of operation, the voltage drops to 0.60 V. **(i)** Calculate the linear voltage decay rate. **(ii)** Estimate the total lifetime if the EOL criterion is 20% power loss. **(iii)** If accelerated testing at 90°C shows 100 μV/hr decay, what is the Acceleration Factor (AF) for 80°C operation, given activation energy $E_a = 85$ kJ/mol?

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Four Categories of Degradation:**

1. **Electrochemical Degradation** — Driven by electrode potential excursions.
   - *Example:* Pt dissolution at high cathode potential (>0.85 V). Pt → Pt²⁺ + 2e⁻. Dissolved Pt redeposits on larger particles (Ostwald ripening), reducing ECSA and increasing activation overpotential.

2. **Chemical Degradation** — Driven by reactive species and impurities.
   - *Example:* Membrane attack by hydroxyl radicals (·OH) generated via Fenton's reaction: $H_2O_2 + Fe^{2+} \rightarrow \cdot OH + OH^- + Fe^{3+}$. The radicals cleave C–F bonds in Nafion, releasing F⁻ ions (measured as FER) and causing membrane thinning.

3. **Mechanical Degradation** — Driven by physical stress and cycling.
   - *Example:* Membrane pinhole formation from repeated humidity cycling (swelling at 100% RH, contraction at low RH). Pinholes cause H₂ crossover → mixed potential → OCV drop.

4. **Thermal Degradation** — Driven by temperature extremes and gradients.
   - *Example:* Freeze damage to MEA. At sub-zero temperatures, water in the catalyst layer expands ~9% upon freezing, mechanically disrupting the ionomer/catalyst interface and creating dead zones with no electrochemical activity.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Voltage decay rate:**

$$k_V = \frac{V_0 - V_{2500}}{2500} = \frac{0.65 - 0.60}{2500} = \frac{0.05}{2500} = 20 \times 10^{-6} \text{ V/hr} = 20 \text{ } \mu\text{V/hr}$$

**(ii) Total lifetime:**

For 20% power loss: $V_{EOL} = 0.80 \times 0.65 = 0.52$ V

$$\tau_{life} = \frac{V_0 - V_{EOL}}{k_V} = \frac{0.65 - 0.52}{20 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{0.13}{20 \times 10^{-6}} = 6500 \text{ hours}$$

**(iii) Acceleration Factor:**

$$AF = e^{\frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_{use}} - \frac{1}{T_{acc}}\right)}$$

Convert temperatures: $T_{use} = 80 + 273 = 353$ K, $T_{acc} = 90 + 273 = 363$ K

$$\frac{E_a}{R} = \frac{85{,}000}{8.314} = 10{,}224.7 \text{ K}$$

$$\frac{1}{353} - \frac{1}{363} = 0.002833 - 0.002755 = 7.81 \times 10^{-5} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

$$AF = e^{10{,}224.7 \times 7.81 \times 10^{-5}} = e^{0.7986} \approx 2.22$$

**Interpretation:** 1 hour at 90°C ≈ 2.22 hours at 80°C. The accelerated test of $k_V = 100$ $\mu$V/hr at 90°C corresponds to $k_V = 100/2.22 \approx 45$ $\mu$V/hr at 80°C — *much higher than the 20 $\mu$V/hr from the actual cell*, suggesting the accelerated test represents a more aggressive duty cycle.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Discuss the **carbon corrosion mechanism** in PEM fuel cells. Under what operational conditions is it most severe? What are the consequences for fuel cell performance?

**(b)** [7 Marks] With the help of a suitable diagram, explain the **Ostwald ripening process** for Pt nanoparticles. Starting from initial ECSA of 80 m²/g and particle size 3 nm, calculate the **percentage ECSA loss** if particles grow to 7 nm. (Assume spherical particles and Pt density 21.45 g/cm³.)

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Carbon Corrosion:**

Carbon black (Vulcan XC-72) is the most common catalyst support in PEMFCs. While electronically conductive and high-surface-area, it is **thermodynamically unstable** at high potentials.

**Mechanism (electrochemical):**

$$C + 2H_2O \rightarrow CO_2 + 4H^+ + 4e^- \quad E° = 0.207 \text{ V vs SHE}$$

**Most Severe Conditions:**
- **Air-air start-up (fuel starvation):** When H₂ is absent on the anode but air is present on the cathode, the entire fuel cell voltage can appear across the cathode, driving its potential above 1.5 V. Carbon corrosion becomes extremely rapid.
- **High cathode potentials (>1.0 V)** during OCV hold or load transients.
- **Local H₂ starvation** due to flow maldistribution.

**Consequences:**
1. **Loss of electrical connectivity** in catalyst layer → increased ohmic resistance.
2. **Pt nanoparticle detachment** from collapsing carbon surface → ECSA loss.
3. **Catalyst layer thinning and pore collapse** → severe mass transport loss at high current density.
4. **Increased MEA thickness loss** → membrane-electrode delamination risk.

**Mitigation:**
- Use **graphitized carbon** (higher corrosion resistance)
- **Engineered start-up protocols** (auxiliary load, N₂ purge)
- **Pt-alloy catalysts** that lower ORR overpotential (reducing cathode potential)
- Alternative supports: **N-doped carbon, TiO₂, indium tin oxide, ceramic**

**(b) Ostwald Ripening Calculation:**

Ostwald ripening: Small Pt particles (high surface energy) dissolve and redeposit onto larger particles (lower surface energy) via Pt²⁺ ion migration through the ionomer.

**Given:**
- $d_0 = 3$ nm, $d_f = 7$ nm
- $ECSA_0 = 80$ m²/g
- $\rho_{Pt} = 21.45$ g/cm³ = 21.45 × 10⁻²¹ g/nm³

**Formula for ECSA (spherical particles):**

$$ECSA = \frac{A}{m} = \frac{4\pi r^2}{\rho_{Pt} \cdot \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3} = \frac{3}{\rho_{Pt} \cdot r}$$

**ECSA ratio:**

$$\frac{ECSA_f}{ECSA_0} = \frac{r_0}{r_f} = \frac{d_0}{d_f} = \frac{3}{7} = 0.4286$$

**Percentage ECSA loss:**

$$\Delta ECSA\% = \left(1 - \frac{ECSA_f}{ECSA_0}\right) \times 100\% = (1 - 0.4286) \times 100\% = 57.14\%$$

**Final ECSA:**

$$ECSA_f = 0.4286 \times 80 = 34.3 \text{ m}^2/\text{g}$$

**Conclusion:** A particle growth from 3 nm to 7 nm results in **57% ECSA loss**, demonstrating the catastrophic impact of Ostwald ripening on catalyst utilization. This is well beyond the DOE 2025 target of < 40% ECSA loss after 30,000 voltage cycles.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Common Pitfalls — Degradation & Lifetime Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to state the 20% EOL criterion** explicitly when solving lifetime problems. Always define $V_{EOL}$ first.
> 2. **Mixing up the Arrhenius AF formula** direction. AF > 1 means accelerated test is harsher; AF < 1 is impossible for elevated temperature.
> 3. **Confusing ECSA** with mass-specific surface area — both are m²/g in PEMFC context, but the *measurement technique* (CV H-desorption at 0.21 mC/cm²) must be cited.
> 4. **Not converting temperatures** to Kelvin in Arrhenius equations. Marks deducted: typically **1 mark per error**.
> 5. **Omitting diagrams** in Part B — a 7-mark sub-question without a supporting sketch loses 1–2 marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2**

- **Four degradation categories:** Electrochemical, Chemical, Mechanical, Thermal
- **Pt dissolution threshold:** > 0.85 V vs RHE → Ostwald ripening → ECSA loss
- **Carbon corrosion threshold:** > 0.207 V (thermodynamic) but severe at > 1.0 V
- **EOL Criterion:** 20% power loss OR 0.55 V/cell
- **DOE Targets:** 5,000 hr (LDV), 25,000–30,000 hr (HDV), 40,000 hr (buses)
- **Linear decay model:** $V(t) = V_0 - k_V t$, with $k_V < 10$ $\mu$V/hr (LDV target)
- **Lifetime formula:** $\tau_{life} = (V_0 - V_{EOL}) / k_V$
- **Arrhenius AF:** $AF = \exp[(E_a/R)(1/T_{use} - 1/T_{acc})]$, with $E_a \approx 80$–$100$ kJ/mol
- **ECSA calculation:** $ECSA = Q_{H-desorp} / (m_{Pt} \times 0.21)$ mC/cm²
- **Membrane health metrics:** Fluoride Emission Rate (FER) < $10^{-8}$ g/(cm²·hr), H₂ crossover < 10 mA/cm²
- **Top 5 failure modes** (most-tested): Pt dissolution, Carbon corrosion, Membrane thinning, GDL flooding, BPP passivation
- **Mitigation toolkit:** Pt-alloy catalysts, graphitized supports, radical scavengers (Ce), coated BPPs, mechanical reinforcement
- **Key formula to memorize:** ECSA ratio = $d_0/d_f$ (for spherical particles)
- **Platinum loading targets:** 0.1 g/kW (LDV), 0.05 g/kW (HDV) — major cost driver
- **Cold-start challenge:** Sub-zero start requires < 30s to rated power — water management critical
- **Impurity tolerance:** < 50 ppb SO₂, < 4 ppm CO (with 2% air bleed) in reformate H₂

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Innovations in fuel cell materials and designs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT832 - HYDROGEN AND FUEL CELL VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Innovations in fuel cell materials and designs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Innovation in Fuel Cell Materials and Designs** refers to the systematic advancement of constituent materials (membranes, electrocatalysts, gas diffusion layers, bipolar plates, seals) and the geometric/architectural configuration of individual cells and stacks to enhance **power density**, **durability**, **thermal/water management**, **cost reduction**, and **operational envelope** (temperature, pressure, fuel flexibility) of fuel cell systems used in vehicular applications.

In the context of **OEMUT832 – Hydrogen and Fuel Cell Vehicles**, this topic is the technological heart of Module 2 because the *commercial viability* of a Fuel Cell Electric Vehicle (FCEV) such as the Toyota Mirai, Hyundai NEXO, or Mercedes GLC F-CELL is constrained less by the underlying electrochemistry and more by **material limits** (Pt loading, membrane conductivity, plate corrosion) and **design limits** (water flooding, heat rejection, reactant distribution).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Restaurant Kitchen" Model

Imagine a fuel cell as a **high-end restaurant kitchen**:

- **Proton Exchange Membrane (PEM)** → the *head chef's pass-through window* — only the right "items" (H⁺ protons) pass, never the "raw meat" (electrons or gas). The faster and cleaner this pass, the better the restaurant runs.
- **Catalyst Layer (Pt/C)** → the *cooking station* — where the actual reaction (cooking) happens. A better, faster stove = smaller, cheaper kitchen.
- **Gas Diffusion Layer (GDL)** → the *delivery conveyor belt* bringing ingredients (H₂, O₂) and taking away the "waste" (water vapor).
- **Bipolar Plate (BPP)** → the *wall and plumbing* between two kitchens (cells). It must conduct electricity, distribute gas, and remove heat.
- **Stack** → the entire *food court* — many kitchens stacked together to feed thousands.

**Innovation** is simply the process of making each of these components **cheaper, faster, smaller, and longer-lasting**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU Metrics to Remember:**
> - **Pt loading** has dropped from ~4 mg/cm² (1990s) to **0.025–0.1 mg/cm²** in 2020+ vehicles.
> - **Durability target** (DOE 2030): **25,000 hours** for trucks, **8,000 hours** for light-duty FCEVs.
> - **Power density target**: **> 1.0 W/cm²** at 0.65 V per cell (membrane electrode assembly active area).
> - **Cost target (DOE)**: **$40/kW** by 2030 (system level, high-volume manufacturing).

## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Examinations

This topic directly maps to the following Course Outcomes (COs) in the 2024 Scheme:

| CO Code | Course Outcome | Bloom's Level |
|---|---|---|
| **CO2** | Identify and compare advanced materials and design configurations for fuel cell systems. | Understand / Analyze |
| **CO3** | Evaluate the performance parameters of novel fuel cell designs. | Evaluate |

Examiners frequently test the **"Compare-and-Contrast"** pattern (e.g., Nafion vs. hydrocarbon membranes, metallic vs. composite BPPs) because it neatly fits Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks) KTU templates.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of Fuel Cell Innovation

Innovation in fuel cell systems can be organized into **four engineering pillars**, each addressing a specific bottleneck:

### Pillar 1 — Membrane (Electrolyte) Innovation

The membrane is the **single most expensive and failure-prone** component in a PEMFC stack.

**Traditional Material: Nafion™ (DuPont)**
- Perfluorosulfonic acid (PFSA) polymer.
- Operates only in **hydrated state** (RH > 50%).
- Maximum operating temperature: **~80 °C**.
- Proton conductivity: **0.1 S/cm** (fully hydrated, 25 °C).
- Cost: **~US$500–800/m²**.

**Innovations:**

| Innovation | Key Feature | Advantage |
|---|---|---|
| **Reinforced Nafion (Nafion 211/212/115)** | PTFE-reinforced thin films | Lower cost, thinner (25–50 µm), reduced crossover |
| **Hydrocarbon Membranes** (SPEEK, PBI, PBI/PA) | Aromatic backbone with sulfonic acid | Cheaper, less CO poisoning, operable at **120–200 °C** |
| **High-Temperature PEM (HT-PEM)** | PBI + H₃PO₄ doping | Operates at **160–200 °C**, no humidification needed, better CO tolerance |
| **Anion Exchange Membranes (AEM)** | Quaternary ammonium groups, OH⁻ transport | Enables **non-precious metal catalysts** (Ag, Ni, Fe) → huge cost cut |
| **Composite / Nanofiber Membranes** | SiO₂, ZrP, or GO nano-fillers | Improved mechanical strength, reduced swelling |
| **Reinforced Aquivion®** | Short-side-chain PFSA | Higher conductivity at low RH |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HT-PEM fuel cells (160–200 °C)** are increasingly important for FCEVs because they tolerate up to **3% CO** in reformate H₂, enabling on-board reforming or use of lower-purity industrial hydrogen.

### Pillar 2 — Catalyst Innovation

The **oxygen reduction reaction (ORR)** at the cathode is ~6 orders of magnitude slower than the hydrogen oxidation reaction (HOR) at the anode. Hence innovation is cathode-focused.

**Key Performance Metric: Mass Activity (A/g_Pt) at 0.9 V iR-free**
- DOE 2020 target: **0.44 A/mg_Pt**
- DOE 2030 target: **0.60 A/mg_Pt**

**Innovations:**

| Innovation | Concept | Benefit |
|---|---|---|
| **Pt-Co / Pt-Ni alloys** | 3d-transition metal alloyed with Pt | d-band shift → weaker O binding → higher ORR activity (**4–6×** pure Pt) |
| **Core-Shell Nanostructures** (Pt-skin on Pd or Ni core) | Only surface = Pt, bulk = cheap metal | **< 0.1 mg_Pt/cm²** loading achievable |
| **Shape-Controlled Nanocrystals** (octahedral, nanowires, nanoframes) | Expose Pt(111) facets preferentially | Higher specific activity, less dissolution |
| **Pt-M (M = Y, La, Gd) — Pt skin + rare earth oxide** | "Pt skin" with rare earth in sub-surface | Activity **> 10×** pure Pt; reported in 2024 MIT/Argonne work |
| **Non-Precious Metal Catalysts (NPMCs)** | Fe-N-C, Co-N-C MOFs | Designed for **AEMFC** to avoid Pt entirely |
| **Single-Atom Catalysts (SACs)** | Isolated Pt atoms on N-doped C | ~100% atom utilization; emerging research frontier |

### Pillar 3 — Bipolar Plate (BPP) Innovation

BPP contributes **~30% of stack cost** and **> 80% of stack mass** in some designs.

**Functional Requirements** (KTU examiner favorite):
1. Electrical conductivity **> 100 S/cm**
2. Thermal conductivity **> 10 W/m·K**
3. Corrosion current **< 1 µA/cm²**
4. H₂ permeability **< 2 × 10⁻⁶ cm³/s·cm²**
5. Flexural strength **> 25 MPa**

**Material Innovations:**

| Material | Conductivity | Corrosion Resistance | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Graphite (traditional)** | 100–1000 S/cm | Excellent | Brittle, expensive machining |
| **Metallic (SS 316L)** | > 10⁴ S/cm | Poor (passive film needed) | Toyota Mirai uses coated SS |
| **Coated Metals (Au, CrN, DLC, TiN, C-PPy)** | Maintained | Restored | **Industry standard** for automotive |
| **Composite (graphite + polymer binder)** | 100–300 S/cm | Excellent | Injection-moldable → low cost |
| **Carbon-Carbon Composite** | 200–400 S/cm | Excellent | Aerospace-grade (expensive) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Diamond-Like Carbon (DLC)** coating on metallic BPPs is the *industry standard* in modern FCEVs (Hyundai NEXO 2nd gen). It provides both corrosion protection and low interfacial contact resistance (**ICCR < 5 mΩ·cm²**).

### Pillar 4 — GDL & MEA Architecture Innovation

**Gas Diffusion Layer (GDL)** is typically a **Carbon Paper** (Toray TGP-H) or **Carbon Cloth** with a **Microporous Layer (MPL)**.

**Innovations:**
- **Graded GDLs** — variable PTFE loading to manage water removal.
- **Hierarchical pore structures** — large pores for gas, small pores for water capillary pumping.
- **3D-printed GDLs** — tunable porosity & permeability.
- **Metal-foam-based GDLs** (Ni, Cu) for high-power density.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations. They appear in nearly every Part B question on fuel cell design.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_{Nernst} = E^0 + \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln \left( p_{H_2} \, p_{O_2}^{1/2} \right)$ | Thermodynamic open-circuit voltage | V |
| 2 | $\eta_{act} = \dfrac{RT}{\alpha n F} \ln \left( \dfrac{i}{i_0} \right)$ | Tafel activation overpotential | V |
| 3 | $\eta_{ohm} = i \cdot R_{ASR}$ | Ohmic loss (R_ASR = area-specific resistance) | V |
| 4 | $\eta_{conc} = \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln \left( 1 - \dfrac{i}{i_L} \right)$ | Concentration overpotential | V |
| 5 | $P_{stack} = N_{cells} \cdot V_{cell} \cdot I$ | Stack power output | W |
| 6 | $\rho_{vol} = \dfrac{P_{stack}}{V_{stack}}$ | **Volumetric power density** | W/L |
| 7 | $\rho_{grav} = \dfrac{P_{stack}}{m_{stack}}$ | **Gravimetric power density** | W/kg |
| 8 | $\eta_{util,H_2} = \dfrac{\dot{n}_{H_2,reacted}}{\dot{n}_{H_2,supplied}}$ | Hydrogen utilization (typ. **0.85–0.95** in FCEV) | – |
| 9 | $V_{cell} = E_{Nernst} - \eta_{act,a} - \eta_{act,c} - \eta_{ohm} - \eta_{conc}$ | Polarization equation (full cell) | V |
| 10 | $R_{ASR} = \dfrac{t_m}{\sigma_m} + R_{contact}$ | Membrane + contact ASR | Ω·cm² |
| 11 | $\sigma_m(T) = A \exp \left( -\dfrac{E_a}{RT} \right)$ | Membrane conductivity (Arrhenius) | S/cm |
| 12 | $D_{O_2}^{eff} = D_{O_2} \cdot \dfrac{\varepsilon}{\tau}$ | Effective O₂ diffusivity in GDL (porosity ε, tortuosity τ) | cm²/s |

**Standard Constants** (memorize for derivations):
- R = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- F = **96,485 C/mol** (Faraday's constant)
- n = 2 (for H₂ → 2H⁺ + 2e⁻)
- α (charge-transfer coefficient) = **0.5** (symmetric)
- F = 96,485 C/mol is also **96485 A·s/mol**

> [!WARNING]
> **Common unit trap in KTU papers:** R is in **J/(mol·K)** when working in SI, but in **8.314 × 10⁻³ kJ/(mol·K)** if you want to cancel kJ with F. Always check unit consistency before solving.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Innovation Area | Real FCEV Application | Why It Matters |
|---|---|---|
| Low-Pt catalyst (Pt-Co) | Toyota Mirai Gen 2 | Pt loading reduced to **0.012 g/vehicle** |
| Coated SS bipolar plate | Hyundai NEXO | Stack mass reduced by **~30%** vs graphite |
| 3D fine-mesh flow field | Bosch 2024 stack | 20% higher power density |
| HT-PEM (PBI) | Plug Power material handling vehicles | Tolerates impure H₂ from on-site reformers |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I: Stack Power Density from Single-Cell Polarization

**Problem Statement (KTU-Style):**
A PEM fuel cell stack contains **120 cells** in series. Each cell has an active area of **300 cm²**. At a current density of **1.2 A/cm²**, the average cell voltage is **0.65 V**. Calculate:
1. The stack voltage.
2. The stack current.
3. The stack power.
4. The volumetric power density if the stack (including end plates) occupies **18 liters**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Stack Voltage**

Cells in series → voltages add.

$$
V_{stack} = N_{cells} \cdot V_{cell}
$$

$$
V_{stack} = 120 \times 0.65 = 78.0 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 2 — Stack Current**

Current is the same through every cell (series). Current density × area:

$$
I = i \cdot A
$$

$$
I = 1.2 \, \dfrac{\text{A}}{\text{cm}^2} \times 300 \, \text{cm}^2 = 360 \text{ A}
$$

**Step 3 — Stack Power**

$$
P_{stack} = V_{stack} \cdot I
$$

$$
P_{stack} = 78.0 \, \text{V} \times 360 \, \text{A} = 28{,}080 \, \text{W} \approx 28.08 \, \text{kW}
$$

**Step 4 — Volumetric Power Density**

$$
\rho_{vol} = \dfrac{P_{stack}}{V_{stack}}
$$

$$
\rho_{vol} = \dfrac{28{,}080 \, \text{W}}{18 \, \text{L}} = 1560 \, \dfrac{\text{W}}{\text{L}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is well above the DOE 2030 target of 850 W/L — illustrating that modern FCEV stacks are *already* exceeding legacy benchmarks.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II: Nernst Voltage for a PEMFC at Non-Standard Conditions

**Problem Statement:**
For a PEMFC operating at **T = 353 K (80 °C)** with p(H₂) = **1.5 bar** and p(O₂) = **0.21 bar** (air at 1 atm), calculate the **thermodynamic reversible voltage E**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Standard Potential**

At standard conditions (25 °C, 1 bar), the hydrogen/oxygen fuel cell standard potential is:

$$
E^0 = 1.229 \, \text{V}
$$

**Step 2 — Nernst Equation**

For the overall reaction: $H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2} O_2 \rightarrow H_2O_{(l)}$

$$
E = E^0 + \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln \left( p_{H_2} \cdot p_{O_2}^{1/2} \right)
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Values**

- R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)
- T = 353 K
- n = 2
- F = 96,485 C/mol
- p(H₂) = 1.5 bar
- p(O₂) = 0.21 bar

$$
\dfrac{RT}{nF} = \dfrac{8.314 \times 353}{2 \times 96{,}485}
$$

$$
\dfrac{RT}{nF} = \dfrac{2935.0}{192{,}970} = 0.01521 \, \text{V}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Log Term**

$$
p_{H_2} \cdot p_{O_2}^{1/2} = 1.5 \times (0.21)^{0.5}
$$

$$
(0.21)^{0.5} = 0.4583
$$

$$
1.5 \times 0.4583 = 0.6874
$$

$$
\ln(0.6874) = -0.3749
$$

**Step 5 — Final E**

$$
E = 1.229 + (0.01521) \times (-0.3749)
$$

$$
E = 1.229 - 0.00570
$$

$$
E \approx 1.223 \, \text{V}
$$

**Step 6 — Temperature Correction to E⁰ (if T = 80 °C, not 25 °C)**

Using the empirical correction:
$$
E^0(T) = 1.229 - 0.000846 \cdot (T - 298)
$$

$$
E^0(353) = 1.229 - 0.000846 \times 55 = 1.229 - 0.0465 = 1.1825 \, \text{V}
$$

$$
E = 1.1825 + 0.01521 \times (-0.3749) = 1.1825 - 0.00570 \approx 1.177 \, \text{V}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **realistic OCV** of a practical PEMFC at 80 °C with air (≈1.18 V), not 1.229 V. KTU examiners often expect students to apply *both* the T-correction and the partial-pressure correction.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III: Activation Overpotential (Tafel Slope) from Exchange Current Density

**Problem Statement:**
The exchange current density of the ORR on a Pt-Co catalyst is $i_0 = 2.5 \times 10^{-8}$ A/cm². Operating at i = 1.0 A/cm², T = 353 K, α = 0.5, n = 2. Compute the activation overpotential.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Tafel Equation**

$$
\eta_{act} = \dfrac{RT}{\alpha n F} \ln \left( \dfrac{i}{i_0} \right)
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Pre-factor**

$$
\dfrac{RT}{\alpha n F} = \dfrac{8.314 \times 353}{0.5 \times 2 \times 96{,}485}
$$

$$
= \dfrac{2935.0}{96{,}485} = 0.03042 \, \text{V}
$$

This is the **Tafel slope (b)** in volts per natural log decade.

**Step 3 — Compute Log Term**

$$
\dfrac{i}{i_0} = \dfrac{1.0}{2.5 \times 10^{-8}} = 4.0 \times 10^{7}
$$

$$
\ln(4.0 \times 10^{7}) = \ln(4) + 7 \ln(10) = 1.386 + 16.118 = 17.504
$$

**Step 4 — Final Overpotential**

$$
\eta_{act} = 0.03042 \times 17.504 = 0.5325 \, \text{V}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This **~530 mV** loss is why ORR catalysis is the single biggest focus of material innovation. A 10× higher $i_0$ (e.g., from Pt-Co alloying) drops the loss to ~0.42 V.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Membrane vs. Catalyst vs. BPP Innovation

| Innovation Parameter | Nafion (Baseline) | Hydrocarbon (PBI) | AEM | Pt-Co Catalyst | DLC-coated BPP |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Conductivity (S/cm)** | 0.10 | 0.05–0.08 | 0.05–0.10 | – (i₀: 10⁻⁸ → 10⁻⁷) | – (R_contact ↓) |
| **Max Operating T (°C)** | 80 | 200 | 60–80 | 200 stable | 200 stable |
| **CO Tolerance (ppm)** | < 10 | > 30,000 | < 10 | Reduced | – |
| **Cost Factor** | 1× (baseline) | 0.5× | 0.4× | 0.3× (Pt-loading) | 0.6× (vs. Au coat) |
| **Durability (hours)** | 5,000 | 8,000 | 2,000 | 25,000 (DOE target) | 20,000+ |
| **KTU Exam Tag Frequency** | ★★★ | ★★★ | ★★ | ★★★ | ★★ |

---

## 3.5 Quantitative Check: Pt Loading Reduction Trend

**Historical evolution (KTU favorite "trend" question):**

$$
\text{Pt}_{loading, year} = 4.0 \times 10^{-0.032 \cdot (year - 1990)} \text{ mg/cm}^2
$$

For **1990**: Pt = 4.0 mg/cm² (Los Alamos Pajarito Mfg)
For **2005**: Pt = 0.4 mg/cm²
For **2015**: Pt = 0.05 mg/cm²
For **2025**: Pt ≈ **0.01–0.02 mg/cm²** (Mirai Gen 2)

This **100× reduction** was achieved by combining:
1. Pt-alloy catalysts (4× activity)
2. Ultra-low loading electrodes (10× dispersion)
3. Improved membrane/ionomer interfaces (2.5× utilization)

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Marking Scheme Note:** KTU Part A expects **3–4 crisp points** or **a labeled diagram + brief explanation**. Length: ~5–6 lines.

### Question A.1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**"List any three innovations in proton exchange membrane materials for fuel cells. State one advantage of each."**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Reinforced PFSA (Nafion 211/212):** PTFE-reinforced thin membranes (25–50 µm) — *advantage: lower gas crossover and cost compared to cast Nafion 117.*

2. **High-Temperature PEM (PBI/H₃PO₄):** Phosphoric-acid-doped polybenzimidazole — *advantage: operates at 160–200 °C without external humidification and tolerates > 30,000 ppm CO.*

3. **Anion Exchange Membranes (AEM):** Quaternary-ammonium-functionalized hydrocarbon polymer — *advantage: enables non-precious-metal catalysts (Ag, Ni) and uses earth-abundant materials, drastically reducing cost.*

### Question A.2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**"Differentiate between metallic and composite bipolar plates for PEM fuel cells."**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Metallic (SS 316L) | Composite (Graphite-Polymer) |
|---|---|---|
| **Electrical Conductivity** | Excellent (~10⁴ S/cm) | Moderate (~100–300 S/cm) |
| **Mechanical Strength** | High | Moderate |
| **Corrosion Resistance** | Poor (needs coating) | Inherent (no coating) |
| **Manufacturability** | Stamping (low cost, high volume) | Injection molding (low cost) |
| **Weight** | Heavy (8 g/cm³) | Light (~1.5 g/cm³) |
| **Durability** | High (with DLC/CrN coat) | High (no coating needed) |
| **Application** | Toyota Mirai, Hyundai NEXO | Compact stationary FC |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Essay Question (14 Marks)

> **Marking Scheme:** 14 marks = 7 + 7. Each sub-part must be **fully solved with a labeled diagram where applicable**.

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the major innovations in **electrocatalyst materials** for PEM fuel cells. Compare Pt-alloy catalysts with non-precious-metal catalysts (Fe-N-C).

**(b) [7 Marks]** A PEM fuel cell operates at 1.0 A/cm², 65 °C, with partial pressures p(H₂) = 1.2 bar, p(O₂) = 0.18 bar. The exchange current density for the ORR on a Pt-Co catalyst is $i_0 = 1.5 \times 10^{-7}$ A/cm². Using the Tafel equation, determine the activation overpotential and comment on how Pt-Co alloying helps reduce this loss.

---

#### Model Solution to (a) — Catalyst Innovations

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The ORR at the cathode is the kinetic bottleneck. Innovations target increasing the **mass activity (A/mg_Pt)** and reducing **Pt loading** without sacrificing durability.

**Key Innovations (4 marks):**
1. **Pt-3d-Transition Metal Alloys (Pt-Co, Pt-Ni, Pt-Fe):** Alloying compresses the Pt d-band, weakening Pt-O binding and accelerating ORR kinetics. Mass activity 4–6× higher than pure Pt.
2. **Core-Shell Nanostructures (Pt-skin on Pd or Ni):** Only the outermost atomic layer is Pt — bulk is cheap transition metal. Achieves 0.025 mg_Pt/cm².
3. **Shape-Controlled Nanocrystals (octahedra, nanowires, nanoframes):** Expose favorable Pt(111) facets → higher specific activity.
4. **Non-Precious Metal Catalysts (NPMCs):** Fe-N-C and Co-N-C MOF-derived materials for AEMFC — eliminate Pt entirely.
5. **Single-Atom Catalysts (SACs):** Isolated Pt atoms on N-doped carbon supports with ~100% atom utilization — emerging frontier.

**Comparison Table (2 marks):**

| Aspect | Pt-Alloy | Fe-N-C NPMCs |
|---|---|---|
| Mass Activity | 0.4–0.6 A/mg_Pt | 0.05–0.10 A/mg total |
| Cost | High (Pt) | Very low |
| Durability in PEMFC | 25,000 h | < 2,000 h (Fe demetallation) |
| Operating pH | Acidic (PEM) | Suitable for AEM |
| Maturity | Commercial (Mirai Gen 2) | Research stage |

---

#### Model Solution to (b) — Quantitative Activation Overpotential

**Given:**
- i = 1.0 A/cm², T = 338 K (65 °C)
- $i_0 = 1.5 \times 10^{-7}$ A/cm²
- α = 0.5, n = 2

**Step 1 — Tafel Equation (2 marks):**

$$
\eta_{act} = \dfrac{RT}{\alpha n F} \ln \left( \dfrac{i}{i_0} \right)
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Pre-factor (2 marks):**

$$
\dfrac{RT}{\alpha n F} = \dfrac{8.314 \times 338}{0.5 \times 2 \times 96{,}485}
$$

$$
= \dfrac{2810.1}{96{,}485} = 0.02913 \, \text{V}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Log Term (1 mark):**

$$
\dfrac{i}{i_0} = \dfrac{1.0}{1.5 \times 10^{-7}} = 6.67 \times 10^{6}
$$

$$
\ln(6.67 \times 10^{6}) = \ln(6.67) + 6 \ln(10) = 1.897 + 13.816 = 15.71
$$

**Step 4 — Final Overpotential (1 mark):**

$$
\eta_{act} = 0.02913 \times 15.71 = 0.458 \, \text{V} \approx 458 \, \text{mV}
$$

**Step 5 — Comment on Pt-Co Benefit (1 mark):**
A pure Pt catalyst has $i_0 \approx 1 \times 10^{-9}$ A/cm², giving $\eta_{act} \approx 590$ mV. The Pt-Co alloy's 150× higher exchange current density reduces the overpotential by ~130 mV — directly translating into ~12% higher power output at the same current density.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the design innovations in **bipolar plates and flow fields** for automotive PEMFC stacks. Include a brief note on serpentine vs. interdigitated designs.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A fuel cell stack has 80 cells, active area 250 cm², and operates at 0.70 V per cell on average. The stack occupies 12 liters. Calculate the **stack power** and **volumetric power density**. If a new membrane innovation reduces the ASR from 0.10 to 0.05 Ω·cm², estimate the new cell voltage at the same current density of 1.0 A/cm² (assume other losses unchanged).

---

#### Model Solution to (a) — BPP & Flow Field Innovations

**Innovations in Bipolar Plates (3 marks):**
1. **Coated Stainless Steel (DLC, CrN, Au):** Replaces expensive graphite; enables mass production via stamping. Used in Toyota Mirai Gen 2.
2. **Graphite-Polymer Composites:** Injection-moldable → 60% lower manufacturing cost.
3. **Hybrid Metal-Composites (Cu-clad-C):** Combining best of both.
4. **3D-Printed Metallic Plates:** Topology-optimized channels reduce pressure drop and weight.

**Flow Field Design Comparison (4 marks):**

| Design | Pressure Drop | Water Removal | Uniformity | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Single Serpentine** | High (3–10 kPa) | Excellent | Good | High-power automotive |
| **Multi-Serpentine** | Moderate | Good | Better | Most FCEVs |
| **Interdigitated** | Very High | **Excellent** (forced convection) | Excellent | High-current density |
| **Parallel** | Low (0.5 kPa) | Poor (flooding risk) | Poor | Low-power stationary |
| **Pin/Metal-foam** | Moderate | Excellent (3D) | Excellent | R&D, future |

> **Conclusion:** Automotive stacks (e.g., Hyundai NEXO) favor **multi-serpentine + interdigitated hybrid** to balance pressure drop and water removal.

---

#### Model Solution to (b) — Stack Performance Calculation

**Step 1 — Stack Current (1 mark):**

$$
I = i \times A = 1.0 \times 250 = 250 \, \text{A}
$$

**Step 2 — Stack Voltage & Power (2 marks):**

$$
V_{stack} = 80 \times 0.70 = 56 \, \text{V}
$$

$$
P_{stack} = V_{stack} \times I = 56 \times 250 = 14{,}000 \, \text{W} = 14 \, \text{kW}
$$

**Step 3 — Volumetric Power Density (1 mark):**

$$
\rho_{vol} = \dfrac{14{,}000}{12} = 1166.67 \, \text{W/L}
$$

**Step 4 — Effect of ASR Reduction (3 marks):**

Ohmic loss with old ASR:

$$
\eta_{ohm,old} = i \cdot R_{ASR,old} = 1.0 \times 0.10 = 0.100 \, \text{V}
$$

Ohmic loss with new ASR:

$$
\eta_{ohm,new} = 1.0 \times 0.05 = 0.050 \, \text{V}
$$

Improvement per cell:

$$
\Delta V = 0.100 - 0.050 = 0.050 \, \text{V}
$$

New cell voltage:

$$
V_{cell,new} = 0.70 + 0.050 = 0.75 \, \text{V}
$$

New stack power:

$$
P_{new} = 80 \times 0.75 \times 250 = 15{,}000 \, \text{W} = 15 \, \text{kW}
$$

**Improvement:** ~7.1% more power at the same current — showing that *membrane innovation alone* (reducing ASR by 50%) yields a meaningful stack-level performance gain.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mark-Loss Traps on This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Tafel with Butler-Volmer:** The Tafel equation is an *approximation* of the Butler-Volmer equation valid only when $i \gg i_0$. Examiners deduct 1 mark if you apply Tafel at low current densities (near OCV).
>
> 2. **Mixing up PFSA and PBI temperatures:** Nafion works *only* below ~95 °C; PBI operates *only* at 160–200 °C. Students often write "PBI is used at 80 °C" — this is wrong and loses 2 marks.
>
> 3. **Forgetting unit cancellation in $\frac{RT}{nF}$:** R must be in J/(mol·K) when F is in C/mol. If you mistakenly use R = 1.987 cal/(mol·K), you must also convert F's units, or your answer will be off by 4.18×.
>
> 4. **Ignoring the temperature correction to E⁰:** $E^0 = 1.229$ V is valid only at 25 °C. At 80 °C, it is 1.182 V. Examiners often test this directly.
>
> 5. **Pt loading in mg/cm² vs. g/vehicle:** Confusing these units is a 2-mark deduction in calculation problems.
>
> 6. **Confusing AEMFC and PEMFC ionic transport:** AEMFC carries **OH⁻**; PEMFC carries **H⁺**. This dictates catalyst choice and is a frequent Part A trap.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Notes — Read Before Exam**

- ✅ **Membrane innovations:** Nafion (PFSA) baseline → HT-PEM (PBI/H₃PO₄) for 160–200 °C → AEM for low-cost catalysts → composite/reinforced for durability.
- ✅ **Catalyst innovations:** Pt-alloys (Pt-Co, Pt-Ni) give 4–6× mass activity boost; core-shell structures drop loading to < 0.05 mg_Pt/cm²; single-atom catalysts are the future frontier.
- ✅ **Bipolar plate innovations:** Coated SS 316L (DLC, CrN) for automotive; composite plates for low-cost stationary.
- ✅ **GDL innovations:** Graded PTFE, hierarchical porosity, metal foams for high-power density.
- ✅ **Flow field innovations:** Serpentine (high ΔP, good water removal), interdigitated (forced convection, best water management), parallel (low ΔP, flooding risk), metal foam (future).
- ✅ **Must-memorize equations:** Nernst, Tafel, full polarization curve, $P = N V i A$, volumetric and gravimetric power density, $R_{ASR}$ definition.
- ✅ **Key DOE targets (2030):** $40/kW, 1.0 W/cm², 25,000 h durability, < 0.05 mg_Pt/cm².
- ✅ **Real FCEV examples:** Toyota Mirai Gen 2 (3D fine-mesh flow field), Hyundai NEXO (DLC-coated BPP), Plug Power (HT-PEM material handling).
- ✅ **Temperature ranges:** PEMFC (60–80 °C), HT-PEM (160–200 °C), SOFC (600–800 °C), MCFC (650 °C), PAFC (200 °C).
- ✅ **AEM vs PEM:** AEM uses OH⁻ transport → non-Pt catalysts possible → key cost-reduction pathway.

---

topics.title: Comparative analysis of different fuel cell technologies (PEMFC, Solid Oxide Fuel Cells, Alkaline Fuel Cells)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT832 - HYDROGEN AND FUEL CELL VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Comparative analysis of different fuel cell technologies (PEMFC, Solid Oxide Fuel Cells, Alkaline Fuel Cells)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Fuel Cell? (Foundation Recap)

A **fuel cell** is an electrochemical energy conversion device that converts the chemical energy of a fuel (typically hydrogen) and an oxidant (typically oxygen from air) directly into electrical energy, with water and heat as by-products. Unlike a battery, it does not consume its electrodes or run down — it produces electricity as long as fuel and oxidant are supplied.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A fuel cell is a galvanic cell in which the chemical energy of a fuel is converted directly into electrical energy by an electrochemical process, without thermal/mechanical intermediate stages (Carnot limitation is bypassed).

### 1.2 Classification Basis for Fuel Cells

Fuel cells are classified primarily based on the **type of electrolyte** used, which directly dictates the operating temperature, charge carrier, catalyst, and application domain.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Electrolyte Matters:**
> The electrolyte determines:
> - Operating temperature window (60 °C → 1000 °C)
> - Type of ion transported (H⁺, O²⁻, OH⁻, CO₃²⁻)
> - Catalyst selection (Platinum group vs. Nickel cermet vs. Silver)
> - Fuel flexibility (pure H₂ vs. hydrocarbons vs. reformed fuel)
> - Suitability for vehicular applications

### 1.3 The Three Fuel Cell Technologies in Focus

#### (a) Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cell (PEMFC)

**Formal Definition:** A PEMFC uses a solid polymer membrane (typically **Nafion™**) as the electrolyte, which conducts protons (H⁺ ions) from anode to cathode. It operates at low temperatures (**60–100 °C**), making it the **dominant technology for automotive and portable applications**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The PEMFC as a "Sieve Sandwich":**
> Imagine a slice of cheese (the Nafion membrane) sandwiched between two porous bread slices (the gas diffusion electrodes). Hydrogen gas is piped onto one side, oxygen onto the other. The cheese only lets positive protons through (like a one-way sieve), forcing electrons to take a detour through your car's motor — that's your electricity!

**Key Metrics:**
- Operating Temperature: **60–100 °C**
- Power Density: High (compact)
- Charge Carrier: **H⁺** (proton)
- Catalyst: **Platinum (Pt)** — expensive but fast kinetics
- Primary Application: Cars, buses, trucks (Toyota Mirai, Hyundai Nexo)

#### (b) Solid Oxide Fuel Cell (SOFC)

**Formal Definition:** A SOFC uses a **solid ceramic electrolyte** (typically Yttria-Stabilized Zirconia, YSZ) that conducts **oxide ions (O²⁻)** from cathode to anode at elevated temperatures (**600–1000 °C**). High temperature enables internal reforming of hydrocarbon fuels and yields very high electrical efficiency.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The SOFC as a "Hot Ceramic Sandwich Oven":**
> Picture two ceramic plates with a hard ceramic cookie between them, all glowing red-hot. The oxygen molecules hit the cathode side, get stripped of electrons (gaining 2 negative charges), and migrate through the ceramic cookie to meet hydrogen on the other side. The "trick" of the detour electrons around the cookie is what lights up your grid. Because the oven is super hot, even cheap natural gas or biogas can be fed directly inside!

**Key Metrics:**
- Operating Temperature: **600–1000 °C**
- Power Density: Moderate (large footprint)
- Charge Carrier: **O²⁻** (oxide ion)
- Catalyst: **Nickel cermet** (Ni-YSZ) — cheap, no precious metal
- Primary Application: Stationary power, CHP systems, large trucks

#### (c) Alkaline Fuel Cell (AFC)

**Formal Definition:** An AFC uses an **aqueous solution of potassium hydroxide (KOH)** as the electrolyte, which conducts **hydroxide ions (OH⁻)** from cathode to anode. It operates at moderate temperatures (**60–250 °C**) and is the oldest, most mature fuel cell type — it powered the **Apollo space missions**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The AFC as a "Bubble Bath Generator":**
> Imagine a bathtub filled with potash-laced water (alkaline KOH solution) with two electrodes dipped in. Hydrogen enters one side, oxygen the other. Inside the bath, ions shuffle around carrying charges. Pure water comes out as a byproduct — which astronauts literally drank in space! However, the bath gets poisoned if you let CO₂ in (it forms carbonates), so it needs "scrubbed" ultra-pure oxygen and hydrogen.

**Key Metrics:**
- Operating Temperature: **60–250 °C**
- Power Density: Moderate
- Charge Carrier: **OH⁻** (hydroxide ion)
- Catalyst: **Nickel, Silver, or Platinum** (cheaper options than PEMFC)
- Primary Application: Space (Apollo, Space Shuttle), submarines, military

### 1.4 Universal Hydrogen–Oxygen Cell Foundation

Regardless of type, all three cells share the **overall reaction**:

$$H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O \quad (+ \text{heat} + \text{electrical work})$$

with standard thermodynamic values at 25 °C:
- **ΔG° = −237.2 kJ/mol** (Gibbs free energy, maximum electrical work)
- **ΔH° = −285.8 kJ/mol** (HHV — Higher Heating Value, enthalpy of combustion)
- **E° = 1.229 V** (standard reversible open-circuit voltage)
- **n = 2** (electrons transferred per mole of H₂)
- **F = 96,485 C/mol** (Faraday's constant)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:**
> Students often quote ΔH° = −241.8 kJ/mol (Lower Heating Value, LHV) and ΔH° = −285.8 kJ/mol (HHV) interchangeably. For **vehicle applications**, LHV is preferred since exhaust water leaves as vapor. Use LHV = −241.8 kJ/mol when discussing automotive fuel cell efficiency.

---

## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Electrochemistry of Each Fuel Cell — Half-Reactions

#### PEMFC Electrochemistry

At the anode, hydrogen is oxidized; the proton passes through the membrane while the electron goes through the external circuit:

$$H_2 \rightarrow 2H^+ + 2e^- \quad \text{(Anode, Oxidation)}$$

At the cathode, oxygen combines with protons and electrons to form water:

$$\tfrac{1}{2}O_2 + 2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2O \quad \text{(Cathode, Reduction)}$$

**Charge Carrier:** H⁺ travels anode → cathode through Nafion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Nafion Membrane Magic:** Nafion is a sulfonated tetrafluoroethylene copolymer. Its sulfonate groups (–SO₃⁻) attract and conduct protons when hydrated, but reject electrons and gases — providing ionic conduction with electronic insulation.

#### SOFC Electrochemistry

At the cathode, oxygen is reduced and gains electrons to become an oxide ion:

$$\tfrac{1}{2}O_2 + 2e^- \rightarrow O^{2-} \quad \text{(Cathode, Reduction)}$$

The oxide ion migrates through the YSZ ceramic to the anode, where it reacts with hydrogen:

$$H_2 + O^{2-} \rightarrow H_2O + 2e^- \quad \text{(Anode, Oxidation)}$$

**Charge Carrier:** O²⁻ travels cathode → anode (reverse direction of PEMFC).

#### AFC Electrochemistry

At the cathode, oxygen is reduced by water, forming hydroxide ions:

$$\tfrac{1}{2}O_2 + H_2O + 2e^- \rightarrow 2OH^- \quad \text{(Cathode, Reduction)}$$

The hydroxide ions migrate through the KOH electrolyte to the anode:

$$H_2 + 2OH^- \rightarrow 2H_2O + 2e^- \quad \text{(Anode, Oxidation)}$$

**Charge Carrier:** OH⁻ travels cathode → anode.

> [!NOTE]
> **Direction Logic:**
> In all fuel cells, **cations move toward the cathode** and **anions move toward the anode**. This is the universal rule from electrochemical convention.

### 2.2 The Nernst Equation — Reversible Cell Voltage

The actual reversible cell voltage depends on partial pressures and temperature:

$$E_{Nernst} = E^0 + \frac{RT}{nF} \ln\left[ \frac{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}}{P_{H_2O}} \right]$$

Where:
- **E⁰ = 1.229 V** at 25 °C (standard condition)
- **R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)** (universal gas constant)
- **T** = cell absolute temperature (K)
- **n = 2** electrons per H₂ molecule
- **F = 96,485 C/mol**
- **P_{H₂}, P_{O₂}, P_{H₂O}** = partial pressures (atm)

**Temperature Correction (Empirical, often used in KTU):**

$$E^0(T) = 1.229 - 0.00085 \cdot (T - 298.15) \quad \text{(in Volts, T in Kelvin)}$$

This shows that as temperature increases, the theoretical open-circuit voltage **decreases** — a key trade-off in SOFC operation.

### 2.3 Thermodynamic Efficiency

The **maximum theoretical (reversible) efficiency** of an H₂/O₂ cell:

$$\eta_{thermo} = \frac{\Delta G}{\Delta H}$$

- **HHV basis:** $\eta = \frac{237.2}{285.8} = 0.830 = 83.0\%$
- **LHV basis (vehicle-relevant):** $\eta = \frac{237.2}{241.8} = 0.981 = 98.1\%$

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Markdown Table)

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Applicable To |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Standard EMF | $E^0 = -\Delta G^0 / (nF)$ | V | All fuel cells |
| 2 | Standard value | $E^0 = 1.229$ at 25 °C | V | All H₂/O₂ cells |
| 3 | Nernst Equation | $E = E^0 + \frac{RT}{nF} \ln \left( \frac{P_{H_2} P_{O_2}^{1/2}}{P_{H_2O}} \right)$ | V | All fuel cells |
| 4 | Temperature correction | $E^0(T) = 1.229 - 0.00085(T - 298.15)$ | V | All H₂/O₂ cells |
| 5 | Faraday's constant | $F = 96{,}485$ | C/mol | All |
| 6 | Theoretical Efficiency (HHV) | $\eta = 237.2 / 285.8 = 0.830$ | — | All fuel cells |
| 7 | Theoretical Efficiency (LHV) | $\eta = 237.2 / 241.8 = 0.981$ | — | Automotive |
| 8 | Energy from H₂ (HHV) | $\Delta H^0 = -285.8$ | kJ/mol | All |
| 9 | Energy from H₂ (LHV) | $\Delta H^0 = -241.8$ | kJ/mol | All |
| 10 | Gibbs free energy | $\Delta G^0 = -237.2$ | kJ/mol | All |
| 11 | Voltage efficiency | $\eta_V = V_{cell} / E^0$ | — | Stack analysis |
| 12 | Ohmic loss (area-specific resistance) | $\Delta V_{ohmic} = I \cdot ASR$ | V | Stack design |
| 13 | Activation loss (Tafel) | $\Delta V_{act} = \frac{RT}{\alpha nF} \ln\left(\frac{i}{i_0}\right)$ | V | All fuel cells |
| 14 | Concentration loss | $\Delta V_{conc} = -\frac{RT}{nF} \ln\left(1 - \frac{i}{i_L}\right)$ | V | All fuel cells |
| 15 | Nernst loss (SOFC, due to fuel utilization) | $\Delta V_{Nernst} = \frac{RT}{2F} \ln\left(\frac{1}{U_f}\right)$ | V | SOFC systems |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real Engineering Tip:**
> In a real PEMFC operating at 0.65 V/cell with E⁰ = 1.23 V, the voltage efficiency η_V ≈ 53%. Adding kinetic, ohmic, and concentration losses typically gives an overall stack efficiency of **50–60% (LHV)** for PEMFC and **50–60% electrical** for SOFC.

### 2.5 Why Different Vehicles Use Different Fuel Cells

| Application | Best Fit | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| **Passenger Cars** | PEMFC | Fast start-up, compact, robust |
| **Buses / Trucks (short range)** | PEMFC | High power density |
| **Heavy Trucks (long-haul)** | SOFC | High efficiency, fuel flexibility |
| **Trains, Ships** | SOFC | Large footprint tolerable, high efficiency |
| **Spacecraft, Submarines** | AFC | High efficiency, pure water byproduct |
| **Portable electronics** | PEMFC | Compact, low temp |

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Reversible Cell Voltage from Gibbs Free Energy

The maximum non-PV (electrical) work obtainable from a chemical reaction equals the change in Gibbs free energy:

$$W_{max,elec} = -\Delta G$$

For n moles of electrons transferred per mole of fuel at cell potential E:

$$W_{max,elec} = n \cdot F \cdot E$$

Equating both expressions:

$$n \cdot F \cdot E = -\Delta G$$

Solving for E:

$$E = -\frac{\Delta G}{nF}$$

**Step-by-step for H₂/O₂ cell at standard conditions:**

At 25 °C, 1 atm, $\Delta G^0 = -237.2$ kJ/mol, n = 2, F = 96,485 C/mol.

$$E^0 = -\frac{(-237{,}200 \text{ J/mol})}{(2)(96{,}485 \text{ C/mol})}$$

$$E^0 = \frac{237{,}200}{192{,}970} = 1.229 \text{ V}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This value (1.229 V) is the **theoretical open-circuit voltage of a single H₂/O₂ fuel cell** at standard conditions. A real PEMFC stack operates at 0.6–0.7 V per cell under load.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Effect of Temperature on Open-Circuit Voltage

Using the Gibbs–Helmholtz relation:

$$\left(\frac{\partial E^0}{\partial T}\right)_P = \frac{\Delta S^0}{nF}$$

Where ΔS⁰ for H₂ + ½O₂ → H₂O (liquid) is **−163.3 J/(mol·K)**.

$$\frac{\partial E^0}{\partial T} = \frac{-163.3}{(2)(96{,}485)} = -8.46 \times 10^{-4} \text{ V/K}$$

Thus, the empirical linearized form:

$$E^0(T) = 1.229 - 0.000846 \cdot (T - 298.15) \text{ V}$$

**Numerical example:** For a PEMFC operating at T = 353 K (80 °C):

$$E^0(353) = 1.229 - 0.000846 \cdot (353 - 298.15)$$

$$E^0(353) = 1.229 - 0.000846 \cdot 54.85$$

$$E^0(353) = 1.229 - 0.0464 = 1.183 \text{ V}$$

This means operating above 25 °C **lowers the theoretical voltage** — but it also **accelerates reaction kinetics**, which is why SOFC (high T) compensates by achieving high current densities.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Full Cell Voltage Under Load (Polarization Curve)

The actual operating voltage of a fuel cell is the theoretical voltage minus all losses:

$$V_{cell}(i) = E_{Nernst} - \Delta V_{act} - \Delta V_{ohmic} - \Delta V_{conc}$$

**Expanding each loss term:**

$$V_{cell}(i) = E_{Nernst} - \frac{RT}{\alpha nF} \ln\!\left(\frac{i}{i_0}\right) - i \cdot ASR + \frac{RT}{nF} \ln\!\left(1 - \frac{i}{i_L}\right)$$

Where:
- **α** = charge transfer coefficient (≈ 0.5)
- **i** = current density (A/cm²)
- **i₀** = exchange current density (A/cm²)
- **i_L** = limiting current density (A/cm²)
- **ASR** = area-specific resistance (Ω·cm²)

**Numerical example (PEMFC at i = 0.5 A/cm², T = 353 K):**

Assume: E_Nernst = 1.18 V, α = 0.5, i₀ = 10⁻³ A/cm², i_L = 1.5 A/cm², ASR = 0.15 Ω·cm²

**Activation loss:**

$$\Delta V_{act} = \frac{(8.314)(353)}{(0.5)(2)(96{,}485)} \ln\!\left(\frac{0.5}{0.001}\right)$$

$$\Delta V_{act} = 0.0304 \cdot \ln(500) = 0.0304 \cdot 6.215 = 0.189 \text{ V}$$

**Ohmic loss:**

$$\Delta V_{ohmic} = 0.5 \cdot 0.15 = 0.075 \text{ V}$$

**Concentration loss:**

$$\Delta V_{conc} = \frac{(8.314)(353)}{(2)(96{,}485)} \ln\!\left(1 - \frac{0.5}{1.5}\right)$$

$$\Delta V_{conc} = 0.0152 \cdot \ln(0.6667) = 0.0152 \cdot (-0.405) = -0.0062 \text{ V (subtracted)}$$

**Total cell voltage:**

$$V_{cell} = 1.18 - 0.189 - 0.075 - (-0.0062) \cdot (\text{corrected sign})$$

$$V_{cell} \approx 0.916 \text{ V (corrected)} \approx 0.91 \text{ V}$$

This matches typical PEMFC polarization curves at moderate current density.

### 3.4 Derivation 4: SOFC Fuel Utilization Effect (Nernst Loss)

In a SOFC, not all fuel is consumed; the unused fuel exits as exhaust. The fuel utilization coefficient U_f is defined as:

$$U_f = \frac{\text{mass of fuel reacted}}{\text{mass of fuel input}}$$

For an SOFC operating at T = 1073 K (800 °C), partial pressure of H₂ drops from inlet to outlet proportionally:

$$P_{H_2,out} = P_{H_2,in} \cdot (1 - U_f)$$

The Nernst loss from incomplete fuel utilization:

$$\Delta V_{Nernst} = \frac{RT}{2F} \ln\!\left(\frac{1}{1 - U_f}\right)$$

**Example: U_f = 0.85, T = 1073 K:**

$$\Delta V = \frac{(8.314)(1073)}{(2)(96{,}485)} \ln\!\left(\frac{1}{0.15}\right)$$

$$\Delta V = 0.0462 \cdot \ln(6.667) = 0.0462 \cdot 1.897 = 0.0876 \text{ V}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **major SOFC-only loss** because fuel exits the cell. PEMFC systems recirculate unreacted hydrogen, so this loss is essentially absent there.

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | PEMFC | SOFC | AFC |
|---|---|---|---|
| Electrolyte | Nafion™ polymer | YSZ ceramic | KOH (aq) |
| Charge Carrier | H⁺ | O²⁻ | OH⁻ |
| Operating Temperature | **60–100 °C** | **600–1000 °C** | **60–250 °C** |
| Catalyst | Platinum | Nickel cermet | Ni / Ag / Pt |
| Anode Reaction | H₂ → 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ | H₂ + O²⁻ → H₂O + 2e⁻ | H₂ + 2OH⁻ → 2H₂O + 2e⁻ |
| Cathode Reaction | ½O₂ + 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂O | ½O₂ + 2e⁻ → O²⁻ | ½O₂ + H₂O + 2e⁻ → 2OH⁻ |
| Water Management | Critical (humidification) | Produced at anode | Produced at anode |
| CO₂ Sensitivity | Mild (10 ppm) | None | **Severe (forms K₂CO₃)** |
| Electrical Efficiency | 50–60 % | 50–60 % | 60–70 % |
| Cold Start-up | Yes (minutes) | **No (hours)** | Moderate |
| Power Density | High | Low–Moderate | Moderate |
| Cost (relative) | High (Pt catalyst) | Moderate (high T materials) | Low–Moderate |
| Primary Vehicle Use | Cars, buses | Heavy trucks, ships | Submarines, space |
| Maturity | Commercial | Pre-commercial | Mature (niche) |

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
Differentiate between PEMFC and SOFC based on (i) electrolyte used, (ii) operating temperature, and (iii) charge carrier.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | PEMFC | SOFC |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Electrolyte | Solid polymer membrane (Nafion) | Solid ceramic (Yttria-Stabilized Zirconia, YSZ) |
| (ii) Operating Temperature | 60–100 °C (low) | 600–1000 °C (high) |
| (iii) Charge Carrier | H⁺ (proton) | O²⁻ (oxide ion) |

PEMFC is suitable for vehicles due to fast start-up, while SOFC offers high efficiency and fuel flexibility for stationary and heavy-duty use.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
List any three advantages and two disadvantages of Alkaline Fuel Cells (AFC) compared to PEMFC.

**Model Answer:**

**Advantages of AFC over PEMFC:**
1. **Higher efficiency** (60–70% vs 50–60%) due to faster oxygen reduction kinetics in alkaline media.
2. **Cheaper catalysts** — Nickel, Silver, or non-precious metals can replace platinum.
3. **No precious metal required** for many components, reducing overall cost.

**Disadvantages of AFC:**
1. **CO₂ sensitivity** — even small amounts of CO₂ from air form K₂CO₃, poisoning the electrolyte.
2. **Liquid electrolyte management** — risk of leakage, corrosion, and freeze issues.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

**Q3. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2 Choice Question]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of neat sketches and equations, explain the working principle of a Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cell (PEMFC). State its anode and cathode half-reactions.

**(b) [7 Marks)** Compare the performance, electrolyte, operating temperature, catalyst, charge carrier, and applications of PEMFC, SOFC, and AFC in a tabular form.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — PEMFC Working Principle:**

A PEMFC consists of a **Nafion polymer membrane** sandwiched between two porous electrodes (anode and cathode), each coated with a **platinum catalyst**.

**Construction:**
- **Membrane Electrode Assembly (MEA):** Anode catalyst layer + Nafion membrane + Cathode catalyst layer.
- **Bipolar plates** on either side distribute reactant gases and collect current.
- **Humidifier** keeps the membrane hydrated for proton conduction.

**Working Principle:**
1. **Hydrogen gas** is fed to the anode side through flow channels in the bipolar plate.
2. At the anode catalyst (Pt), hydrogen molecules split into protons and electrons:
$$H_2 \rightarrow 2H^+ + 2e^-$$
3. Protons migrate through the **hydrated Nafion membrane** (which acts as a one-way sieve, allowing only H⁺) toward the cathode.
4. Electrons, blocked by the membrane, are forced through the **external circuit** — this constitutes the **useful electric current** that powers the load.
5. At the cathode catalyst (Pt), protons, electrons, and oxygen combine to form water and release heat:
$$\tfrac{1}{2}O_2 + 2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2O$$
6. Product water is removed from the cathode; heat is rejected via cooling plates.

**Net Reaction:**
$$H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O + \text{Electrical Energy} + \text{Heat}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sketches:** Students should draw: (i) a labeled cross-section of the MEA, (ii) arrows showing H₂ input, O₂ input, H⁺ ion path through membrane, e⁻ path through external circuit, and H₂O output.

**Part (b) — Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | PEMFC | SOFC | AFC |
|---|---|---|---|
| Electrolyte | Nafion (solid polymer) | YSZ (solid ceramic) | KOH (aqueous liquid) |
| Operating Temperature | 60–100 °C | 600–1000 °C | 60–250 °C |
| Charge Carrier | H⁺ | O²⁻ | OH⁻ |
| Catalyst | Platinum | Nickel cermet | Nickel / Silver / Pt |
| Electrical Efficiency | 50–60 % | 50–60 % | 60–70 % |
| Power Density | High (0.5–1 W/cm²) | Low–Moderate (0.2–0.4 W/cm²) | Moderate (~0.3 W/cm²) |
| CO₂ Sensitivity | Low (10 ppm tolerance) | None | **High** (forms K₂CO₃) |
| Startup Time | Minutes | **Hours** | Moderate |
| Applications | Cars, buses | Trucks, ships, CHP | Space, submarines |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the operating principle, half-reactions, and applications of Solid Oxide Fuel Cells (SOFC). Why is it preferred for heavy-duty vehicles?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A PEMFC operates at 80 °C with H₂ at 1.2 atm, O₂ at 1.0 atm, and product H₂O vapor at 0.05 atm. Calculate the Nernst reversible voltage.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — SOFC Principle and Applications:**

The SOFC uses a **solid ceramic electrolyte** — typically **Yttria-Stabilized Zirconia (YSZ)** — which becomes ionically conductive for O²⁻ ions at temperatures above 600 °C.

**Half-Reactions:**

At the cathode (air side), oxygen gains electrons to form oxide ions:
$$\tfrac{1}{2}O_2 + 2e^- \rightarrow O^{2-}$$

These O²⁻ ions migrate through the YSZ electrolyte to the anode (fuel side):

$$H_2 + O^{2-} \rightarrow H_2O + 2e^-$$

The electrons released flow through the external circuit, producing electricity.

**Why SOFC for Heavy-Duty Vehicles:**
1. **Higher efficiency** (50–60% electrical; up to 85% with CHP) reduces hydrogen consumption over long hauls.
2. **Fuel flexibility** — can internally reform natural gas, methanol, or biogas, not requiring pure H₂.
3. **No precious metal catalyst** — uses cheap nickel cermet, lowering cost at scale.
4. **Solid-state** — no liquid electrolyte leakage, robust for vibration-heavy truck operations.

**Applications:** Stationary power plants, Combined Heat and Power (CHP) units, large ships, heavy trucks, auxiliary power units (APUs).

**Part (b) — Numerical Nernst Calculation:**

**Given:** T = 80 °C = 353 K, P_{H₂} = 1.2 atm, P_{O₂} = 1.0 atm, P_{H₂O} = 0.05 atm, n = 2.

**Step 1 — Calculate E⁰ at 353 K:**

$$E^0(T) = 1.229 - 0.00085(T - 298.15)$$

$$E^0(353) = 1.229 - 0.00085 \cdot 54.85$$

$$E^0(353) = 1.229 - 0.0466 = 1.182 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Nernst Equation:**

$$E = E^0 + \frac{RT}{nF} \ln\!\left( \frac{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}}{P_{H_2O}} \right)$$

**Step 3 — Compute RT/nF:**

$$\frac{RT}{nF} = \frac{(8.314)(353)}{(2)(96{,}485)} = \frac{2934.8}{192{,}970} = 0.01521 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the log term:**

$$\frac{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}}{P_{H_2O}} = \frac{1.2 \cdot (1.0)^{0.5}}{0.05} = \frac{1.2}{0.05} = 24.0$$

$$\ln(24.0) = 3.178$$

**Step 5 — Final Nernst Voltage:**

$$E = 1.182 + (0.01521)(3.178) = 1.182 + 0.0483 = 1.230 \text{ V}$$

**Answer:** E_Nernst ≈ **1.23 V** at the given operating conditions.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> Students often forget to **correct E⁰ for temperature** before applying Nernst. Always compute E⁰(T) first, then use it. Also ensure natural logarithm (ln), not log₁₀. Marks are split: (2) for E⁰ correction, (2) for RT/nF calculation, (2) for log term, (1) for final answer.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Module 2:**

- [ ] **Overall Reaction:** $H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O$ (universal for all three cells)
- [ ] **Standard EMF:** $E^0 = 1.229$ V at 25 °C
- [ ] **HHV Efficiency:** 83.0 % | **LHV Efficiency:** 98.1 %
- [ ] **PEMFC:** Nafion membrane, H⁺ carrier, 60–100 °C, Pt catalyst → passenger cars
- [ ] **SOFC:** YSZ ceramic, O²⁻ carrier, 600–1000 °C, Ni catalyst → heavy trucks & stationary
- [ ] **AFC:** KOH liquid, OH⁻ carrier, 60–250 °C, Ni/Ag catalyst → space & submarines
- [ ] **Nernst Equation:** $E = E^0 + \frac{RT}{nF} \ln\!\left(\frac{P_{H_2} P_{O_2}^{1/2}}{P_{H_2O}}\right)$
- [ ] **Temperature Effect:** Higher T → lower E⁰ but better kinetics (favors SOFC at high T)
- [ ] **AFC Limitation:** CO₂ poisoning → requires pure O₂ and H₂ → limits automotive use
- [ ] **SOFC Limitation:** Slow start-up (hours) → unsuitable for personal cars
- [ ] **PEMFC Limitation:** Expensive Pt catalyst + strict water management
- [ ] **Direction Rule:** Cations move to cathode; anions move to anode (internal current)
- [ ] **Faraday's Constant:** F = 96,485 C/mol — memorize for all electrochemical calculations
- [ ] **Fuel Utilization (SOFC):** $U_f = 0.85$ typical → Nernst loss ≈ 0.09 V at 800 °C
- [ ] **Why SOFC for heavy duty?** High efficiency + fuel flexibility + no Pt + solid-state robustness
- [ ] **Why PEMFC for cars?** Fast start-up + high power density + proven commercial viability (Mirai, Nexo)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "NAP" for ion carriers:**
> - **N**afion → **P**roton (H⁺)
> - **A**lkaline (KOH) → h**y**droxide (OH⁻)
> - (YSZ → oxide O²⁻)
>
> For vehicle suitability: PEMFC = cars, SOFC = trucks, AFC = space.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hydrogen production methods: Steam methane reforming, electrolysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT832 - HYDROGEN AND FUEL CELL VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Hydrogen production methods: Steam methane reforming, electrolysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🟦 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Directive (Module 3):** This topic carries the foundational engineering weight for understanding the **upstream hydrogen economy**. As per the KTU 2024 Scheme Course Outcomes (aligned with **CO3 / CO4** depending on the elective cluster), students must be able to *compare, evaluate, and recommend* hydrogen production pathways based on efficiency, carbon intensity, and scalability.

## 1.1 What is Hydrogen Production?

**Formal KTU Definition:**
Hydrogen production refers to the set of industrial, thermochemical, and electrochemical processes used to generate molecular hydrogen (H₂) from hydrogen-rich feedstocks such as natural gas (CH₄), water (H₂O), or biomass. The output hydrogen must be separated, purified (often to **99.999% purity** for fuel cell vehicles), compressed or liquefied, and stored before dispatch to refueling stations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric for KTU Exams:**
> - **Higher Heating Value (HHV) of H₂ = 285.8 kJ/mol ≈ 39.4 kWh/kg**
> - **Lower Heating Value (LHV) of H₂ = 240.0 kJ/mol ≈ 33.3 kWh/kg**
> - **Hydrogen color coding**: Grey (fossil), Blue (fossil + CCUS), Green (renewable electrolysis)

## 1.2 The Two Pillars of This Module

| Production Route | Classification | Current Global Share | KTU Examination Weight |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Steam Methane Reforming (SMR)** | Thermochemical | **~48%** of dedicated H₂ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ (High) |
| **Electrolysis** | Electrochemical | **~2%** (but fastest growing) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ (High) |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy – "The Two Coffee Machines"

Imagine you are running a café that needs pure water for an espresso machine:

1. **Steam Methane Reforming** is like **boiling dirty river water over a natural gas stove**: You burn fossil fuel (CH₄) to create steam, which strips hydrogen out of methane. It is **fast, cheap, and dirty** — the dominant route today, producing CO₂ as a byproduct.

2. **Electrolysis** is like using a **solar-powered water purifier**: You feed **pure water** and run electricity through it. The water splits into hydrogen and oxygen cleanly. It is **expensive and slow**, but if the electricity comes from renewables (solar/wind), the hydrogen is **carbon-free (green H₂)**.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Viva:** *"SMR = Stove Method (Reforming) • Electrolysis = Electric Method (Splitting)"*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 🟧 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 STEAM METHANE REFORMING (SMR) – The Thermochemical Route

### 2.1.1 Operational Principle
SMR uses **high-temperature steam (700–900 °C)** to break the C–H bonds in methane, releasing hydrogen. It is a **catalytic, endothermic** process.

### 2.1.2 The Two-Stage Reaction Chain

**Stage 1 — Primary Reforming (Strongly Endothermic):**

$$CH_4 + H_2O \leftrightarrow CO + 3H_2 \quad \Delta H_{298} = +206 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**Stage 2 — Water-Gas Shift (WGS) Reaction (Moderately Exothermic):**

$$CO + H_2O \leftrightarrow CO_2 + H_2 \quad \Delta H_{298} = -41 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**Net Overall Reaction:**

$$CH_4 + 2H_2O \rightarrow CO_2 + 4H_2 \quad \Delta H_{net} = +165 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

### 2.1.3 Step-by-Step Process Flow (Why Each Step Exists)

| Step | Process | Engineering Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Desulfurization** | Remove H₂S (poison to Ni catalyst) via ZnO beds |
| 2 | **Pre-heating** | Heat CH₄ + H₂O mixture to ~500 °C (waste heat recovery) |
| 3 | **Primary Reformer** | Tubular reactor with **Ni/Al₂O₃** catalyst at 700–900 °C, **3–25 bar** |
| 4 | **Water-Gas Shift Reactor** | Two-stage (HT-WGS at 350 °C, LT-WGS at 200 °C) to maximize H₂ |
| 5 | **CO₂ Removal** | MEA/amine scrubbing or PSA adsorption |
| 6 | **Pressure Swing Adsorption (PSA)** | Purify H₂ to **99.99%+** for fuel cell grade |
| 7 | **Methanation (optional)** | Convert residual CO + CO₂ → CH₄ to protect PEM fuel cells |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** SMR efficiency is typically **65–75% (LHV basis)**. To produce **1 kg of H₂**, SMR consumes approximately **~3.5–4.0 kg of natural gas** and emits **~9–10 kg of CO₂** (this is **grey hydrogen**).

### 2.1.4 Why SMR Dominates the Market
- Mature technology since the **1930s**
- Lowest capital and operating cost (~$1–2/kg H₂)
- Massive existing natural gas infrastructure

---

## 2.2 ELECTROLYSIS – The Electrochemical Route

### 2.2.1 Operational Principle
Electrolysis uses **direct electrical current** to split water molecules into hydrogen and oxygen. It is governed by **Faraday's Laws of Electrolysis**.

**Half-Reactions (Acidic / PEM):**
$$2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2 \quad \text{(Cathode)}$$

$$H_2O \rightarrow \frac{1}{2}O_2 + 2H^+ + 2e^- \quad \text{(Anode)}$$

**Overall Cell Reaction:**
$$H_2O \rightarrow H_2 + \frac{1}{2}O_2 \quad \Delta H_{298} = +285.8 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

### 2.2.2 Types of Electrolyzers (KTU Exam Favorite)

| Parameter | **Alkaline (ALK)** | **PEM** | **Solid Oxide (SOEC)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Electrolyte | KOH solution (25–30%) | Proton Exchange Membrane | YSZ (Yttria-Stabilized Zirconia) |
| Operating Temp | **60–80 °C** | **50–80 °C** | **700–850 °C** |
| Efficiency (LHV) | 63–70% | 65–80% | 75–85% |
| Current Density | 0.2–0.4 A/cm² | 1.0–2.0 A/cm² | 0.3–1.0 A/cm² |
| Hydrogen Purity | 99.5–99.9% | **99.99%+** | 99.9% |
| Dynamic Response | Slow | **Fast** | Very Slow |
| KTU Use-Case | Mature, bulk plants | Vehicle/refueling | High-temp, future tech |

### 2.2.3 Why Voltage Matters (Thermodynamic Foundation)

At Standard Conditions (25 °C, 1 atm):

- **Reversible (Thermodynamic) Voltage:** $E_{rev} = \dfrac{\Delta G}{nF} = \mathbf{1.229 \text{ V}}$
- **Thermoneutral Voltage:** $E_{tn} = \dfrac{\Delta H}{nF} = \mathbf{1.481 \text{ V}}$
- **Actual Operating Voltage:** $V_{cell} = 1.8 - 2.5 \text{ V}$ (losses due to overpotentials)

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The minimum *thermodynamic* voltage is **1.229 V**, but in practice, we must supply **1.481 V** just to maintain isothermal operation. Anything above 1.481 V at a given current is dissipated as **heat (Joule losses)**.

---

## 2.3 KTU FORMULA CHEAT SHEET (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pin this table for the KTU ESE 2024 exam** — these equations appear every cycle.

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Form | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Faraday's 1st Law (Mass) | $m = \dfrac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$ | m = mass (g), I = current (A), t = time (s), n = electrons, F = **96485 C/mol** | H₂ production rate |
| 2 | Faraday's 2nd Law | $\dfrac{m_1}{m_2} = \dfrac{E_1}{E_2}$ | E = equivalent weight | Multi-species cells |
| 3 | Reversible Voltage | $E_{rev} = \dfrac{\Delta G^\circ}{nF}$ | $\Delta G^\circ = +237.2$ kJ/mol | Theoretical minimum |
| 4 | Thermoneutral Voltage | $E_{tn} = \dfrac{\Delta H^\circ}{nF}$ | $\Delta H^\circ = +285.8$ kJ/mol | Isothermal operation |
| 5 | Cell Efficiency (Voltage) | $\eta_v = \dfrac{E_{tn}}{V_{cell}}$ | V_cell = actual voltage | Electrolyzer efficiency |
| 6 | Energy Efficiency (HHV) | $\eta_E = \dfrac{m_{H_2} \cdot HHV}{V_{cell} \cdot I \cdot t}$ | HHV = 39.4 kWh/kg | Overall system |
| 7 | SMR Net Enthalpy | $\Delta H_{net} = 2(\Delta H_{SMR}) + \Delta H_{WGS}$ | kJ/mol | Reaction design |
| 8 | SMR Stoichiometric Ratio | $S/C = \dfrac{mol \ H_2O}{mol \ CH_4}$ | Ratio = 2.5–3.0 typical | Reformer design |

> [!TIP]
> **Remember:** $\mathbf{F = 96485 \text{ C/mol}}$ appears in 90% of KTU numericals. Commit it to memory.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application | Why It Matters |
|---|---|---|
| **Automotive** | Refueling stations for FCEV (Toyota Mirai, Hyundai Nexo) | Need 99.99% pure H₂ for PEM fuel cells |
| **Steel Industry** | Direct Reduced Iron (DRI) using green H₂ | Replace coke in blast furnaces |
| **Grid Storage** | Power-to-Gas (P2G) | Store surplus solar/wind as H₂ |
| **Aviation** | e-Fuels synthesis | H₂ + CO₂ → synthetic jet fuel |
| **Ammonia Synthesis** | Haber-Bosch process | H₂ + N₂ → NH₃ for fertilizers |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🟥 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Thermodynamic Voltage of an Electrolyzer

We derive the minimum reversible voltage required to split water at standard conditions.

**Given:**
- Standard Gibbs free energy of formation: $\Delta G_f^\circ(H_2O, l) = -237.2$ kJ/mol
- Standard enthalpy of formation: $\Delta H_f^\circ(H_2O, l) = -285.8$ kJ/mol
- Faraday's constant: $F = 96485$ C/mol
- Number of electrons transferred per mole of H₂: $n = 2$

### Step 1 — Net Gibbs Free Energy Change
For the reaction $H_2O(l) \rightarrow H_2(g) + \frac{1}{2}O_2(g)$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta G_{rxn}^\circ &= \left[\Delta G_f^\circ(H_2) + \tfrac{1}{2}\Delta G_f^\circ(O_2)\right] - \Delta G_f^\circ(H_2O) \\
&= \left[(0) + \tfrac{1}{2}(0)\right] - (-237.2) \\
&= +237.2 \text{ kJ/mol}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2 — Apply Electrical Work Equation
The maximum electrical work obtainable (or required) from a reaction is $W_{elec} = nFE$. Equating to Gibbs free energy:

$$nFE_{rev} = \Delta G^\circ$$

### Step 3 — Solve for E_rev

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{rev} &= \dfrac{\Delta G^\circ}{nF} \\
&= \dfrac{237200 \text{ J/mol}}{(2)(96485 \text{ C/mol})} \\
&= \dfrac{237200}{192970} \\
&= \mathbf{1.229 \text{ V}}
\end{aligned}$$

> ✅ **Result verified against standard electrochemistry references.**

### Step 4 — Derive Thermoneutral Voltage

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{tn} &= \dfrac{\Delta H^\circ}{nF} \\
&= \dfrac{285800}{(2)(96485)} \\
&= \dfrac{285800}{192970} \\
&= \mathbf{1.481 \text{ V}}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5 — Physical Interpretation

| Voltage | Meaning | Energy Mode |
|---|---|---|
| 1.229 V | Electrical energy needed (reversible) | Pure electrical work |
| (1.481 − 1.229) = **0.252 V** | Additional heat needed | Thermal energy (T·ΔS) |
| 1.481 V | Total (electrical + thermal) | Thermoneutral point |

> If $V_{cell} > E_{tn}$, the excess electrical energy dissipates as **Joule heating** — wasted.

---

## 3.2 Derivative Problem: H₂ Production via Faraday's Law (KTU Numerical Type)

> **Problem:** An alkaline electrolyzer operates at a current of **500 A** for **1 hour**. The cell uses **n = 2** electrons per molecule of H₂. Calculate:
> (a) The mass of H₂ produced (in grams)
> (b) The mass of O₂ produced (in grams)
> (c) The volume of H₂ at STP (in liters)

**Given:**
- I = 500 A, t = 1 hr = 3600 s, n = 2, F = 96485 C/mol
- M(H₂) = 2 g/mol, M(O₂) = 32 g/mol
- Molar volume at STP = 22.4 L/mol

### Part (a) — Mass of H₂

$$\begin{aligned}
Q &= I \times t = 500 \times 3600 = 1.8 \times 10^6 \text{ C} \\
m_{H_2} &= \dfrac{M_{H_2} \cdot Q}{n \cdot F} \\
&= \dfrac{2 \times 1.8 \times 10^6}{2 \times 96485} \\
&= \dfrac{3.6 \times 10^6}{192970} \\
&= \mathbf{18.65 \text{ g}}
\end{aligned}$$

### Part (b) — Mass of O₂

For O₂, n = 4 electrons per molecule:

$$\begin{aligned}
m_{O_2} &= \dfrac{32 \times 1.8 \times 10^6}{4 \times 96485} \\
&= \dfrac{5.76 \times 10^7}{385940} \\
&= \mathbf{149.2 \text{ g}}
\end{aligned}$$

### Part (c) — Volume of H₂ at STP

$$\begin{aligned}
n_{H_2} &= \dfrac{m_{H_2}}{M_{H_2}} = \dfrac{18.65}{2} = 9.325 \text{ mol} \\
V_{H_2} &= n_{H_2} \times 22.4 = 9.325 \times 22.4 \\
&= \mathbf{208.9 \text{ L}}
\end{aligned}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivative Problem: SMR Energy Balance (KTU 14-Mark Type)

> **Problem:** A steam methane reformer processes **1000 mol/hr** of CH₄ with a steam-to-carbon ratio (S/C) of **3.0**.
> Determine:
> (a) The moles of H₂ produced per hour (assume 100% conversion + 100% WGS)
> (b) Total heat duty (kW) for the reformer
> (c) CO₂ emitted per kg of H₂

**Given:**
- Feed: 1000 mol/hr CH₄, S/C = 3.0 → 3000 mol/hr H₂O
- $\Delta H_{SMR} = +206$ kJ/mol, $\Delta H_{WGS} = -41$ kJ/mol

### Part (a) — H₂ Produced

$$\begin{aligned}
CH_4 + 2H_2O &\rightarrow CO_2 + 4H_2 \\
\text{Per mol CH}_4: 4 \text{ mol H}_2 \\
\text{For 1000 mol/hr: } 4 \times 1000 &= \mathbf{4000 \text{ mol/hr H}_2}
\end{aligned}$$

Mass flow: $4000 \times 2 = 8000$ g/hr = **8 kg/hr H₂**

### Part (b) — Total Reformer Heat Duty

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{SMR} &= 1000 \times 206 = 206000 \text{ kJ/hr} \\
Q_{WGS} &= 1000 \times (-41) = -41000 \text{ kJ/hr} \quad (\text{heat released}) \\
Q_{net} &= 206000 - 41000 = 165000 \text{ kJ/hr} \\
Q_{net} \text{ in kW} &= \dfrac{165000}{3600} = \mathbf{45.83 \text{ kW}}
\end{aligned}$$

### Part (c) — CO₂ per kg of H₂

Per 1000 mol CH₄: 1 mol CO₂ per CH₄ → **1000 mol CO₂/hr**

$$\begin{aligned}
m_{CO_2} &= 1000 \times 44 = 44000 \text{ g/hr} = 44 \text{ kg/hr} \\
\text{CO}_2/\text{kg H}_2 &= \dfrac{44}{8} = \mathbf{5.5 \text{ kg CO}_2 / \text{kg H}_2}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note:** KTU textbook numbers cite **~9 kg CO₂/kg H₂** for full lifecycle SMR including furnace fuel. Our reformer-only calculation gives 5.5; the difference comes from the **external combustion of natural gas** to provide reformer heat.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (For 14-Mark Answers)

| Parameter | **SMR (Grey H₂)** | **PEM Electrolysis (Green H₂)** |
|---|---|---|
| Production cost (USD/kg) | $1.0 – $2.0 | $3.0 – $8.0 |
| Energy efficiency | 65 – 75% | 60 – 80% |
| CO₂ emissions (kg/kg H₂) | 9 – 10 | **0** (if renewable) |
| Capital cost | Moderate | High |
| Maturity | Fully commercial | Scaling rapidly |
| Purity | 99.99% (after PSA) | 99.99%+ (after dryer) |
| Best suited for | Bulk industrial H₂ | Decentralized, transport |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🟩 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Steam Methane Reforming (SMR) Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Natural Gas CH4 Feed] --> B[Desulfurizer ZnO Bed]
    C[Deionized Water H2O] --> D[Steam Generator 700C]
    B --> E[Preheater Mixed Feed]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Primary Reformer Ni Catalyst 800C 20 bar]
    F --> G[High Temp WGS Reactor 350C]
    G --> H[Low Temp WGS Reactor 200C]
    H --> I[CO2 Removal Amine Scrubber]
    I --> J[Pressure Swing Adsorption PSA]
    J --> K[Pure H2 99.99 percent Compression]
    J --> L[CO2 Vent or Sequestration]
    K --> M[Hydrogen Storage Buffer Tank]
    M --> N[FCEV Refueling Station]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: PEM Electrolyzer Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P[Renewable Power Source Solar or Wind] --> R[Rectifier AC to DC]
    R --> CELL[PEM Electrolyzer Stack]
    W1[Deionized Water Tank] --> PUMP[Water Pump and Circulation Unit]
    PUMP --> CELL
    CELL --> ANODE[Anode Side O2 Output]
    CELL --> CATHODE[Cathode Side H2 Output]
    ANODE --> SEP1[O2 Separator and Vent]
    CATHODE --> DRY[H2 Gas Dryer]
    DRY --> COMP[H2 Compressor to 350 bar or 700 bar]
    COMP --> TANK[High Pressure H2 Storage Tank]
    TANK --> DISP[Dispenser for FCEV Refueling]
    CELL --> CTRL[Control Unit Monitoring Voltage Temperature Pressure]
    CTRL --> R
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Comparison of H₂ Production Pathways

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FossilRoute[Fossil Feedstock]
    NG[Natural Gas CH4] --> SMR1[Steam Methane Reforming]
    COAL[Coal] --> GAS[Coal Gasification]
    end
    SMR1 --> GREYH2[Grey H2 9 kg CO2 per kg H2]
    GAS --> GREYH2_B[Grey Brown H2]
    SMR1 --> CCS[Carbon Capture and Storage] --> BLUEH2[Blue H2 1 to 3 kg CO2 per kg H2]
    subgraph RenewableRoute[Renewable Feedstock]
    WATER[Water H2O] --> ELECPEM[PEM Electrolyzer]
    WATER --> ELECALK[Alkaline Electrolyzer]
    WATER --> ELECSOEC[Solid Oxide Electrolyzer]
    SOLAR[Solar PV] --> ELECPEM
    WIND[Wind Turbine] --> ELECPEM
    NUCLEAR[Nuclear Power] --> ELECPEM
    end
    ELECPEM --> GREENH2[Green H2 0 kg CO2 per kg H2]
    ELECALK --> GREENH2
    ELECSOEC --> GREENH2
    GREYH2 --> STORAGE[H2 Storage and Transport]
    BLUEH2 --> STORAGE
    GREENH2 --> STORAGE
    STORAGE --> FCEV[Fuel Cell Electric Vehicles]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Voltage vs. Current Density (Polarization Curve)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PolarizationCurve[Typical PEM Electrolyzer Polarization]
    A[Low Current Activation Loss Region] --> B[Kinetics Dominated 1.4 to 1.8 V]
    B --> C[Ohmic Region Linear 1.5 to 2.0 V]
    C --> D[Concentration Loss High Current 2.0 plus V]
    end
    E[Reversible Erev 1.229 V] -.Reference.-> A
    F[Thermoneutral Etn 1.481 V] -.Reference.-> B
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU:** The polarization curve plots **cell voltage (Y-axis)** against **current density (X-axis)**. The **point where the curve crosses 1.481 V** is the *thermoneutral operating point* — most commercial electrolyzers are sized to operate just slightly above this point for maximum efficiency.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->
