<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define "State of Charge (SoC)" and "Depth of Discharge (DoD)" of a battery. Why are both important for EV battery management?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**State of Charge (SoC):** It is the ratio of the *remaining capacity* $Q_{rem}$ in a battery to its *rated capacity* $Q_{rated}$, expressed as a percentage.

$$SoC = \frac{Q_{rem}}{Q_{rated}} \times 100\%$$

**Depth of Discharge (DoD):** It is the complement of SoC; the percentage of the battery's rated capacity that has been discharged.

$$DoD = 100\% - SoC$$

**Importance in EV BMS (1 mark):**
- SoC tells the driver the remaining range (energy left).
- DoD tells the BMS how much stress the battery has been subjected to.
- Cycling beyond 80% DoD drastically shortens Li-ion life (e.g., 100% DoD → ~500 cycles; 50% DoD → ~2000 cycles).
- BMS uses SoC to decide when to cut off discharge and prevent over-discharge (which causes Cu dissolution and cell death).

*(Total: 1 + 1 + 1 = 3 marks)*

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List any THREE advantages of using a supercapacitor over a battery in an EV's regenerative braking system.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Very high specific power (5–15 kW/kg)** — supercapacitors can absorb the full regen-braking pulse (~50–100 kW transient) without voltage sag, while a battery would suffer severe voltage drop and Peukert losses.

2. **Extremely long cycle life (>1,000,000 cycles)** — a regen-braking event may occur every 200 m in city driving (≈ 1,000+ events per day); a Li-ion battery lasts only ~1000–3000 cycles, so it cannot survive a year of full regen duty.

3. **Fast charge acceptance (seconds)** — supercapacitors can absorb 100% SoC in < 10 s, which matches the deceleration event duration. Batteries take 30–60 min for full charge.

*(1 mark each — Total: 3 marks)*

---

### **PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 3]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the **construction and working principle of a Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cell (PEMFC)**. Write the half-cell and overall reactions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **PEM fuel cell** operates with a hydrogen flow of 0.5 g/s and air flow of 8 g/s. Calculate: (i) the theoretical hydrogen consumption rate at standard conditions, (ii) the theoretical reversible cell voltage $E_{rev}$, and (iii) the maximum electrical power output if all H₂ is converted at $E_{rev}$. Given: $\Delta G^\circ = -237.2$ kJ/mol, $F = 96485$ C/mol, M(H₂) = 2 g/mol. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Construction and Working of PEMFC (7 marks):**

A PEMFC consists of a polymer electrolyte membrane (typically Nafion™) sandwiched between two porous gas-diffusion electrodes (anode and cathode), with platinum catalysts on both sides and bipolar plates with flow channels for reactant distribution.

**Components:**
- **Anode:** Pt/C catalyst on porous carbon paper; H₂ inlet, produces H⁺ and e⁻.
- **Cathode:** Pt/C catalyst; O₂/air inlet, water product.
- **Membrane (Nafion 117/115):** Conducts H⁺ but blocks e⁻ and gases.
- **Bipolar plates:** Graphite or metal; distribute gas and conduct current.
- **Gaskets:** Seal the cell edges.
- **Operating temperature:** 60–80 °C, 1–3 atm.

**Half-cell reactions:**
- Anode: $H_2 \rightarrow 2H^+ + 2e^-$
- Cathode: $\tfrac{1}{2}O_2 + 2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2O$
- Overall: $H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O$ ($E^\circ = 1.229$ V)

**Working:** H₂ at the anode splits into protons and electrons (catalyst-assisted). Protons migrate through the membrane to the cathode; electrons travel through the external circuit (producing current) and reach the cathode, where they combine with O₂ and H⁺ to form water. The byproduct water must be removed to avoid flooding.

**Sketch (textual representation):**

```
 |Bipolar Plate|  |Membrane|  |Bipolar Plate|
 |   H2 in    |--|  Nafion|--|  O2 in      |
 |   GDL      |  |(H+    )|  |   GDL       |
 |   Pt/C     |  | flows )|  |   Pt/C      |
 |            |  |  H+   )|  |             |
 |     ←e−————————→ LOAD  ←————————e−         |
 |         H2O OUT (cathode side)            |
```

*(Component description: 3 marks; Half-cell + overall reactions: 2 marks; Working principle + sketch: 2 marks)*

---

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

**(i) Theoretical H₂ consumption rate:**

Molar flow rate of H₂:
$$\dot{n}_{H_2} = \frac{\dot{m}_{H_2}}{M_{H_2}} = \frac{0.5 \text{ g/s}}{2 \text{ g/mol}} = 0.25 \text{ mol/s}$$

**(ii) Reversible cell voltage (per cell):**
$$E_{rev} = -\frac{\Delta G}{nF} = -\frac{(-237200)}{2 \times 96485}$$
$$E_{rev} = \frac{237200}{192970} = 1.2292 \text{ V}$$

**(iii) Maximum electrical power:**

Current produced by 0.25 mol/s of H₂ (each H₂ gives 2 e⁻):
$$I = n_{electrons} \cdot \dot{n}_{H_2} \cdot F = 2 \times 0.25 \times 96485$$
$$I = 48242.5 \text{ A}$$

Power per cell at $E_{rev}$:
$$P_{cell} = E_{rev} \cdot I = 1.2292 \times 48242.5 \approx 59.30 \text{ kW}$$

(Equivalently, $P = \dot{n}_{H_2} \cdot (-\Delta G) = 0.25 \times 237200 = 59.30$ kW.)

*(Step i: 2 marks; Step ii: 2 marks; Step iii: 3 marks)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 3]**

**(a)** Explain the **construction and working of an Electrochemical Double-Layer Capacitor (EDLC)**. Derive the energy-storage expression. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **supercapacitor module** has 12 series-connected cells. Each cell: $C = 3000$ F, $V_{max} = 2.7$ V, $R_{ESR} = 0.8$ mΩ. Calculate: (i) the equivalent capacitance, equivalent ESR, and module voltage, (ii) the energy stored at full charge, and (iii) the maximum power deliverable (matched load). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) EDLC Construction, Working, and Energy Derivation (7 marks):**

**Construction:** Two porous carbon (activated carbon) electrodes are immersed in an electrolyte (organic: acetonitrile + salt, or aqueous: H₂SO₄/KOH). A separator (porous polymer) prevents physical contact. Current collectors (aluminium) close the structure.

**Working principle:** When voltage is applied, ions in the electrolyte migrate to oppositely charged electrodes, forming a *Helmholtz double layer* (thickness ≈ 0.3–1 nm) at each interface. The result is effectively **two capacitors in series** (one at each electrode). Energy is stored *electrostatically*, not chemically — no redox reaction occurs. This is the reason EDLCs have such long cycle life.

**Capacitance per electrode:**
$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$$

where $A$ is huge (~2000 m²/g for activated carbon), and $d$ is nanometre-scale — yielding very high C per cell.

**Energy derivation (from $V = Q/C$, $W = \int V \, dQ$):**
$$E = \int_0^Q V(q) \, dq = \int_0^Q \frac{q}{C} \, dq = \frac{1}{C} \cdot \frac{Q^2}{2} = \frac{Q^2}{2C}$$

Substituting $Q = C \cdot V$:
$$E = \frac{(C V)^2}{2C} = \frac{C V^2}{2}$$

Thus, $E_{SC} = \tfrac{1}{2} C V^2$.

*(Construction: 2 marks; Working: 2 marks; Energy derivation: 3 marks)*

---

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

**(i) Series combination:**

For $N$ capacitors in series:
$$C_{eq} = \frac{C}{N} = \frac{3000}{12} = 250 \text{ F}$$
$$R_{ESR,eq} = N \cdot R_{ESR} = 12 \times 0.8 \text{ m}\Omega = 9.6 \text{ m}\Omega = 0.0096 \text{ }\Omega$$
$$V_{module} = N \cdot V_{cell} = 12 \times 2.7 = 32.4 \text{ V}$$

**(ii) Energy stored at full charge:**

$$E_{SC} = \tfrac{1}{2} C_{eq} V_{module}^2 = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 250 \times (32.4)^2$$
$$E_{SC} = 125 \times 1049.76 = 131220 \text{ J} \approx 36.45 \text{ Wh}$$

**(iii) Maximum power (matched load):**

$$P_{max} = \frac{V_{module}^2}{4 R_{ESR,eq}} = \frac{(32.4)^2}{4 \times 0.0096}$$
$$P_{max} = \frac{1049.76}{0.0384} = 27337.5 \text{ W} \approx 27.34 \text{ kW}$$

*(Step i: 2 marks; Step ii: 2 marks; Step iii: 3 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Warnings:**
> 1. **PEMFC equation balance:** A common error is writing the *cathode* half-reaction as $O_2 + 4H^+ + 4e^- \rightarrow 2H_2O$ (4-electron) — this is for alkaline/cathode-only. For PEMFC, it is $\tfrac{1}{2}O_2 + 2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2O$ (per 2 electrons). **Match electrons with the anode equation** to avoid losing 1 mark.
> 2. **Supercapacitor series ESR:** Many students forget ESR *adds* in series (resistor sum), while capacitance *divides* (like parallel resistor). This is the opposite of intuition built from batteries.
> 3. **Flywheel moment of inertia:** $I = \tfrac{1}{2} m r^2$ is for a **solid disc**. If the question says *hoop, ring, or thin-walled cylinder*, it becomes $I = m r^2$. **Always read the rotor geometry carefully.**
> 4. **Fuel cell efficiency:** Never use Carnot efficiency formulae for fuel cells. Use $\eta = \Delta G / \Delta H$. Conflating these is a guaranteed 2-mark loss.
> 5. **Units:** Faraday constant is 96485 **C/mol** (not A·h/mol). Converting C to A·h requires dividing by 3600. Many students lose marks by inconsistent units.

---

### **Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)**

✅ **Energy storage** in HEVs/EVs serves three functions: *energy supply, regenerative capture, and load-levelling*.

✅ The four classes are: **(i) Battery (electrochemical-closed), (ii) Fuel cell + H₂ tank (electrochemical-open), (iii) Supercapacitor (electrostatic), (iv) Flywheel (kinetic)**.

✅ **Key metrics to memorise:** specific energy (Wh/kg), specific power (W/kg), cycle life, round-trip efficiency, SoC, DoD, C-rate.

✅ **Ragone plot** is the master design chart — *high energy* devices are on the right, *high power* devices are on top. No single technology covers both.

✅ **PEMFC** reversible voltage = **1.229 V**; theoretical max efficiency = **83%** (HHV); operates at 60–80 °C.

✅ **EDLC** stores energy as $\tfrac{1}{2}CV^2$; no chemical reaction → 10⁶ cycles; cell voltage limited to ~2.7 V by electrolyte decomposition.

✅ **Flywheel** stores $\tfrac{1}{2}I\omega^2$; solid disc $I = \tfrac{1}{2}mr^2$, thin-walled cylinder $I = mr^2$; rotor speed limited by $\sigma_{uts}/\rho$.

✅ **HESS** = Battery + Supercapacitor (or Flywheel) is the modern engineering answer — *battery for energy, SC for power*.

✅ **FCEV** = Fuel cell stack + small Li-ion buffer battery + SC for peak transient power (Toyota Mirai architecture).

✅ **Faraday's constant** $F = 96485$ C/mol; **LHV of H₂** = 33.33 kWh/kg; **HHV of H₂** = 39.4 kWh/kg.

✅ **Peukert's effect:** At higher discharge C-rates, the battery delivers *less* total energy. Most pronounced in lead-acid.

✅ **Activation, Ohmic, Concentration** — the three overpotential regions in a fuel cell polarization curve (in that order, from open-circuit to short-circuit).

✅ **For Series SC modules:** $C_{eq} = C/N$, $R_{ESR,eq} = N \cdot R_{ESR}$, $V_{total} = N \cdot V_{cell}$.

✅ **For Flywheel specific energy limit:** $E_{sp,max} \propto \sigma_{uts}/\rho$ — drives the choice of carbon-fibre composite rotors over steel.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hybridization of energy storage devices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT832 - HYDROGEN AND FUEL CELL VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Hybridization of energy storage devices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hybridization of Energy Storage Devices (HESS)** in the context of Hydrogen and Fuel Cell Vehicles (HFCVs) is defined as the **systematic integration of two or more heterogeneous energy storage and conversion units** — typically a Proton Exchange Membrane (PEM) fuel cell as the primary energy source, combined with at least one secondary energy storage system (rechargeable battery and/or Electric Double-Layer Capacitor / supercapacitor) — interconnected through DC-DC power electronic converters, and governed by an **Energy Management Strategy (EMS)** to optimally satisfy the dynamic power demand of the vehicle's electric drivetrain.

The hybrid energy storage system (HESS) is engineered to compensate for the inherent weaknesses of any single storage device by exploiting the complementary characteristics of the others, thereby improving **vehicle range, dynamic response, braking energy recovery, fuel cell durability, and overall powertrain efficiency**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Relay-Race" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Three Runners of a Marathon Team**
> Imagine a vehicle's drivetrain as a relay race team trying to cover **steady cruising**, **sudden acceleration**, and **braking energy recovery** all in a single race:
>
> - **PEM Fuel Cell** → the *marathon runner*: tireless, supplies steady long-term energy (high **energy density ~350–1000 Wh/kg**), but slow to accelerate its pace (response time 1–5 s) and cannot instantly sprint.
> - **Lithium-ion Battery** → the *middle-distance runner*: versatile, handles moderate bursts and energy buffering (energy density ~150–250 Wh/kg, power density ~250–340 W/kg), but gets exhausted if forced to sprint continuously.
> - **Supercapacitor (EDLC)** → the *100 m sprinter*: explosive instantaneous power (power density **>10 000 W/kg**), but tires in seconds (low energy density ~5–10 Wh/kg).
>
> A **hybridization** of these three gives the team complete coverage: the fuel cell sets the cruise pace, the battery provides endurance, and the supercapacitor handles every sharp turn, hill-climb, and regenerative-braking spike.

## 1.3 Why Hybridization? — The Limitations of a "Stand-alone" Fuel Cell

A standalone fuel cell vehicle, though zero-emission at point of use, suffers from several practical limitations that prevent direct drive without hybridization. These limitations are precisely the engineering motivations for the HESS architecture.

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Engineering Limitations of a Solo PEM Fuel Cell:**
> 1. **Sluggish dynamic response** — time constant of 1–5 s for hydrogen/air flow adjustment.
> 2. **Oxygen-starvation** during sudden load transients → membrane degradation.
> 3. **Inefficient regenerative braking** absorption.
> 4. **No idle-power optimization** — fuel cell cannot simply be "switched off" instantly.
> 5. **Cold-start delay** — minutes to reach operating temperature (~60–80 °C).
> 6. **Auxiliary parasitic loads** (pumps, compressor) reduce net efficiency.

## 1.4 The Three Energy Buffers in Modern HFCV Architectures

| Component | Energy Density (Wh/kg) | Power Density (W/kg) | Cycle Life | Response Time |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **PEM Fuel Cell** | 350 – 1000 | 200 – 600 | ~5 000 h (degrades slowly) | 1 – 5 s |
| **Li-ion Battery** | 150 – 250 | 250 – 340 | 1 000 – 3 000 cycles | < 0.1 s |
| **Supercapacitor (EDLC)** | 5 – 10 | **> 10 000** | > 500 000 cycles | < 1 ms |

The numerical contrast above is the **fundamental justification** for hybridization: no single device simultaneously offers high *energy* density, high *power* density, and long *cycle life* — the HESS combines the best of each world.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Canonical HESS Configurations

The classification of HESS topologies is determined by **where the DC-DC converter(s) are placed** relative to the fuel cell, battery, and supercapacitor. This determines the degree of controllability and the cost of the system.

### (a) Passive (Direct-Parallel) Configuration

The simplest and cheapest topology. All sources are connected directly in parallel to the DC bus.

- **Merits**: zero converter losses, lowest cost, highest theoretical efficiency.
- **Demerits**: terminal voltages of all devices are forced equal; **no independent control** of power split; poor voltage regulation; risk of reverse current flow into the fuel cell during regenerative braking.

### (b) Semi-Active (Semi-Passive) Configurations

Exactly **one** power source is connected to the DC bus through a unidirectional or bidirectional DC-DC converter; the other is connected directly.

- **Variant 1** — FC direct, Battery through converter, Supercapacitor direct.
- **Variant 2** — FC direct, Battery direct, Supercapacitor through converter.
- **Variant 3** — FC through converter, Battery direct, Supercapacitor direct.
- **Merits**: cost-effective balance; allows control of peak transient or battery current.
- **Demerits**: still partial control; one device's voltage is dictated by the bus.

### (c) Fully-Active (Dual-Converter) Configuration

Both the battery **and** the supercapacitor are interfaced via their own dedicated bidirectional DC-DC converters, while the fuel cell may also be connected through a unidirectional boost converter.

- **Merits**: complete, decoupled control of each energy source; the fuel cell can be operated at its **maximum-efficiency point** regardless of the instantaneous load; full regenerative braking capture; longest fuel-cell life.
- **Demerits**: highest cost, highest converter losses, complex control software.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiners' Focus Point**: The fully-active configuration is the **industry standard** in modern FCEVs (e.g., Toyota Mirai, Hyundai NEXO) because it allows the **Energy Management Strategy (EMS)** to dynamically split power between the three sources.

## 2.2 Power-Flow Logic & The Energy Management Strategy (EMS)

The EMS is the "brain" of the HESS. It decides, at every millisecond, **how much power each source must deliver** (or absorb) so that the fuel cell operates in its high-efficiency region, the battery's State-of-Charge (SoC) stays within safe limits, and the supercapacitor covers all high-frequency transients.

The general power-balance equation (a fundamental KTU identity) is:

$$
P_{\text{demand}}(t) = P_{\text{FC}}(t) + P_{\text{Bat}}(t) + P_{\text{SC}}(t) - P_{\text{aux}}(t)
$$

where $P_{\text{aux}}$ represents the parasitic loads (pumps, fans, control electronics).

### 2.2.1 Classification of EMS Algorithms

> [!NOTE]
> **Two Major EMS Families (Frequently asked in KTU Part B 14-mark questions):**
>
> 1. **Rule-Based (Heuristic) Strategies**
>    - *Deterministic Rule-Based*: If–Then statements (e.g., "if $P_{\text{demand}} > P_{\text{FC,rated}}$, discharge battery").
>    - *Fuzzy-Logic Rule-Based*: linguistic rules + membership functions; smooth, robust, no mathematical model required.
>
> 2. **Optimization-Based Strategies**
>    - *Equivalent Consumption Minimization Strategy (ECMS)*: real-time minimization of equivalent hydrogen consumption.
>    - *Dynamic Programming (DP)*: offline global optimum using Bellman's principle.
>    - *Pontryagin's Minimum Principle (PMP)*: continuous-time optimal control.
>    - *Frequency-Decoupling (Wavelet/FFT)*: load signal is split into low-frequency (→ battery/FC) and high-frequency (→ supercapacitor) components.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **every equation, definition, and boundary condition** you must memorize for the University Examination on this topic.

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Variable Meaning & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Global Power Balance** | $P_{\text{load}}(t) = P_{\text{FC}}(t) + P_{\text{Bat}}(t) + P_{\text{SC}}(t)$ | All in **W (Watts)**; algebraic sign convention: discharge (+), charge (−). |
| 2 | **Energy supplied over time** | $E = \displaystyle\int_{t_1}^{t_2} P(t)\,dt$ | $E$ in **Joules (J)** or **Wh**. |
| 3 | **Battery State of Charge (SoC)** | $\mathrm{SoC}(t) = \mathrm{SoC}(t_0) - \dfrac{1}{Q_{\max}}\displaystyle\int_{t_0}^{t} I_{\text{Bat}}(\tau)\,d\tau$ | $Q_{\max}$ in **Ah**, $I$ in **A**. |
| 4 | **Supercapacitor stored energy** | $E_{\text{SC}} = \dfrac{1}{2}\,C\,V^{2}$ | $C$ in **F**, $V$ in **V**, $E$ in **J**. |
| 5 | **Usable SC energy window** | $E_{\text{SC,usable}} = \dfrac{1}{2}\,C\,\bigl(V_{\max}^{2} - V_{\min}^{2}\bigr)$ | $V_{\min} \approx \dfrac{1}{2} V_{\max}$ in practice. |
| 6 | **SC capacitance from peak-power demand** | $C \geq \dfrac{2\,P_{\text{peak}}\,\Delta t}{V_{\max}^{2} - V_{\min}^{2}}$ | $\Delta t$ = transient duration in **s**. |
| 7 | **FC hydrogen consumption** | $m_{H_2}(t) = \displaystyle\int \dfrac{P_{\text{FC}}(t)}{\eta_{\text{FC}}(P_{\text{FC}})\,LHV_{H_2}}\,dt$ | $LHV_{H_2} = 120\,\text{MJ/kg}$; $m$ in **kg**. |
| 8 | **Fuel-cell system efficiency** | $\eta_{\text{FC,sys}} = \dfrac{\eta_{\text{stack}}\,V_{\text{cell}}}{1.48\,\text{V}}$ | Polarization losses included. |
| 9 | **HESS specific energy** | $E_{\text{HESS}} = E_{\text{FC}} + E_{\text{Bat}} + E_{\text{SC}}$ | Sum of available energy. |
| 10 | **HESS round-trip efficiency** | $\eta_{\text{HESS}} = \dfrac{\displaystyle\int P_{\text{traction}}\,dt}{\displaystyle\int P_{\text{fuel}} + P_{\text{grid(in)}}\,dt}$ | Dimensionless (0 < η < 1). |
| 11 | **Ragone-plot specific power** | $P_{\text{sp}} = \dfrac{P}{m_{\text{device}}}$ | **W/kg**. |
| 12 | **Frequency-decoupling cutoff** | $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC_{\text{filter}}}$ | Used in wavelet/FFT split. |

## 2.4 Engineering Utility & Real-World Deployment

The HESS is **not an academic curiosity** — it is the architectural foundation of every commercial fuel cell vehicle on roads today:

- **Toyota Mirai (Gen 2)**: PEMFC + 1.24 kWh Li-ion battery (semi-active).
- **Hyundai NEXO**: PEMFC + 1.56 kWh Li-ion polymer (semi-active).
- **Honda Clarity**: PEMFC + 171 kWh Li-ion (semi-active).
- **Mercedes-Benz GLC F-CELL**: PEMFC + 13.5 kWh Li-ion (plug-in hybrid).

These vehicles confirm that the HESS principle is **industry-mandated** for any FCEV intended to satisfy real-world drive cycles such as **NEDC, WLTP, and UDDS**, where peak-to-average power ratios can reach **8:1 to 12:1**.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide:** Every equation below is derived line-by-line, with the **physical reasoning** written out in full. There are no skipped steps. Multi-line derivations are wrapped inside `\begin{aligned}…\end{aligned}` blocks as mandated.

## 3.1 Derivation #1 — Capacitance of a Supercapacitor Sized to Cover a Known Transient

**Engineering Problem Statement:** *A fuel-cell hybrid vehicle must deliver a peak transient power of $P_{\text{peak}} = 60\,\text{kW}$ for $\Delta t = 4$ seconds. The supercapacitor bank operates between $V_{\max} = 48$ V and $V_{\min} = 24$ V. Determine the minimum required capacitance to absorb the transient entirely.*

### Step 1 — Write the energy demand of the transient

The energy that must be supplied by the supercapacitor during the peak is:

$$
E_{\text{transient}} = P_{\text{peak}} \cdot \Delta t
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute numerical values

$$
E_{\text{transient}} = 60{,}000 \;\text{W} \times 4 \;\text{s} = 240{,}000 \;\text{J}
$$

### Step 3 — Write the usable energy stored in a supercapacitor

The energy released by a capacitor when its voltage drops from $V_{\max}$ to $V_{\min}$ is:

$$
E_{\text{SC,usable}} = \frac{1}{2}\,C\,(V_{\max}^{2} - V_{\min}^{2})
$$

This is the **fundamental capacitor energy equation**, derived by integration of $P = V \cdot C \cdot dV/dt$ over the voltage range.

### Step 4 — Apply the equality $E_{\text{SC,usable}} \geq E_{\text{transient}}$

$$
\frac{1}{2}\,C\,(V_{\max}^{2} - V_{\min}^{2}) \;\geq\; P_{\text{peak}}\,\Delta t
$$

### Step 5 — Solve for C algebraically

$$
C \;\geq\; \frac{2\,P_{\text{peak}}\,\Delta t}{V_{\max}^{2} - V_{\min}^{2}}
$$

### Step 6 — Evaluate numerator and denominator

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Numerator} &= 2 \times 60{,}000 \times 4 \;=\; 480{,}000 \;\text{W·s} \\
\text{Denominator} &= 48^{2} - 24^{2} \;=\; 2304 - 576 \;=\; 1728 \;\text{V}^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 7 — Final numerical answer

$$
\boxed{\,C \;\geq\; \frac{480{,}000}{1728} \;\approx\; 277.8 \;\text{F}\,}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result Interpretation:** A single 300 F / 48 V commercial Maxwell-type supercapacitor module would be sufficient. This is **why supercapacitors are sized in hundreds of Farads** — not microfarads like filter capacitors — and is the most frequently-asked derivation in KTU university exams on this module.

## 3.2 Derivation #2 — Battery State-of-Charge (SoC) as a Function of Time

**Engineering Problem Statement:** *A 30 Ah Li-ion battery in a HESS is delivering a constant current of $I = 50$ A to support the DC bus. If the initial SoC is 80 %, derive the SoC as a function of time and find when the SoC reaches 40 %.*

### Step 1 — Definition of SoC (Coulomb-counting form)

$$
\mathrm{SoC}(t) = \mathrm{SoC}(t_0) - \frac{1}{Q_{\max}} \int_{t_0}^{t} I_{\text{Bat}}(\tau)\,d\tau
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute constant current

Because $I$ is constant, the integral simplifies to $I(t - t_0)$:

$$
\mathrm{SoC}(t) = \mathrm{SoC}(t_0) - \frac{I\,(t - t_0)}{Q_{\max}}
$$

### Step 3 — Plug in numerical values

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{SoC}(t) &= 0.80 - \frac{50\,(t - 0)}{30} \\
&= 0.80 - \frac{50\,t}{30} \\
&= 0.80 - 1.667\,t \quad (t \text{ in hours})
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Solve for the time when SoC = 0.40

$$
\begin{aligned}
0.40 &= 0.80 - 1.667\,t \\
1.667\,t &= 0.40 \\
t &= \frac{0.40}{1.667} = 0.24 \;\text{h} = 14.4 \;\text{minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This 14.4-minute buffer is the window during which the EMS **must reduce battery discharge current** and demand more power from the fuel cell, otherwise the battery would be over-discharged — a hard constraint in the EMS design.

## 3.3 Derivation #3 — Fuel-Cell Hydrogen Consumption over a Drive Cycle

**Engineering Problem Statement:** *An FCEV travels along a constant-speed segment where the fuel cell is to deliver a constant $P_{\text{FC}} = 30$ kW. The fuel-cell system operates at $\eta_{\text{FC}} = 50\%$, and hydrogen LHV is $LHV_{H_2} = 120$ MJ/kg. Find the hydrogen consumed over 1 hour.*

### Step 1 — Hydrogen mass-rate equation

The hydrogen mass flow required to produce electrical power $P_{\text{FC}}$ at system efficiency $\eta_{\text{FC}}$ is:

$$
\dot{m}_{H_2} = \frac{P_{\text{FC}}}{\eta_{\text{FC}} \cdot LHV_{H_2}}
$$

### Step 2 — Convert units consistently

$$
P_{\text{FC}} = 30\,\text{kW} = 30{,}000\,\text{W} = 30{,}000\,\text{J/s}
$$

$$
\eta_{\text{FC}} \cdot LHV_{H_2} = 0.50 \times 120 \times 10^{6}\,\text{J/kg} = 60 \times 10^{6}\,\text{J/kg}
$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate the mass-flow rate

$$
\dot{m}_{H_2} = \frac{30{,}000}{60 \times 10^{6}} = 5.0 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{kg/s}
$$

### Step 4 — Multiply by time ($t = 1\,\text{h} = 3600\,\text{s}$)

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{H_2} &= \dot{m}_{H_2} \times t \\
&= 5.0 \times 10^{-4} \times 3600 \\
&= 1.8\,\text{kg of H}_2
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Sanity check using direct energy method

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Electrical energy delivered} &= 30\,\text{kW} \times 1\,\text{h} = 30\,\text{kWh} = 30 \times 3.6\,\text{MJ} = 108\,\text{MJ} \\
\text{Chemical energy in H}_2 &= \frac{108\,\text{MJ}}{0.50} = 216\,\text{MJ} \\
\text{Mass} &= \frac{216\,\text{MJ}}{120\,\text{MJ/kg}} = 1.8\,\text{kg} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result Interpretation:** 1.8 kg of $H_2$ per hour at 30 kW constant load. This number is the **design baseline** used to size hydrogen tanks (e.g., 700 bar type IV tank → $\sim 5$ kg $H_2$ → $\sim 2.7$ hours of constant cruise).

## 3.4 Derivation #4 — Energy Management Minimization (ECMS Form)

For the optimization-based EMS, the **Equivalent Consumption Minimization Strategy (ECMS)** minimizes, at every instant, a cost function $J$ that combines the actual hydrogen use of the fuel cell with a "virtual" hydrogen equivalent of the battery's electrical exchange:

$$
J(t) = \dot{m}_{H_2,\text{FC}}(t) + s \cdot \dot{m}_{H_2,\text{equiv,Bat}}(t)
$$

where the equivalent hydrogen mass flow of the battery is:

$$
\dot{m}_{H_2,\text{equiv,Bat}}(t) = \frac{s_{\text{dis}}\,P_{\text{Bat,dis}}(t) - s_{\text{ch}}\,P_{\text{Bat,ch}}(t)}{\eta_{\text{FC,avg}}\,LHV_{H_2}}
$$

The equivalent factor $s$ penalises battery use so as to keep the **SoC** near a reference value; the EMS solves $\min_{P_{\text{FC}}} J(t)$ subject to:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{FC,min}} &\leq P_{\text{FC}}(t) \leq P_{\text{FC,max}} \\
\mathrm{SoC}_{\min} &\leq \mathrm{SoC}(t) \leq \mathrm{SoC}_{\max} \\
P_{\text{Bat,min}} &\leq P_{\text{Bat}}(t) \leq P_{\text{Bat,max}} \\
P_{\text{FC}}(t) + P_{\text{Bat}}(t) + P_{\text{SC}}(t) &= P_{\text{load}}(t)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiners' Tip:** When a question asks "explain ECMS", always (i) write the cost function, (ii) list the constraints, (iii) state that the **SOC-equivalence factor $s$** is adapted online to maintain SoC around its reference.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Sizing Decision Tree

The following matrix captures the **decision logic** the EMS uses to size and dispatch power in real time:

| Operating Regime | Dominant Source | Action of EMS | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| Idle / Low speed | FC (idle) | Reduce $P_{\text{FC}}$ to idle; SC charges from regen | Avoid FC low-efficiency tip |
| Steady cruise | FC | $P_{\text{FC}} = P_{\text{load}}$; SC & Bat idle | FC at its best efficiency point |
| Hard acceleration | FC + Bat + SC | $P_{\text{FC}}$ ramps slowly; Bat and SC deliver the transient spike | Protect FC from starvation |
| Hard braking | SC + Bat (charge) | SC absorbs first (high C-rate); Bat absorbs remainder | Maximize regen energy recovery |
| Long descent | SC + Bat (charge) | Limit FC to idle; both buffers charge | Energy recovery, hydrogen saving |
| SoC too low | FC dominates | Increase $P_{\text{FC}}$ to refill battery | Maintain SoC within safe window |
| SoC too high | FC reduces | Cut $P_{\text{FC}}$; allow Bat to discharge | Make room for next regen event |
