# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The questions below are modelled precisely on the **KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Examination pattern**: 3-mark direct questions in Part A, 14-mark questions with internal choice (a) and (b) carrying 7 marks each in Part B. Each 14-mark question is provided in **two alternative full versions** so the student can practise both.

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**
> **Define the term "Hybridization of Energy Storage Devices" in the context of a fuel cell vehicle. Why is a standalone PEM fuel cell not sufficient to drive a vehicle directly?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
*Hybridization of energy storage devices* is the engineering practice of combining two or more heterogeneous energy sources — typically a PEM fuel cell as the primary energy source, a Li-ion battery as a mid-term buffer, and a supercapacitor as a high-power buffer — within a single powertrain, interconnected via DC-DC converters and governed by an Energy Management Strategy (EMS). A standalone PEM fuel cell is not sufficient because (i) its dynamic response time is too slow (1–5 s) to follow sudden acceleration and braking demands, (ii) it cannot absorb regenerative braking energy efficiently, (iii) it suffers oxygen-starvation damage during transients, and (iv) it is inefficient at part-load and idling. HESS overcomes each of these deficiencies by delegating fast and peak power to the supercapacitor and battery, while the fuel cell operates near its **maximum-efficiency point**.

### Question 2
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
> **List any three advantages of a "fully-active" HESS configuration over a "passive" HESS configuration.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The three advantages are:
1. **Independent and decoupled control** of the fuel cell, battery, and supercapacitor power flows — enabling the fuel cell to be held at its high-efficiency operating point regardless of the load.
2. **Full regenerative braking energy recovery** — the supercapacitor and battery can charge independently without reverse current into the fuel cell.
3. **Bus voltage stability** — the DC bus voltage is regulated by the converters, decoupling it from the state-of-charge of the battery or supercapacitor.

*(A fourth point often accepted: extended fuel-cell lifetime due to protection from transient starvation — +1 mark if the answer is detailed.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question Choice A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 ESE Model]**
> **(a)** With the help of neat block diagrams, explain the three configurations of a Hybrid Energy Storage System (HESS) used in fuel cell vehicles. Compare their merits and demerits. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Derive the expression for the capacitance of a supercapacitor required to meet a peak transient power $P_{\text{peak}}$ for a duration $\Delta t$, when the capacitor voltage swings between $V_{\max}$ and $V_{\min}$. A 48 V supercapacitor bank is required to deliver a peak power of 80 kW for 5 seconds, with $V_{\min} = 24$ V. Calculate the minimum capacitance. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 (2 marks) — Define the three configurations:**
The three HESS configurations are **Passive (Direct-Parallel)**, **Semi-Active (Semi-Passive)**, and **Fully-Active (Dual-Converter)**. They are classified by the number and placement of DC-DC converters.

**Step 2 (3 marks) — Diagrams and description:**

- **Passive:** All three devices — fuel cell, battery, supercapacitor — are connected directly in parallel to the DC bus. The DC bus voltage equals the common terminal voltage of all three.
- **Semi-Active:** Only **one** device (commonly the supercapacitor or the battery) is interfaced to the bus through a DC-DC converter; the other two are directly connected.
- **Fully-Active:** **All three** sources are interfaced to the DC bus through their own dedicated DC-DC converters (FC uses a unidirectional boost; battery and SC use bidirectional converters).

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Comparison table:**

| Property | Passive | Semi-Active | Fully-Active |
|---|---|---|---|
| No. of converters | 0 | 1 | 2 or 3 |
| Cost | Lowest | Medium | Highest |
| Control flexibility | None | Partial | Complete |
| Regen capture | Poor | Moderate | Excellent |
| FC protection | Poor | Moderate | Best |
| Industry use | Rare | Common | **Standard (Mirai, NEXO)** |

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 (2 marks) — Energy of the transient:**

$$
E_{\text{transient}} = P_{\text{peak}} \cdot \Delta t
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Usable energy of a supercapacitor:**

$$
E_{\text{SC,usable}} = \frac{1}{2}\,C\,\bigl(V_{\max}^{2} - V_{\min}^{2}\bigr)
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark) — Equate the two to obtain sizing relation:**

$$
C \;\geq\; \frac{2\,P_{\text{peak}}\,\Delta t}{V_{\max}^{2} - V_{\min}^{2}}
$$

**Step 4 (2 marks) — Numerical substitution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &\geq \frac{2 \times 80{,}000 \times 5}{48^{2} - 24^{2}} \\
&= \frac{800{,}000}{2304 - 576} \\
&= \frac{800{,}000}{1728} \\
&\approx 463.0 \;\text{F}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,C_{\min} \;\approx\; 463 \;\text{F}\,}
$$

---

### ⭐ Question Choice B (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 ESE Model]**
> **(a)** Explain, with a labelled block diagram, the role of an Energy Management Strategy (EMS) in a fuel cell hybrid vehicle. Describe the Equivalent Consumption Minimization Strategy (ECMS) and the Frequency-Decoupling (Wavelet) approach. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A fuel cell vehicle uses a HESS comprising a 30 kW PEM fuel cell and a Li-ion battery of 25 Ah capacity. The drive cycle demands a constant $P_{\text{load}} = 40$ kW for 45 minutes. Assuming the FC delivers its rated power and the battery covers the deficit, determine (i) the energy delivered by each source, (ii) the depth-of-discharge (DoD) of the battery, and (iii) the hydrogen consumed if $\eta_{\text{FC}} = 50\%$ and $LHV_{H_2} = 120$ MJ/kg. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Role of EMS:** The EMS is the supervisory controller of the HESS. It receives the driver's torque demand and decides, in real time, how much power to draw from the fuel cell, the battery, and the supercapacitor, subject to constraints such as SoC limits, FC ramp rate, and converter current limits.

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Block diagram (described in words):** *Driver pedal → Vehicle Control Unit (VCU) → EMS → DC-DC converter command signals to FC, Bat, SC → Power flows to DC bus → Inverter → Motor → Wheels. Feedback signals: SoC, FC current, SC voltage, vehicle speed.*

**Step 3 (2 marks) — ECMS description:** ECMS minimizes, at every sampling instant, the cost function

$$
J(t) = \dot{m}_{H_2,\text{FC}}(t) + s \cdot \dot{m}_{H_2,\text{equiv,Bat}}(t)
$$

where $s$ is the **SoC-equivalence factor** (adapted online to keep SoC near its reference), and $\dot{m}_{H_2,\text{equiv,Bat}}$ converts battery power into an equivalent hydrogen mass flow using the average fuel-cell efficiency. The result is a near-optimal real-time power split.

**Step 4 (2 marks) — Frequency-Decoupling description:** The load power $P_{\text{load}}(t)$ is decomposed (using FFT or wavelet transform) into a **low-frequency** component — which is allocated to the fuel cell (slow but efficient) — and a **high-frequency** component — which is allocated to the supercapacitor (fast and high-power). The battery handles the mid-frequency drift and SoC trim.

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Total energy demand:**

$$
E_{\text{total}} = P_{\text{load}} \times t = 40\,\text{kW} \times 0.75\,\text{h} = 30\,\text{kWh} = 108\,\text{MJ}
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Energy from fuel cell (rated 30 kW for 0.75 h):**

$$
E_{\text{FC}} = 30 \times 0.75 = 22.5\,\text{kWh} = 81\,\text{MJ}
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark) — Energy from battery (deficit):**

$$
E_{\text{Bat}} = 30 - 22.5 = 7.5\,\text{kWh} = 27\,\text{MJ}
$$

**Step 4 (2 marks) — Battery DoD:**

The battery's nominal energy is $E_{\text{Bat,nom}} = V_{\text{nom}} \times Q_{\max}$. With $V_{\text{nom}} = 3.7\,\text{V/cell}$ (assumed for a 25 Ah cell, single-cell basis for DoD %):

$$
\mathrm{DoD} = \frac{E_{\text{Bat}}}{E_{\text{Bat,nom}}} = \frac{7.5\,\text{kWh}}{3.7 \times 25\,\text{Wh}} = \frac{7500}{92.5} \approx 81.1\,\%
$$

(If the full pack voltage is given differently, the student must use it. The DoD is energy delivered / nominal energy.)

**Step 5 (2 marks) — Hydrogen consumed:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{H_2} &= \frac{E_{\text{FC,chem}}}{\eta_{\text{FC}}\,LHV_{H_2}} \\
&= \frac{81\,\text{MJ}}{0.50 \times 120\,\text{MJ/kg}} \\
&= \frac{81}{60} = 1.35\;\text{kg of H}_2
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,E_{\text{FC}} = 22.5\,\text{kWh}, \quad E_{\text{Bat}} = 7.5\,\text{kWh}, \quad \mathrm{DoD} \approx 81\,\%, \quad m_{H_2} = 1.35\,\text{kg}\,}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on this Topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting to include the $1/2$ factor** in the supercapacitor energy equation $E = \tfrac{1}{2}CV^{2}$ → loses 1 mark.
> 2. **Sign convention confusion** in the power-balance equation — always state that *charging* power is negative and *discharging* is positive.
> 3. **Mixing LHV and HHV** of hydrogen — KTU 2024 syllabus uses **LHV = 120 MJ/kg** by default.
> 4. **Using battery current instead of power** in the SoC equation — both are acceptable, but the **unit consistency** is what examiners check.
> 5. **Describing ECMS without mentioning the SoC-equivalence factor $s$** — a 2-mark deduction is typical.
> 6. **Confusing semi-active and fully-active diagrams** — verify the *number of converters* explicitly in the diagram.
> 7. **Writing "frequency decoupling" without naming FFT or wavelet** — examiners expect at least one specific method.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 15-Point "Must-Know" Checklist for Hybridization of Energy Storage Devices:**
>
> 1. **Definition**: HESS = FC + Battery + (optionally) SC, linked by DC-DC converters and an EMS.
> 2. **Three configurations** are Passive, Semi-Active, and Fully-Active — fully-active is industry standard.
> 3. **Why hybridize**: PEM fuel cell alone has slow dynamics, no regen capture, low part-load efficiency, and cold-start issues.
> 4. **The complementary trio**: FC → high energy density, Battery → mid energy + power, SC → high power + long cycle life.
> 5. **Power balance equation** must always be stated: $P_{\text{load}} = P_{\text{FC}} + P_{\text{Bat}} + P_{\text{SC}}$.
> 6. **Supercapacitor energy**: $E = \tfrac{1}{2}CV^{2}$ — memorize this in both forms (energy stored and usable energy).
> 7. **Sizing relation**: $C \geq \dfrac{2 P_{\text{peak}} \Delta t}{V_{\max}^2 - V_{\min}^2}$.
> 8. **SoC equation** (Coulomb counting): $\mathrm{SoC}(t) = \mathrm{SoC}(t_0) - \dfrac{1}{Q_{\max}}\int I\,dt$.
> 9. **Hydrogen consumption** uses **LHV = 120 MJ/kg** unless the question states HHV.
> 10. **EMS families**: rule-based (deterministic, fuzzy) and optimization-based (ECMS, DP, PMP, frequency-decoupling).
> 11. **ECMS** uses an SoC-equivalence factor $s$ to convert battery power into equivalent hydrogen cost.
> 12. **Frequency-decoupling** uses FFT or wavelet to split load into low- (→ FC/Bat) and high-frequency (→ SC) parts.
> 13. **Ragone plot** maps energy density vs power density — the visual justification for hybridization.
> 14. **Regenerative braking** is captured by the SC first (high C-rate), then the Bat — never sent back to the FC.
> 15. **Commercial examples**: Toyota Mirai, Hyundai NEXO, Honda Clarity, Mercedes GLC F-CELL — all use semi-active or fully-active HESS.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Short-term storage systems: flywheel accumulators, ultra-capacitors
Energy management strategies and implementation challenges
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT832 - HYDROGEN AND FUEL CELL VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Energy management strategies and implementation challenges

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Energy Management Strategy (EMS)** in a Hydrogen and Fuel Cell Vehicle (HFCV / FCHV) is defined as the supervisory control algorithm that optimally allocates the instantaneous power demand of the vehicle between two or more on-board energy sources — primarily the **Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cell (PEMFC)** stack, the **Battery Pack** (typically Li-ion), and the **Ultra-Capacitor (UC)** bank — while satisfying the drive-cycle torque/speed request, State-of-Charge (SoC) constraints, hydrogen consumption minimization, and fuel cell lifetime extension targets.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, EMS is treated as a **multi-input, multi-output (MIMO) constrained optimization problem** solved in real time (typically at 10–100 ms sampling intervals) by the **Vehicle Control Unit (VCU) / Hybrid Control Unit (HCU)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Module 3 specifically demands the student to (a) classify EMS into rule-based and optimization-based, (b) explain the thermodynamic and electrochemical rationale for hybridization, and (c) identify real-world implementation bottlenecks such as durability, cold-start, and refuelling infrastructure.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Two-Jug Water Pouring" Intuition

Imagine you are thirsty and have **two jugs** of water:

- **Jug A (Fuel Cell):** A large tank that pours water slowly and steadily, but once you pour, you cannot put water back. It also dislikes being filled and emptied in quick bursts.
- **Jug B (Battery):** A smaller, fast-filling, fast-emptying jug that can be topped up (via regenerative braking) and emptied quickly.
- **The Tap (Wheels):** Demands water at a variable, unpredictable rate (drive cycle).

The **Energy Management Strategy is the "pouring policy"** of the human standing between the jugs and the tap.

- If you only used **Jug A**, the tap would run dry during sudden bursts (acceleration) and you might waste water when the tap is closed (idle/cruise).
- If you only used **Jug B**, you would have to constantly refill (recharge) it externally.
- **The intelligent strategy** uses Jug A as the steady "trickle" source and Jug B as the "burst" buffer, recharging Jug B from spills (regenerative braking) and from Jug A when the tap demand is low.

This pouring policy, when formalized mathematically, becomes the **EMS**.

---

### 1.3 Why Hybridization is Fundamentally Necessary

A standalone fuel cell cannot meet transient drive-cycle demands efficiently because:

| Vehicle Demand | Fuel Cell Response | Mismatch |
|---|---|---|
| Sudden acceleration | Slow electrochemical + compressor inertia (~2–5 s) | **Power deficit** |
| Sudden braking / idle | Cannot absorb regen energy | **Energy waste** |
| Cold start (−20 °C) | Membrane ice formation, sluggish kinetics | **No power available** |
| Steady cruise | Near-optimal efficiency point | **Good match** |

A battery/UC fills these gaps. The EMS decides *when*, *how much*, and *from where*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard KTU Metric:** A well-tuned EMS can improve the **hydrogen fuel economy** by **20–35 %** and extend **fuel cell lifetime by 2–3×** compared to a fuel-cell-only vehicle.

---

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

The following constants and metrics must be memorized for KTU 2024 evaluations:

- **Faraday's Constant:** $F = 96\,485$ **C/mol**
- **Universal Gas Constant:** $R = 8.314$ **J/(mol·K)**
- **Lower Heating Value of Hydrogen (LHV):** $\Delta H_{LHV} = 240$ **kJ/mol**
- **Higher Heating Value of Hydrogen (HHV):** $\Delta H_{HHV} = 286$ **kJ/mol**
- **Standard Operating PEMFC Temperature:** **60–80 °C**
- **Typical FC System Efficiency:** **50–60 %** (LHV basis)
- **Vehicle-Level Battery SoC Operating Window:** **40 %–80 %**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Energy Management Strategies

The KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus mandates a clear binary classification:

#### A. Rule-Based Strategies (Heuristic / Causal)
These strategies use a predefined set of **IF–THEN rules** derived from engineering intuition, not from optimization mathematics. They are simple, real-time implementable, and require minimal computation.

**1. Thermostat (On–Off) Strategy**
- Fuel cell is **ON** when battery SoC < $SoC_{low}$ (e.g., 40 %).
- Fuel cell is **OFF** when battery SoC > $SoC_{high}$ (e.g., 80 %).
- **Disadvantage:** Frequent cycling damages the fuel cell stack (start-stop stress).

**2. Load Following (Power Tracking) Strategy**
- Fuel cell supplies *exactly* the demanded power minus the battery contribution.
- The fuel cell operating point is forced to follow the load → poor efficiency at low loads.

**3. State Machine Strategy**
- Multiple discrete operating modes (e.g., FC-only, Battery-only, Hybrid, Regen).
- Mode transitions governed by SoC thresholds, power demand thresholds, and FC temperature.
- **Most commonly used in production FCHVs** (e.g., Toyota Mirai, Hyundai Nexo).

**4. Frequency-Decoupling / Filtering Strategy**
- Low-frequency components of power demand → Fuel Cell.
- High-frequency components → Battery/UC.
- Implemented using **Low-Pass Filter (LPF)** or **Wavelet Transform**.

#### B. Optimization-Based Strategies
These strategies find the mathematically *optimal* power split by solving (or approximating) a cost function.

**1. Equivalent Consumption Minimization Strategy (ECMS)**
- Treats electrical energy in the battery as "virtual hydrogen."
- Real-time minimization of an instantaneous cost function:

$$J_{ECMS} = \dot{m}_{H_2}(P_{fc}) + s(t) \cdot \frac{P_{bat}}{\eta_{dis}} \cdot \text{LHV}_{H_2}^{-1}$$

- The **equivalence factor** $s(t)$ is the tuning knob.

**2. Global Optimization (Offline Benchmarks)**
- **Dynamic Programming (DP):** Bellman's principle of optimality. Computationally expensive; used as a benchmark only.
- **Pontryagin's Minimum Principle (PMP):** Converts the global problem into a local two-point boundary value problem; provides the co-state $\lambda(t)$.

**3. Intelligent / Learning-Based Strategies**
- **Fuzzy Logic Control:** Rule-base with linguistic variables.
- **Neural Networks:** Trained on optimal DP/PMP trajectories.
- **Reinforcement Learning (RL):** Agent learns the policy through interaction with a drive-cycle simulator.

---

### 2.2 The Core Cost Function — Hydrogen Consumption Minimization

The mathematical heart of every optimization-based EMS is the minimization of total hydrogen mass consumed over drive cycle $t \in [t_0, t_f]$:

$$J^{*} = \min_{P_{fc}(t)} \int_{t_0}^{t_f} \dot{m}_{H_2}(P_{fc}(t),\, T_{fc}(t)) \, dt$$

Subject to:
- $P_{fc,min} \leq P_{fc}(t) \leq P_{fc,max}$
- $SoC_{min} \leq SoC(t) \leq SoC_{max}$
- $P_{fc}(t) + P_{bat}(t) = P_{req}(t)$
- $\dot{m}_{H_2} \geq 0$ (one-way flow, no fuel-cell regen)
- $\eta_{bat} \cdot P_{bat,dis} + P_{bat,chg} / \eta_{bat} = P_{bat}$ (battery power sign convention)

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **high-yield formula sheet** for KTU 2024 ESE and internal assessments on this topic. Master it.

| # | Formula / Relation | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dot{m}_{H_2} = \dfrac{P_{fc}}{\eta_{fc} \cdot \text{LHV}_{H_2}}$ | Hydrogen mass flow rate | **kg/s** |
| 2 | $P_{fc} = V_{cell} \cdot I \cdot N_{cells}$ | Fuel cell stack power | **W** |
| 3 | $\text{SoC}(t+\Delta t) = \text{SoC}(t) - \dfrac{V_{oc} - \sqrt{V_{oc}^2 - 4 R_{int} P_{bat}}}{2 R_{int} Q_{bat}} \Delta t$ | Battery SoC dynamics | dimensionless |
| 4 | $H(t) = P_{bat,dis}(t) \cdot \eta_{bat} - P_{bat,chg}(t)/\eta_{bat}$ | Battery power convention (positive = discharge) | **W** |
| 5 | $\tau_{fc} \cdot \dot{P}_{fc} + P_{fc} = P_{fc,ref}$ | 1st-order FC dynamics | **W**, **s** |
| 6 | $J_{ECMS} = \dot{m}_{H_2} + s \cdot \dfrac{P_{bat}}{\eta_{dis} \cdot \text{LHV}}$ | ECMS instantaneous cost | **kg/s** (equiv.) |
| 7 | $\eta_{sys} = \dfrac{\int P_{wheels} \, dt}{\int \dot{m}_{H_2} \cdot \text{LHV} \, dt}$ | Vehicle system efficiency | dimensionless |
| 8 | $E_{cap} = \dfrac{1}{2} C V^2$ | Ultra-capacitor stored energy | **J** |
| 9 | $P_{req}(t) = \dfrac{v(t)}{\eta_{drivetrain}} \left( m g (f \cos\theta + \sin\theta) + \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2 + m \delta \dfrac{dv}{dt} \right)$ | Longitudinal dynamics power demand | **W** |
| 10 | $\text{CD}_{FC} = \sum_{k} w_k \cdot \Delta P_{fc,k}$ | Cumulative fuel cell damage index (for lifetime extension) | dimensionless |

> **Notation Quick-Glossary:**
> $V_{oc}$ — open-circuit voltage, $R_{int}$ — internal resistance, $Q_{bat}$ — battery capacity, $\tau_{fc}$ — fuel cell time constant, $w_k$ — damage weight, $\delta$ — mass factor, $\rho$ — air density, $C_d$ — drag coefficient, $f$ — rolling resistance.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The EMS is the **brain** that determines the commercial viability of a fuel cell vehicle. Real-world applications include:

- **Passenger FCHVs** (Toyota Mirai 2nd gen, Hyundai Nexo, Honda Clarity) — State-machine with frequency decoupling.
- **Heavy-Duty Trucks** (Hyundai XCIENT, Daimler GenH2) — ECMS for long-haul hydrogen economy.
- **Fuel Cell Buses** (Ballard / Wrightbus) — Rule-based for predictable city duty cycles.
- **Material Handling & Forklifts** (Plug Power) — Simple on-off thermostat.
- **Aerospace & Drones** — RL-based adaptive EMS for weight-critical applications.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Hydrogen Mass Flow Rate from Fuel Cell Power

**Given:** A PEMFC stack delivers electrical power $P_{fc}$ at system efficiency $\eta_{fc}$ (LHV basis). Derive the hydrogen mass consumption rate.

**Step 1 — Energy balance on the fuel cell system.**
The electrical energy output equals a fraction $\eta_{fc}$ of the chemical energy input from hydrogen:

$$P_{fc} = \eta_{fc} \cdot \dot{n}_{H_2} \cdot \Delta H_{LHV}$$

**Step 2 — Convert molar flow to mass flow.**
Using the molar mass of hydrogen $M_{H_2} = 2.016 \times 10^{-3}$ **kg/mol**:

$$\dot{m}_{H_2} = \dot{n}_{H_2} \cdot M_{H_2}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and simplify.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{fc} &= \eta_{fc} \cdot \dfrac{\dot{m}_{H_2}}{M_{H_2}} \cdot \Delta H_{LHV} \\[6pt]
\dot{m}_{H_2} &= \dfrac{P_{fc} \cdot M_{H_2}}{\eta_{fc} \cdot \Delta H_{LHV}} \\[6pt]
\dot{m}_{H_2} &= \dfrac{P_{fc}}{\eta_{fc} \cdot \text{LHV}_{H_2}} \quad \text{where } \text{LHV}_{H_2} \equiv \dfrac{\Delta H_{LHV}}{M_{H_2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical sanity check.**
With $\text{LHV}_{H_2} = 119.96$ **MJ/kg** (using $\Delta H_{LHV} = 240$ kJ/mol and $M_{H_2} \approx 2$ g/mol), a **50 kW** fuel cell at **$\eta_{fc} = 0.55$** consumes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{H_2} &= \dfrac{50\,000}{0.55 \times 119.96 \times 10^{6}} \\[6pt]
&= 7.58 \times 10^{-7} \text{ kg/s} \\[6pt]
&\approx 2.73 \text{ g/hour}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is consistent with real-world Mirai 2 specifications.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: ECMS Cost Function from Pontryagin's Minimum Principle

**Given:** A fuel-cell / battery hybrid powertrain with state $x = \text{SoC}$ and control $u = P_{fc}$.

**Step 1 — Formulate the global optimal problem.**
Minimize total hydrogen mass over the drive cycle:

$$J^{*} = \min_{u} \int_{0}^{T} \dot{m}_{H_2}(u(t))\, dt$$

Subject to dynamics $\dot{x} = f(x, u)$ and bounds.

**Step 2 — Apply PMP.**
Define the Hamiltonian:

$$\mathcal{H}(x, u, \lambda, t) = \dot{m}_{H_2}(u) + \lambda(t) \cdot f(x, u)$$

The optimal control satisfies:

$$u^{*}(t) = \arg\min_{u} \mathcal{H}(x^{*}(t), u, \lambda^{*}(t), t)$$

**Step 3 — Expand the dynamics term.**
Battery SoC dynamics (Coulomb counting):

$$\dot{x} = \dot{\text{SoC}} = -\dfrac{I_{bat}}{Q_{bat}} = -\dfrac{P_{bat}}{V_{bat} \cdot Q_{bat}}$$

Using power balance $P_{bat} = P_{req} - P_{fc} = P_{req} - u$:

$$\dot{x} = -\dfrac{P_{req}(t) - u}{V_{bat} \cdot Q_{bat}}$$

**Step 4 — Combine into Hamiltonian.**

$$\mathcal{H} = \dfrac{u}{\eta_{fc}(u) \cdot \text{LHV}_{H_2}} - \lambda \cdot \dfrac{P_{req}(t) - u}{V_{bat} \cdot Q_{bat}}$$

**Step 5 — Collect terms in $u$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{H} &= u \left[ \dfrac{1}{\eta_{fc}(u) \cdot \text{LHV}_{H_2}} + \dfrac{\lambda}{V_{bat} \cdot Q_{bat}} \right] - \dfrac{\lambda \cdot P_{req}(t)}{V_{bat} \cdot Q_{bat}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Define the equivalence factor.**

$$s \equiv \dfrac{\lambda \cdot \text{LHV}_{H_2}}{V_{bat} \cdot Q_{bat}}$$

For a constant $s$, the minimization reduces to the local ECMS:

$$u^{*}(t) = \arg\min_{u} \left[ \dot{m}_{H_2}(u) + s \cdot \dfrac{P_{req}(t) - u}{\eta_{dis} \cdot \text{LHV}_{H_2}} \right]$$

This is the **ECMS cost function**, derived rigorously from PMP.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Strategy Comparison (KTU-Mandated Table)

| Property | Thermostat | Load Following | State Machine | ECMS | DP / PMP |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Computation | **Trivial** | Low | Low | Moderate | **High** (offline) |
| Hydrogen Economy | Poor | Moderate | Good | **Very Good** | Best (theoretical) |
| FC Lifetime | **Poor** (cycling) | Moderate | Good | **Good** | Good |
| Real-Time | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ (offline) |
| Adaptivity | None | Low | Medium | **High** | High |
| Implementation Cost | $ | $ | $$ | $$$ | N/A |
| Industrial Use | Forklifts | **Mirai (1st gen)** | **Mirai 2 / Nexo** | Research | Benchmark only |

---

### 3.4 Derivation: 1st-Order Fuel Cell Lag Dynamics

**Rationale:** Fuel cells cannot change power instantaneously due to:
1. Air compressor inertia (~1–3 s).
2. Membrane water management dynamics.
3. Thermal mass of the stack.

**Step 1 — Assume first-order lag.**

$$\tau_{fc} \dfrac{dP_{fc}}{dt} + P_{fc}(t) = P_{fc,ref}(t)$$

**Step 2 — Solve with zero initial condition via Laplace transform.**

$$P_{fc}(s) = \dfrac{1}{1 + \tau_{fc} s} \cdot P_{fc,ref}(s)$$

**Step 3 — Time-domain step response.**

$$P_{fc}(t) = P_{fc,ref} \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau_{fc}}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Engineering implication.**
If $P_{fc,ref}$ jumps from 10 kW → 60 kW, and $\tau_{fc} = 2$ s, then at $t = 2$ s the stack delivers only $60(1 - e^{-1}) \approx 37.9$ kW. The deficit (~22 kW) **must be supplied by the battery** — this is precisely why EMS is indispensable.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an FCHV EMS

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Driver Input Accelerator Pedal and Brake Pedal] --> B[Drive Cycle Decoder]
    B --> C[Reference Power Calculator P_req of t]
    C --> D[Hybrid Control Unit HCU]
    D --> E[EMS Algorithm State Machine plus ECMS]
    E --> F[Fuel Cell Power Command]
    E --> G[Battery Power Command]
    F --> H[PEMFC Stack]
    G --> I[Li ion Battery Pack plus DC DC]
    H --> J[DC Bus Coupler]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Inverter]
    K --> L[Traction Motor]
    L --> M[Vehicle Wheels]
    N[Battery SoC Sensor] --> E
    O[FC Temperature Sensor] --> E
    P[Vehicle Speed Sensor] --> E
    Q[Hydrogen Tank Level] --> E
    E --> R[Thermal Management Controller]
    H --> R
    R --> S[Radiator and Coolant Pump]
```

**Interpretation of the block diagram:**

1. **Driver Pedal → Drive Cycle Decoder:** Translates pedal position into a normalized torque request.
2. **Reference Power Calculator:** Converts the torque request into $P_{req}(t)$ using longitudinal dynamics (rolling, aero, inertia, grade).
3. **HCU + EMS:** The core supervisory controller. It receives $P_{req}$, SoC, FC temperature, vehicle speed, and H₂ level; it then issues two set-points: $P_{fc}^{*}$ and $P_{bat}^{*}$.
4. **DC Bus Coupler:** Common voltage bus (~400 V) where the FC and battery are paralleled.
5. **Sensor Feedback Loop:** SoC, FC temperature, vehicle speed, and H₂ tank level are fed back to the EMS for closed-loop adaptive control.
6. **Thermal Management:** A critical auxiliary subsystem that is co-optimized with the EMS in advanced implementations.

---

### 4.2 Decision Flow — State Machine Strategy (Mirai 2 Simplified)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Startup
    Startup --> FC_Warmup : Cold Start Detected
    FC_Warmup --> BatteryOnly : Stack Below 60 Degree Celsius
    BatteryOnly --> HybridDrive : SoC Above 50 Percent
    HybridDrive --> FC_Only : SoC Below 40 Percent and Power Below 30 kW
    FC_Only --> HybridDrive : Power Demand Above 30 kW
    HybridDrive --> RegenBraking : Brake Pedal Active
    RegenBraking --> HybridDrive : Brake Released and SoC Below 80 Percent
    HybridDrive --> Idle : Zero Power Demand
    Idle --> HybridDrive : Power Demand Above 5 kW
    FC_Only --> Shutdown : Ignition Off
    HybridDrive --> Shutdown : Ignition Off
    BatteryOnly --> Shutdown : Ignition Off
    Idle --> Shutdown : Ignition Off
    RegenBraking --> Shutdown : Ignition Off
    Shutdown --> [*]
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- **Startup → FC_Warmup:** Vehicle ignition initiates a thermal conditioning phase.
- **BatteryOnly:** Until the FC reaches operating temperature, all power comes from the battery.
- **HybridDrive:** The dominant steady-state mode — FC provides the average load, battery handles transients.
- **FC_Only:** Selected during low SoC conditions at low power demand (efficient FC operating region).
- **RegenBraking:** Battery absorbs kinetic energy; FC is idled.

---

### 4.3 Frequency-Decoupling Architecture (Power-Split by Frequency)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Vehicle Power Demand P_req of t] --> B[Low Pass Filter LPF]
    A --> C[Subtractor]
    B --> D[Low Frequency Component P_LF]
    D --> E[Fuel Cell]
    C --> F[High Frequency Component P_HF]
    F --> G[Battery plus Ultra Capacitor]
    E --> H[DC Bus]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Traction Motor]
```

**Interpretation:** The LPF (cutoff frequency $f_c$ typically 0.01–0.1 Hz) splits the demand. The fuel cell receives a smooth, slow-varying signal — protecting it from cycling stress. The battery handles the high-frequency "ripple."

---

### 4.4 ECMS Real-Time Implementation Block

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Inputs: P_req, SoC, v, T_fc] --> B[Compute Equivalence Factor s of t]
    B --> C[Compute J for P_fc candidates]
    C --> D{Choose P_fc minimizing J}
    D --> E[Output P_fc star]
    E --> F[P_bat star = P_req minus P_fc star]
    F --> G[Apply FC Lag Dynamics]
    G --> H[Update SoC]
    H --> I[Next Time Step dt 10 to 100 ms]
    I --> A
```

**Note:** In adaptive ECMS, the equivalence factor $s(t)$ is updated online to maintain SoC near a reference, e.g.:

$$s(t+\Delta t) = s(t) + K_p \cdot (\text{SoC}_{ref} - \text{SoC}(t)) + K_i \int (\text{SoC}_{ref} - \text{SoC})\, dt$$

This PI-type adaptation eliminates SoC drift — a classical pitfall of fixed-s ECMS.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 3]**
**Define Energy Management Strategy (EMS) in a hydrogen fuel cell hybrid electric vehicle. List any two rule-based EMS with one limitation each.**

**Model Answer:**

An **Energy Management Strategy (EMS)** is a supervisory control algorithm implemented in the Vehicle Control Unit (VCU) of a fuel cell hybrid vehicle that optimally distributes the instantaneous power demand $P_{req}(t)$ between the fuel cell stack and the energy storage system (battery / ultra-capacitor). Its objectives are: (i) minimization of hydrogen consumption, (ii) maintenance of battery SoC within safe limits, and (iii) extension of fuel cell lifetime.

**Two Rule-Based EMS:**

1. **Thermostat (On–Off) Strategy**
   - *Working:* FC turns ON when SoC < $SoC_{low}$, OFF when SoC > $SoC_{high}$.
   - *Limitation:* Frequent start–stop cycles cause severe membrane electrode assembly (MEA) degradation and reduce fuel cell lifetime by ~30–50 %.

2. **Load Following Strategy**
   - *Working:* FC output exactly equals the demanded power minus battery contribution.
   - *Limitation:* Forces the FC to operate at low-efficiency points during low power demands (e.g., urban cruise), resulting in 10–20 % higher hydrogen consumption compared to optimized strategies.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 3]**
**State any three implementation challenges of fuel cell hybrid vehicles in real-world deployment.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Hydrogen Refuelling Infrastructure:** The global network of H₂ refuelling stations is sparse (≈1,000 stations worldwide as of 2024), and on-site H₂ production via electrolysis is energy-intensive, limiting consumer adoption.

2. **Cold-Start Performance:** PEMFC stacks require operating temperatures of 60–80 °C; at sub-zero ambient temperatures, water in the membrane freezes, blocking proton conduction and preventing start-up without an external heating system.

3. **Fuel Cell Durability & Cost:** Automotive-grade PEMFCs must survive 5,000–8,000 hours of operation with <10 % voltage degradation. Current platinum-group-metal (PGM) loading (0.1–0.2 mg/cm²) and membrane degradation mechanisms (chemical, mechanical, thermal) drive the stack cost to ₹4–6 lakhs per 100 kW system — non-competitive with ICE.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Sample 2024, Module 3]*

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a Fuel Cell + Battery + Ultra-Capacitor hybrid powertrain. Discuss the role of each energy source. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the ECMS cost function starting from the global hydrogen minimization problem using Pontryagin's Minimum Principle. Define the equivalence factor and explain its role. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:**

A **triple-source hybrid powertrain** combines three complementary energy sources to address the limitations of any single source.

**Block Diagram Description:**

1. **Hydrogen Tank (350 or 700 bar):** Stores compressed H₂ gas as the primary chemical energy reservoir. Energy density ≈ 120 MJ/kg (LHV) — much higher than Li-ion (~0.9 MJ/kg).

2. **PEMFC Stack:** Provides the steady, average power. Best efficiency at 30–50 % of rated power. Cannot absorb regenerative energy; slow transient response ($\tau_{fc} \approx 1$–3 s).

3. **DC-DC Boost Converter (FC side):** Steps up the variable FC voltage (250–450 V depending on load) to the DC bus voltage (~400 V).

4. **Li-ion Battery Pack:** Mid-term energy buffer. Stores regenerative braking energy; provides power during transients. Energy ~1–5 kWh in a typical FCHV.

5. **Bidirectional DC-DC Converter (Battery side):** Matches the battery voltage (200–350 V) to the bus voltage.

6. **Ultra-Capacitor Bank:** High-power, low-energy buffer (50–500 Wh). Handles very short, high-power bursts (kW-level for seconds) — protects the battery from high C-rate stress.

7. **DC Bus:** Common ~400 V DC link where all three sources are paralleled.

8. **Traction Inverter + Motor:** Three-phase inverter drives a Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor (PMSM).

9. **HCU / EMS:** Central controller coordinating all three power flows in real time.

**Role of Each Source:**

| Source | Role | Time Constant | Power Level |
|---|---|---|---|
| Fuel Cell | Steady cruise, base load | 1–3 s | 30–100 kW |
| Battery | Mid-term transients, regen storage | 0.1–1 s | 20–50 kW |
| Ultra-capacitor | Peak transients, acceleration bursts | <0.1 s | 50–100 kW |

The **complementary time-constants** of the three sources enable the EMS to maintain the FC at its high-efficiency operating region while satisfying arbitrary drive-cycle demands.

---

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:**

(See Section 3.2 of these notes for the exhaustive derivation. The KTU-mandated steps are summarized below.)

**Step 1:** State the global optimization problem:

$$J^{*} = \min_{P_{fc}(t)} \int_{0}^{T} \dot{m}_{H_2}(P_{fc}(t))\, dt$$

**Step 2:** Define the state $x(t) = \text{SoC}(t)$ and dynamics:

$$\dot{x} = -\dfrac{P_{req}(t) - P_{fc}(t)}{V_{bat} \cdot Q_{bat}}$$

**Step 3:** Construct the Hamiltonian:

$$\mathcal{H} = \dot{m}_{H_2}(P_{fc}) + \lambda(t) \left[ -\dfrac{P_{req} - P_{fc}}{V_{bat} Q_{bat}} \right]$$

**Step 4:** Expand hydrogen flow as $\dot{m}_{H_2} = P_{fc} / (\eta_{fc} \cdot \text{LHV}_{H_2})$ and rearrange.

**Step 5:** Identify the equivalence factor:

$$s = \dfrac{\lambda \cdot \text{LHV}_{H_2}}{V_{bat} \cdot Q_{bat}}$$

**Step 6:** Write the ECMS local cost:

$$J_{ECMS}(t) = \dot{m}_{H_2}(P_{fc}) + s \cdot \dfrac{P_{req}(t) - P_{fc}(t)}{\eta_{dis} \cdot \text{LHV}_{H_2}}$$

**Step 7 — Role of $s$:**
The **equivalence factor** $s$ converts battery electrical energy into an equivalent hydrogen mass flow. A higher $s$ encourages FC usage (charges the battery); a lower $s$ encourages battery usage. In **adaptive ECMS**, a PI controller on SoC error updates $s$ online to maintain SoC near a reference — this is the **most critical practical implementation** of ECMS.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Sample 2024, Module 3]*

**(a)** Explain the State Machine-based EMS with a state transition diagram. Discuss why it is the most widely adopted strategy in production FCHVs. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** List and briefly explain any five major implementation challenges of FCHVs. For each, suggest one engineering solution. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:**

A **State Machine EMS** partitions the vehicle's operating envelope into a finite number of **discrete modes**. Transitions between modes are governed by **IF–THEN rules** based on real-time sensor inputs: battery SoC, FC temperature, demanded power, and vehicle speed.

**Typical States (Toyota Mirai 2 / Hyundai Nexo architecture):**

1. **Cold Start State:** Vehicle ignition; FC stack temperature < 60 °C. Battery is the sole power source; FC is being warmed.
2. **FC Warm-up State:** Auxiliary heater (or cathode exhaust heat) raises stack temperature to operating range.
3. **Battery-Only State:** FC temperature OK but demand is low (< 5 kW) — FC idles to save H₂ and prevent low-efficiency operation.
4. **Hybrid Drive State (Primary):** FC supplies the average load (selected by LPF or steady-state value); battery handles transients.
5. **FC-Only State:** SoC below 40 % and demand < 30 kW — FC charges battery while propelling the vehicle.
6. **Peak Power State:** Demand > 80 kW (e.g., hard acceleration) — both FC and battery deliver full power.
7. **Regenerative Braking State:** FC idles; battery/UC absorb kinetic energy.
8. **Standby / Shutdown State:** Vehicle off; FC purge cycle initiated.

**Why State Machine is Most Widely Adopted:**

- **Deterministic behavior** — easy to validate and certify for ISO 26262 functional safety.
- **Low computational footprint** — fits on standard automotive ECUs (e.g., Infineon AURIX).
- **Robust to sensor noise** — hysteresis on SoC thresholds prevents chattering.
- **Empirically tunable** — engineers can adjust thresholds using fleet data.
- **Compatible with safety logic** — fault states (e.g., low H₂ pressure) are simply additional states.

**Limitations:** Sub-optimal hydrogen economy (typically 5–10 % worse than ECMS) and limited adaptability to non-standard drive cycles.

---

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:**

| # | Implementation Challenge | Engineering Solution |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **On-board H₂ Storage** — 700 bar tanks store only ~5–6 kg H₂ → range 500–650 km; bulky and expensive (Type IV carbon-fiber cost ≈ ₹10–15 lakhs). | **Liquid H₂ or cryo-compressed H₂** at −253 °C; **metal-organic frameworks (MOFs)** for higher density adsorption; **liquid organic hydrogen carriers (LOHCs)** for chemical storage. |
| 2 | **PEMFC Cold Start** — Membrane water freezes below 0 °C; stack cannot self-start. | **Membrane dry-out operation with gas purge** (Toyota Mirai 2 starts at −30 °C); **self-humidifying membranes**; **integrated resistive heaters** in the stack. |
| 3 | **MEA Degradation** — Pt catalyst dissolution, membrane thinning, bipolar plate corrosion reduce lifetime to 5,000–8,000 hrs (target: 25,000 hrs for HDV). | **Pt-alloy catalysts (Pt-Co, Pt-Ni)** with reduced loading (~0.05 mg/cm²); **reinforced membranes (e.g., Gore Select®)**; **advanced operating-point management via EMS** to avoid high-voltage/low-power stress. |
| 4 | **Thermal Management** — Stack must reject 40–50 % of LHV as heat at 60–80 °C; coolant flow and radiator sizing are critical. | **High-temperature membranes (PBI / phosphoric-acid based)** to 160–200 °C; **integrated cooling loops with motor/inverter waste-heat recovery**. |
| 5 | **Hydrogen Refuelling Infrastructure** — Sparse network, slow refuelling (5–10 min), high production cost (₹400–600/kg grey H₂). | **On-site green H₂ production via PEM electrolysers powered by solar/wind**; **standardized 350/700 bar nozzles (SAE J2600)**; **fleet-based refuelling hubs for buses/trucks**. |
| 6 (Bonus) | **Cost of Pt-Group Metals** — 10–30 g Pt per 100 kW stack at ₹25,000–30,000/g. | **Pt recycling (closed-loop)**; **transition-metal catalysts (Fe-N-C, Mn-N-C)**; **alkaline / SOFC systems** for non-PEM applications. |

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in KTU 2024 Module 3 EMS Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing ECMS with Dynamic Programming:** Many students wrongly state that ECMS gives the *global* optimum. **Correction:** ECMS is a *real-time, sub-optimal approximation* of the global DP solution. ECMS is optimal only when $s(t)$ is correctly chosen (which PMP guarantees, but adaptive ECMS approximates).
>
> 2. **Sign convention on $P_{bat}$:** Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if the student mixes up charging/discharging signs. **Adopt the KTU convention:** $P_{bat} > 0$ = discharge; $P_{bat} < 0$ = charge.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the LHV vs HHV distinction:** When calculating $\dot{m}_{H_2}$, students often use the wrong heating value. **Rule:** For PEMFC systems (water exits as vapor) → use **LHV = 240 kJ/mol**. For SOFC/SOEC (water exits as liquid) → use **HHV = 286 kJ/mol**.
>
> 4. **Ignoring FC dynamics:** A common 2-mark loss is failing to mention that $P_{fc}$ cannot change instantaneously and that the EMS must respect $\tau_{fc} \cdot \dot{P}_{fc} + P_{fc} = P_{fc,ref}$.
>
> 5. **"State Machine = Fuzzy Logic":** These are *different*. State machine uses crisp thresholds; fuzzy logic uses membership functions and linguistic rules. Examiners notice this error immediately.
>
> 6. **Forgetting units in numericals:** Always state $\dot{m}_{H_2}$ in **kg/s** or **g/s** explicitly. Marks are routinely lost for missing units in KTU scripts.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU 2024 Prep):**

- [x] **Definition of EMS:** Supervisory control that splits $P_{req}(t)$ between FC and battery/UC.
- [x] **Why hybridize:** PEMFC has slow transient response ($\tau_{fc} \approx 1$–3 s), cannot absorb regen, and fails cold-start.
- [x] **Three storage classes:** FC (energy-dense, slow) + Battery (mid) + UC (power-dense, fast).
- [x] **Rule-based strategies:** Thermostat, Load Following, State Machine, Frequency Decoupling.
- [x] **Optimization-based strategies:** ECMS (real-time, local), DP (offline, global benchmark), PMP (theoretical, co-state), Fuzzy/RL (intelligent).
- [x] **ECMS cost function:** $J = \dot{m}_{H_2} + s \cdot P_{bat} / (\eta_{dis} \cdot \text{LHV})$.
- [x] **Equivalence factor $s$:** Tuning parameter; updated online via PI control on SoC error.
- [x] **Hydrogen consumption equation:** $\dot{m}_{H_2} = P_{fc} / (\eta_{fc} \cdot \text{LHV}_{H_2})$.
- [x] **Constants to memorize:** $F = 96\,485$ C/mol, $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K), $\text{LHV}_{H_2} = 240$ kJ/mol, $M_{H_2} = 2$ g/mol.
- [x] **State Machine:** Dominant in production (Mirai 2, Nexo); deterministic, certifiable, tunable.
- [x] **DP / PMP:** Used only as offline benchmarks; cannot run in real time.
- [x] **FC 1st-order lag:** $\tau_{fc} \dot{P}_{fc} + P_{fc} = P_{fc,ref}$.
- [x] **Cold start:** Mirai 2 reaches −30 °C operation; key enabler is membrane dry-out + purge.
- [x] **Top 5 implementation challenges:** H₂ storage, cold start, MEA degradation, thermal management, refuelling infrastructure.
- [x] **LHV vs HHV:** PEMFC → LHV; SOFC → HHV.
- [x] **SoC operating window:** 40–80 % typical for battery health preservation.
- [x] **Co-state $\lambda(t)$ in PMP:** Has units of kg-H₂ per unit-SoC; drives the optimal control trajectory.
- [x] **Valuation safeguard:** Always state units; always specify LHV vs HHV; always respect FC dynamics.

> **Final Tip for KTU 2024:** Examiners award full marks only when the answer combines (i) a correct formula, (ii) physical interpretation, (iii) a numerical or block-diagrammatic illustration, and (iv) acknowledgement of practical constraints (cold start, lifetime, SoC bounds). Memorize the formula sheet in Section 2.3 verbatim.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vehicle performance metrics: analysing acceleration, range, and efficiency of hydrogen fuel cell vehicles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT832 - HYDROGEN AND FUEL CELL VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Vehicle performance metrics: analysing acceleration, range, and efficiency of hydrogen fuel cell vehicles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Vehicle Performance Metrics** in the context of Hydrogen Fuel Cell Vehicles (HFCVs) are a quantified set of engineering parameters used to evaluate how effectively a fuel cell powertrain can deliver useful mechanical work to the wheels while satisfying the driver's demands for speed, acceleration, and travel distance under a defined duty cycle.

The three principal performance metrics covered in **KTU Module 4** are:

| # | Metric | Engineering Symbol | Standard Unit |
|---|--------|-------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Acceleration capability | $a \;(m/s^{2})$ & $t_{0\text{-}100}\;(s)$ | m/s² and seconds |
| 2 | Driving range per fill | $R\;(km)$ | kilometres |
| 3 | Energy conversion efficiency | $\eta_{FC},\;\eta_{TTW},\;\eta_{WTW}$ | dimensionless (or %) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 4 Highlight:** The analysis of these three metrics must always account for the **hydrogen-specific properties** — the very low density of gaseous hydrogen, its **Lower Heating Value (LHV) of 120 MJ/kg**, and the fact that the only by-product of the fuel cell reaction is **water vapour**. This is why efficiency analysis of HFCVs is fundamentally different from battery EVs or internal combustion engines.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water-Bottle Athlete"

Imagine a long-distance runner who must carry their own drinking water (instead of sipping from roadside taps). Three things determine how the runner performs on race day:

- **Acceleration (Sprint start)** → How quickly the runner can leave the starting blocks. This depends on **leg muscle strength (fuel cell power output)** and **body weight (vehicle curb weight)**.
- **Range (Race distance)** → How far the runner can go on one full water bottle. This depends on **bottle size (hydrogen tank capacity in kg)** and **sipping rate (hydrogen consumption per km)**.
- **Efficiency (Stamina)** → How much useful motion the runner extracts from each sip. A trained runner wastes less energy, just like a well-tuned fuel cell stack operating near its **polarisation-curve sweet spot** converts more hydrogen chemical energy into electricity.

In an HFCV, the **fuel cell stack + electric motor + DC-DC converter + battery buffer** act together as the "athletic system," while the **hydrogen tank** is the "water bottle."

### 1.3 Why These Metrics Matter for HFCVs

> [!NOTE]
> For an HFCV student, these three metrics are the *grade card* of any fuel cell vehicle design. Without them, you cannot compare a Toyota Mirai against a Hyundai NEXO or a Hyundai XCIENT fuel cell truck. KTU examiners frequently ask students to **derive** acceleration time, **estimate** range, or **compare efficiencies** of competing powertrains — so mastering these equations is non-negotiable.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Tractive Force Equation — The Foundation of All Vehicle Dynamics

Every performance metric begins with the longitudinal force balance on a vehicle climbing a road of slope angle $\alpha$ at speed $v$.

**Step 1 — Identify all opposing forces at the tyre contact patch.**

A vehicle in motion must overcome three external loads in the longitudinal direction:

1. **Rolling Resistance Force** $F_{r}$ — caused by tyre hysteresis deformation as the wheel rolls on asphalt.
2. **Grade Resistance Force** $F_{g}$ — caused by gravity pulling the vehicle back when on an incline.
3. **Aerodynamic Drag Force** $F_{a}$ — caused by air viscosity and pressure differential around the vehicle body.

**Step 2 — Sum the loads and equate to tractive force from the powertrain.**

The powertrain (here, fuel cell + motor) must produce a tractive force $F_{t}$ at the wheels that exceeds these loads so that net force becomes acceleration.

**Step 3 — Apply Newton's Second Law in the longitudinal direction.**

The net force drives the equivalent vehicle mass $m_{eq} = \delta \cdot m$, where $\delta$ is the **mass factor** (accounts for rotating inertia of motor, wheels, drivetrain).

### 2.2 Acceleration Analysis

**Step 1 — Compute net accelerating force.**

$F_{net} = F_{t} - (F_{r} + F_{g} + F_{a})$

**Step 2 — Translate net force to linear acceleration.**

$a = \dfrac{F_{net}}{m \cdot \delta}$

**Step 3 — Integrate to get $0\text{-}v$ time.**

The **0–100 km/h time** ($t_{0\text{-}100}$) is the most reported acceleration metric. Because $F_{r}$, $F_{g}$, $F_{a}$ are functions of speed, a closed-form integral must use either tabulated driving cycles or numerical integration.

For a **flat road** ($\alpha = 0$) at **low speed** (where $F_{a}$ is negligible), the equation simplifies and $t_{0\text{-}v}$ becomes:

$t = \dfrac{m \cdot \delta \cdot v}{F_{t} - \mu_{r} \cdot m \cdot g}$

**Step 4 — Maximum speed determination.**

Maximum speed $v_{max}$ is reached when the tractive force exactly balances the load forces:

$F_{t}(v_{max}) = F_{r}(v_{max}) + F_{g}(v_{max}) + F_{a}(v_{max})$

### 2.3 Range Analysis

**Step 1 — Define usable hydrogen mass on-board.**

The on-board hydrogen mass $m_{H_{2}}$ is determined by the high-pressure tank volume $V_{tank}$ and the storage density $\rho_{H_{2}}$ (in kg/m³) at 350 bar or 700 bar.

**Step 2 — Compute total chemical energy stored.**

$E_{chem} = m_{H_{2}} \times \text{LHV}_{H_{2}}$

Where **LHV of hydrogen = 120 MJ/kg** (the most commonly used reference for vehicle studies since exhaust water leaves as vapour).

**Step 3 — Apply the system efficiency to get useful wheel energy.**

$E_{wheel} = \eta_{system} \times E_{chem} = \eta_{FC} \times \eta_{DC\text{-}DC} \times \eta_{motor} \times E_{chem}$

**Step 4 — Compute the energy demand per kilometre.**

$E_{km} = \int_{0}^{1\,km} (F_{r} + F_{g} + F_{a})\,dx$

**Step 5 — Range is then simply the ratio.**

$R = \dfrac{E_{wheel}}{E_{km}}$

### 2.4 Efficiency Analysis — The Three Layers

**Layer 1 — Fuel Cell Stack Efficiency ($\eta_{FC}$)**

Based on the actual operating cell voltage versus the thermodynamically reversible voltage:

$\eta_{FC} = \dfrac{V_{cell}}{E_{rev}}$

Where the reversible voltage $E_{rev} \approx 1.229\,V$ when using **HHV** of hydrogen, and $E_{rev} \approx 1.481\,V$ when using LHV in the denominator (Gibbs vs. HHV convention debate — **KTU convention: use HHV for $\eta_{FC}$ reporting**).

**Layer 2 — Tank-to-Wheel Efficiency ($\eta_{TTW}$)**

Includes the fuel cell + DC-DC converter + traction motor + transmission:

$\eta_{TTW} = \eta_{FC} \times \eta_{DC\text{-}DC} \times \eta_{motor} \times \eta_{transmission}$

A typical HFCV has $\eta_{TTW} \approx 45\text{-}55\%$.

**Layer 3 — Well-to-Wheel Efficiency ($\eta_{WTW}$)**

Multiplies upstream efficiency of hydrogen production with downstream $\eta_{TTW}$:

$\eta_{WTW} = \eta_{WTP} \times \eta_{TTW}$

Where $\eta_{WTP}$ depends on production route:
- **Green hydrogen (electrolysis with renewables):** $\eta_{WTP} \approx 65\text{-}70\%$
- **Blue hydrogen (SMR with CCS):** $\eta_{WTP} \approx 60\text{-}75\%$
- **Grey hydrogen (SMR without CCS):** $\eta_{WTP} \approx 65\text{-}75\%$ thermodynamically, but with high carbon intensity.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — these are the equations KTU examiners most frequently test.**

| # | Performance Metric | Governing Equation | Variables & Units | Typical HFCV Value |
|---|--------------------|--------------------|-------------------|--------------------|
| 1 | Rolling resistance | $F_{r} = \mu_{r}\,m\,g\,\cos\alpha$ | $\mu_{r}$ (coefficient, dimensionless), $m$ (kg), $g=9.81\,m/s^{2}$ | $\mu_{r} \approx 0.008\text{-}0.015$ |
| 2 | Grade resistance | $F_{g} = m\,g\,\sin\alpha$ | $\alpha$ (road slope, rad) | 0 (flat road) |
| 3 | Aerodynamic drag | $F_{a} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,C_{d}\,A\,v^{2}$ | $\rho = 1.225\,kg/m^{3}$ (air), $C_{d}$ (drag coeff.), $A$ (frontal area, $m^{2}$), $v$ (m/s) | $C_{d} \approx 0.24\text{-}0.29$ |
| 4 | Net acceleration | $a = (F_{t} - F_{r} - F_{g} - F_{a})\,/\,(m\,\delta)$ | $\delta$ (mass factor, $\approx 1.05\text{-}1.1$) | depends on driving cycle |
| 5 | Acceleration time (flat, low speed) | $t = (m\,\delta\,v)\,/\,(F_{t} - \mu_{r}\,m\,g)$ | $t$ in seconds | Mirai: $t_{0\text{-}100}\approx 9.2\,s$ |
| 6 | Max speed condition | $F_{t}(v_{max}) = F_{r} + F_{a}$ | grade $=0$ assumed | Mirai: $v_{max}\approx 175\,km/h$ |
| 7 | Chemical energy on-board | $E_{chem} = m_{H_{2}} \times \text{LHV}_{H_{2}}$ | LHV$_{H_{2}} = 120\,MJ/kg$ | 5 kg tank $\Rightarrow 600\,MJ$ |
| 8 | H2 mass flow rate | $\dot{m}_{H_{2}} = P_{FC}\,/\,(\text{LHV}_{H_{2}} \cdot \eta_{FC})$ | $P_{FC}$ in W | varies with load |
| 9 | Range | $R = E_{wheel}\,/\,E_{km}$ | km | Mirai: $\approx 650\,km$ |
| 10 | Fuel cell efficiency | $\eta_{FC} = V_{cell}\,/\,E_{rev}$ | $E_{rev}\approx 1.229\,V$ (HHV) | 50–60 % at rated power |
| 11 | Tank-to-Wheel efficiency | $\eta_{TTW} = \eta_{FC}\cdot\eta_{DC\text{-}DC}\cdot\eta_{motor}\cdot\eta_{transmission}$ | each term dimensionless | 45–55 % |
| 12 | Well-to-Wheel efficiency | $\eta_{WTW} = \eta_{WTP}\cdot\eta_{TTW}$ | $\eta_{WTP}$ route-dependent | 22–35 % |

### 2.6 Real-World Utility

These metrics are not academic curiosities — they directly drive **OEM (Original Equipment Manufacturer) decisions** about stack size, tank pressure, and battery hybridisation:

- A **higher $\eta_{FC}$** at part-load allows a smaller tank and lower vehicle cost.
- A **larger buffer battery** lets the fuel cell operate near its most efficient point, improving overall range.
- **Aerodynamic drag** dominates highway range, so HFCV manufacturers aggressively pursue $C_{d}<0.25$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A common KTU-style exam trick is to ask why a hydrogen vehicle's *urban* range is often **greater** than its highway range — the answer is that aerodynamic drag $F_{a}\propto v^{2}$ dominates at high speed, consuming more fuel cell energy per kilometre despite the fuel cell's improving part-load efficiency.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Tractive Force Equation

**Starting point:** Newton's Second Law applied to the vehicle as a rigid body moving along the road.

$$
\sum F_{x} = m \cdot \frac{dv}{dt}
$$

**Step 1 — Identify all forces acting in the longitudinal $x$-direction.**

The powertrain supplies a forward tractive force $F_{t}$ at the tyre contact patches. The resisting forces are rolling resistance $F_{r}$, gravitational component along slope $F_{g}$, and aerodynamic drag $F_{a}$.

$$
F_{t} - F_{r} - F_{g} - F_{a} = m \cdot \frac{dv}{dt}
$$

**Step 2 — Express each resistive force.**

Rolling resistance arises from tyre deformation work; it is empirically proportional to the normal force $N = m g \cos\alpha$:

$$
F_{r} = \mu_{r} \cdot N = \mu_{r} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos\alpha
$$

The grade (gravity) component along the slope is:

$$
F_{g} = m \cdot g \cdot \sin\alpha
$$

The aerodynamic drag from Bernoulli-type viscous flow analysis around a bluff body:

$$
F_{a} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho_{air} \cdot C_{d} \cdot A_{f} \cdot v^{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Combine.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{t} - \mu_{r} m g \cos\alpha - m g \sin\alpha - \frac{1}{2}\rho C_{d} A v^{2} &= m \frac{dv}{dt}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Introduce rotational inertia correction.**

Rotating components (motor rotor, gearbox, wheels) carry kinetic energy but not translational kinetic energy directly. Their effective contribution is lumped into the mass factor $\delta > 1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{t} - \mu_{r} m g \cos\alpha - m g \sin\alpha - \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_{d} A v^{2} &= \delta \cdot m \cdot \frac{dv}{dt}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **fundamental vehicle dynamics equation** for any road vehicle, including HFCVs.

### 3.2 Derivation of 0–100 km/h Acceleration Time (Flat Road, Simplified)

**Step 1 — Flat road assumption.**

For flat road, $\alpha = 0$, so $\cos\alpha = 1$ and $\sin\alpha = 0$. Equation becomes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{t} - \mu_{r} m g - \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_{d} A v^{2} &= \delta \, m \, \frac{dv}{dt}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Low-speed approximation.**

At low speeds ($v < 30\,km/h$), aerodynamic drag is small compared to rolling resistance (verified by substituting $v = 8.33\,m/s$, $C_{d}=0.25$, $A=2.2\,m^{2}$: $F_{a}\approx 28\,N$ versus $F_{r}\approx 180\,N$ for $m=1500\,kg$). Hence the $v^{2}$ term can be dropped for back-of-envelope estimation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{t} - \mu_{r} m g &\approx \delta \, m \, \frac{dv}{dt}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve the differential equation assuming constant $F_{t}$.**

For a fully open throttle, motor and fuel cell operate at rated power with tractive force $F_{t} = P_{t}/v$. Strictly, $F_{t}$ varies with $v$. As an approximation for short acceleration bursts at constant tractive force:

$$
\frac{dv}{dt} = \frac{F_{t} - \mu_{r} m g}{\delta \, m} = \text{constant}
$$

**Step 4 — Integrate from $0$ to $v_{f}$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_{0}^{v_{f}} dv &= \int_{0}^{t_{0-v_{f}}} \frac{F_{t} - \mu_{r} m g}{\delta \, m} \, dt
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_{f} &= \frac{F_{t} - \mu_{r} m g}{\delta \, m} \cdot t_{0-v_{f}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the time.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{0-v_{f}} &= \frac{\delta \, m \, v_{f}}{F_{t} - \mu_{r} m g}
\end{aligned}
$$

For $v_{f} = 100\,km/h = 27.78\,m/s$, this gives the **0–100 km/h time** in seconds.

### 3.3 Derivation of Vehicle Range

**Step 1 — Define energy demand per kilometre.**

For a vehicle travelling at constant speed $v$ on flat road, the equilibrium tractive force equals the sum of resistances:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{t} = \mu_{r} m g + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_{d} A v^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Power demand at the wheels.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{wheel} = F_{t} \cdot v = (\mu_{r} m g)\,v + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_{d} A v^{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Energy demand per kilometre.**

Since $P_{wheel}$ is in W and $1\,km = 1000\,m$, energy per km is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{km} = \frac{P_{wheel}}{v} \cdot 1000 = 1000 \cdot (\mu_{r} m g + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_{d} A v^{2})
\end{aligned}
$$

In units of J/km.

**Step 4 — Convert hydrogen mass to wheel-available energy.**

Total chemical energy in tank:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{chem} = m_{H_{2},\,tank} \cdot \text{LHV}_{H_{2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Wheel-available energy (after all conversion losses):

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{wheel} = m_{H_{2},\,tank} \cdot \text{LHV}_{H_{2}} \cdot \eta_{TTW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Range in km.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= \frac{E_{wheel}}{E_{km}} = \frac{m_{H_{2},\,tank} \cdot \text{LHV}_{H_{2}} \cdot \eta_{TTW}}{1000 \cdot (\mu_{r} m g + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_{d} A v^{2})}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **KTU board-exam standard range equation**.

### 3.4 Derivation of Fuel Cell Stack Efficiency

**Step 1 — Define reversible work.**

For the hydrogen–oxygen reaction at standard conditions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{2} + \tfrac{1}{2}O_{2} \rightarrow H_{2}O_{(l)}
\end{aligned}
$$

The Gibbs free energy change $\Delta G = -237.2\,kJ/mol$ at $25^{\circ}C$, and the enthalpy change (HHV) $\Delta H = -285.8\,kJ/mol$.

**Step 2 — Reversible cell voltage.**

Using Faraday's law, each mole of $H_{2}$ transfers 2 moles of electrons. Reversible voltage based on **HHV**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{rev,\,HHV} = \frac{\Delta H}{n F} = \frac{285800}{2 \times 96485} \approx 1.481\,V
\end{aligned}
$$

If we use $\Delta G$ instead, $E_{rev,\,HHV\text{-}Gibbs} \approx 1.229\,V$.

**Step 3 — Actual operating voltage.**

The polarisation curve of a PEMFC gives actual cell voltage $V_{cell}$ as a function of current density $j$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{cell}(j) = E_{rev} - \eta_{act}(j) - \eta_{ohmic}(j) - \eta_{conc}(j)
\end{aligned}
$$

Where the three loss terms are activation, ohmic, and concentration overpotentials.

**Step 4 — Stack efficiency.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{FC} = \frac{V_{cell}}{E_{rev}}
\end{aligned}
$$

For example, at $V_{cell} = 0.7\,V$ and $E_{rev}=1.229\,V$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{FC} = \frac{0.7}{1.229} \approx 0.569 = 56.9\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (For KTU Practice)

**Given:**
- Curb mass $m = 1800\,kg$, mass factor $\delta = 1.08$
- $\mu_{r} = 0.010$, $g = 9.81\,m/s^{2}$
- $C_{d} = 0.26$, $A_{f} = 2.3\,m^{2}$, $\rho = 1.225\,kg/m^{3}$
- Hydrogen tank: $m_{H_{2}} = 5.6\,kg$ at 700 bar
- $\eta_{FC} = 0.55$, $\eta_{motor} = 0.93$, $\eta_{DC\text{-}DC} = 0.96$
- Cruising speed $v = 100\,km/h = 27.78\,m/s$

**Find:** Range $R$ in km.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{r} &= 0.010 \times 1800 \times 9.81 = 176.58\,N
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{a} &= \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times 0.26 \times 2.3 \times (27.78)^{2} \\
     &= 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.26 \times 2.3 \times 771.6 \\
     &= 282.7\,N
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{km} &= 1000 \times (176.58 + 282.7) = 459{,}280\,J/km
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{TTW} &= 0.55 \times 0.96 \times 0.93 = 0.491
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{wheel} &= 5.6 \times 120 \times 10^{6} \times 0.491 = 329.95\,MJ
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= \frac{329.95 \times 10^{6}}{459{,}280} \approx 718\,km
\end{aligned}
$$

This matches the order of magnitude of real HFCVs such as the Toyota Mirai 2.

---

## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Vehicle Performance Analysis Flow (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[HFCV Powertrain Inputs] --> B[Hydrogen Tank: mH2 in kg]
    A --> C[Fuel Cell Stack: rated P_FC in kW]
    A --> D[Vehicle Body: m, Cd, A_f, mu_r]

    B --> E[Chemical Energy: E_chem = mH2 x LHV_H2]
    C --> F[FC Efficiency: eta_FC = V_cell / E_rev]
    D --> G[Tractive Force Balance: Ft - Fr - Fg - Fa = delta m dv/dt]

    E --> H[System Efficiency Chain]
    F --> H
    I[Motor Efficiency: eta_motor] --> H
    J[DC-DC Converter Efficiency: eta_DC] --> H

    H --> K[Tank-to-Wheel Efficiency: eta_TTW]
    K --> L[Range Calculation: R = eta_TTW x E_chem / E_km]
    G --> M[Acceleration: a = F_net / delta m]
    G --> N[Maximum Speed: v_max from Ft = Fa + Fr]

    L --> O[Final Performance Report]
    M --> O
    N --> O

    P[Hydrogen Production Route] --> Q[Well-to-Tank Efficiency: eta_WTP]
    Q --> R[Well-to-Wheel Efficiency: eta_WTW = eta_WTP x eta_TTW]
    K --> R
    R --> O
```

### 4.2 HFCV Powertrain Block Diagram Showing Performance Measurement Points

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H2TANK[H2 Tank 700 bar] -->|mH2 in kg| REGUL[Pressure Regulator]
    REGUL --> FCSTACK[PEMFC Stack]
    FCSTACK -->|eta_FC ~ 55 percent| DCDC[DC-DC Converter]
    DCDC -->|eta_DC ~ 96 percent| INVERTER[Traction Inverter]
    INVERTER -->|eta_motor ~ 93 percent| MOTOR[3-phase AC Motor]
    BAT[Battery Buffer] -->|supplementary power| INVERTER
    MOTOR --> GEAR[Single-speed Gearbox]
    GEAR --> WHEELS[Drive Wheels]
    WHEELS -->|delta m, Cd, A| ROAD[Road: F_r, F_g, F_a]
    ROAD -->|feedback| WHEELS
    METER1[Performance Meter 1: Acceleration] -.measures.-> MOTOR
    METER2[Performance Meter 2: Range] -.measures.-> H2TANK
    METER3[Performance Meter 3: Efficiency] -.measures.-> FCSTACK
```

### 4.3 Efficiency Layer Cake — Visual Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[Layer 1: Fuel Cell Stack Efficiency eta_FC ~ 50 to 60 percent]
    L2[Layer 2: Tank-to-Wheel eta_TTW = eta_FC x eta_DC x eta_motor ~ 45 to 55 percent]
    L3[Layer 3: Well-to-Tank eta_WTP route dependent 60 to 75 percent]
    L4[Layer 4: Well-to-Wheel eta_WTW = eta_WTP x eta_TTW ~ 22 to 35 percent]

    L1 --> L2
    L3 --> L4
    L2 --> L4

    style L1 fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,color:#000
    style L2 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
    style L3 fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000
    style L4 fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** Each Mermaid block deliberately uses purely alphanumeric node IDs (e.g., `H2TANK`, `FCSTACK`) and uppercase label text only — this avoids the rendering pitfalls of reserved keywords or inline markdown formatting inside node labels. Use these diagrams as visual memory anchors during KTU exam revision.
