<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Part A questions expect **2–3 sentences** with one supporting equation. Brevity + precision = full marks.

#### **Question A1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define "Well-to-Wheel efficiency" of a hydrogen fuel cell vehicle and state its typical range for green-hydrogen-fuelled HFCVs.**

**Model Answer:**
Well-to-Wheel efficiency ($\eta_{WTW}$) is the ratio of useful mechanical energy delivered at the vehicle wheels to the primary energy input at the hydrogen production well. It is the product of Well-to-Tank efficiency ($\eta_{WTP}$) and Tank-to-Wheel efficiency ($\eta_{TTW}$):

$$
\eta_{WTW} = \eta_{WTP} \times \eta_{TTW}
$$

For green hydrogen (electrolysis powered by solar/wind), $\eta_{WTW}$ typically lies in the range of **22 % to 35 %**, which is roughly 2× higher than a comparable gasoline ICE vehicle ($\approx 13\text{-}20\%$) on a well-to-wheel basis.

---

#### **Question A2.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Why is the cruising range of an HFCV at 120 km/h typically lower than at 80 km/h on a flat highway?**

**Model Answer:**
The aerodynamic drag force scales with the square of vehicle speed:

$$
F_{a} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,C_{d}\,A_{f}\,v^{2}
$$

Hence energy demand per kilometre, $E_{km} \propto \mu_{r} m g + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_{d} A_{f} v^{2}$, grows rapidly at higher speeds. At 120 km/h the $v^{2}$ term dominates, causing higher hydrogen consumption per km and reducing total range. The fuel cell's part-load efficiency gain at higher current density does **not** offset this loss, hence **range decreases monotonically with cruise speed**.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Part B requires **internal choice**. Students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Both are designed to be answerable in 7–9 pages of a standard answer booklet.

---

#### **PART B — Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the tractive force equation for a hydrogen fuel cell vehicle moving up an incline of angle $\alpha$ at constant speed. Explain the physical origin of each term. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An HFCV has the following specifications:
- Curb mass $m = 1700\,kg$, $\delta = 1.07$
- Rolling resistance coefficient $\mu_{r} = 0.012$
- Aerodynamic drag coefficient $C_{d} = 0.27$, frontal area $A = 2.2\,m^{2}$
- Hydrogen tank: 5.0 kg usable at 700 bar
- LHV of H₂ = 120 MJ/kg, $\eta_{TTW} = 0.50$

Calculate the cruising range in km at a constant speed of 90 km/h on a flat road. Take air density $\rho = 1.225\,kg/m^{3}$, $g = 9.81\,m/s^{2}$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

Starting from Newton's Second Law in the longitudinal direction:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{t} - F_{r} - F_{g} - F_{a} = \delta \cdot m \cdot \frac{dv}{dt}
\end{aligned}
$$

- $F_{r} = \mu_{r}\,m\,g\,\cos\alpha$ — caused by hysteresis losses in the tyre as it deforms under the normal reaction $N = m g \cos\alpha$.
- $F_{g} = m\,g\,\sin\alpha$ — gravitational component acting along the inclined road surface.
- $F_{a} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_{d} A v^{2}$ — pressure and viscous drag from air flowing past the vehicle body.

Combining:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{t} = \mu_{r} m g \cos\alpha + m g \sin\alpha + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_{d} A v^{2} + \delta m \frac{dv}{dt}
\end{aligned}
$$

For constant speed, $\frac{dv}{dt}=0$, eliminating the inertial term.

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1:** Compute resistances at $v = 90\,km/h = 25\,m/s$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{r} &= 0.012 \times 1700 \times 9.81 = 200.12\,N
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{a} &= \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times 0.27 \times 2.2 \times 25^{2} \\
     &= 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.27 \times 2.2 \times 625 \\
     &= 227.53\,N
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Energy demand per km.

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{km} &= 1000 \times (200.12 + 227.53) = 427{,}650\,J/km
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Total wheel-available energy.

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{wheel} &= 5.0 \times 120 \times 10^{6} \times 0.50 = 300\,MJ
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Range.

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= \frac{300 \times 10^{6}}{427{,}650} \approx 701.5\,km
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** Approximately **702 km** at 90 km/h cruising on a flat road.

---

#### **PART B — Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a polarisation curve, derive the expression for fuel cell stack efficiency. Explain why $\eta_{FC}$ decreases as current density increases. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 100 kW PEMFC stack operates at average cell voltage $V_{cell} = 0.68\,V$. The stack has 300 cells in series. Compute:
(i) the stack efficiency using HHV convention
(ii) the tank-to-wheel efficiency assuming $\eta_{DC\text{-}DC} = 0.95$, $\eta_{motor} = 0.94$, $\eta_{transmission} = 0.97$
(iii) the hydrogen mass flow rate at rated power **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

The polarisation curve gives cell voltage $V_{cell}$ as a function of current density $j$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{cell}(j) = E_{rev} - \eta_{act}(j) - \eta_{ohmic}(j) - \eta_{conc}(j)
\end{aligned}
$$

The fuel cell efficiency is the ratio of useful electrical work output to the chemical energy input (using HHV for $E_{rev}=1.229\,V$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{FC} = \frac{V_{cell}}{E_{rev,\,HHV}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Why $\eta_{FC}$ decreases with $j$:**
- **Activation loss $\eta_{act}$** dominates at low $j$ — slow reaction kinetics at the catalyst surface require overpotential.
- **Ohmic loss $\eta_{ohmic}$** grows linearly with $j$ due to ionic resistance in the membrane and electronic resistance in electrodes/bipolar plates.
- **Concentration loss $\eta_{conc}$** dominates at high $j$ — reactant depletion at the catalyst layer causes sharp voltage drop.

Since all three overpotentials **increase** with $j$, $V_{cell}$ falls, and so does $\eta_{FC}$.

**Model Solution (b):**

**(i)** HHV convention: $E_{rev,\,HHV} = 1.229\,V$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{FC} = \frac{0.68}{1.229} = 0.5533 = 55.33\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii)** Tank-to-wheel efficiency:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{TTW} &= \eta_{FC} \times \eta_{DC\text{-}DC} \times \eta_{motor} \times \eta_{transmission} \\
          &= 0.5533 \times 0.95 \times 0.94 \times 0.97 \\
          &= 0.479 \approx 47.9\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii)** Hydrogen mass flow rate:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{H_{2}} &= \frac{P_{FC}}{\text{LHV}_{H_{2}} \cdot \eta_{FC}} \\
                &= \frac{100{,}000}{120 \times 10^{6} \times 0.5533} \\
                &= 1.506 \times 10^{-3}\,kg/s \\
                &= 5.42\,kg/hr
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answers:** (i) **55.33 %**, (ii) **≈ 47.9 %**, (iii) **≈ 5.42 kg/hr** at rated power.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes KTU examiners penalise heavily:**
>
> 1. **LHV vs HHV confusion** — When computing $\eta_{FC}$, you must state which convention is used. LHV convention gives $E_{rev} = 1.481\,V$, HHV gives $1.229\,V$. Mixing them silently costs **2 marks** immediately.
> 2. **Forgetting the mass factor $\delta$** — Many students omit it in acceleration derivations, losing 1 mark.
> 3. **Using $kg/m^{3}$ instead of $J/km$** — Energy per km must be in Joules, not Newton-metres confusion. KTU examiners are strict on unit consistency.
> 4. **Neglecting DC-DC converter efficiency** — A common error is to multiply only $\eta_{FC} \times \eta_{motor}$ and forget the DC-DC stage. This produces an inflated $\eta_{TTW}$ and **3-mark penalty**.
> 5. **Range calculation without $\eta_{TTW}$** — Writing $R = m_{H_{2}} \cdot \text{LHV}_{H_{2}} / E_{km}$ without the efficiency factor ignores real-world losses and is a **4-mark deduction**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Vehicle performance of HFCVs is quantified by three metrics**: acceleration (s), range (km), efficiency (dimensionless).
- **The tractive force equation** $F_{t} - F_{r} - F_{g} - F_{a} = \delta m \frac{dv}{dt}$ is the foundation of all performance calculations.
- **Rolling resistance** scales linearly with normal force ($\mu_{r} m g \cos\alpha$).
- **Aerodynamic drag** scales with $v^{2}$, so it dominates at highway speeds.
- **Mass factor $\delta$** captures rotational inertia of drivetrain components; typical value 1.05–1.10.
- **Acceleration time on flat road (low-speed approximation):** $t_{0-v} = \delta m v / (F_{t} - \mu_{r} m g)$.
- **Range formula:** $R = \dfrac{m_{H_{2}} \cdot \text{LHV}_{H_{2}} \cdot \eta_{TTW}}{1000 \cdot (\mu_{r} m g + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_{d} A v^{2})}$.
- **LHV of hydrogen** = **120 MJ/kg**; HHV = 142 MJ/kg.
- **Fuel cell efficiency** = $V_{cell}/E_{rev}$; HHV convention uses $E_{rev}=1.229\,V$.
- **Three efficiency layers**: $\eta_{FC}$ (50–60 %), $\eta_{TTW}$ (45–55 %), $\eta_{WTW}$ (22–35 %).
- **Higher cruising speed → lower range** because $F_{a} \propto v^{2}$.
- **Buffer battery** in HFCVs lets the fuel cell operate at its most efficient point, raising overall $\eta_{TTW}$.
- **Standard constants to memorise**: $g = 9.81\,m/s^{2}$, $\rho_{air} = 1.225\,kg/m^{3}$, LHV$_{H_{2}} = 120\,MJ/kg$, HHV$_{H_{2}} = 142\,MJ/kg$, Faraday constant $F = 96485\,C/mol$.
- **KTU examiner keywords** to use in answers: "tract effort," "polarisation curve," "mass factor," "well-to-wheel," "system efficiency chain."

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Optimization techniques for fuel cell performance, including calibration and tuning of fuel cell systems for different driving conditions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT832 - HYDROGEN AND FUEL CELL VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Optimization techniques for fuel cell performance, including calibration and tuning of fuel cell systems for different driving conditions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Optimization of Fuel Cell Performance** is the systematic process of adjusting operational parameters—namely air/fuel stoichiometry, operating temperature, pressure, relative humidity, and current density—to maximize the power density, efficiency, durability, and dynamic response of a Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cell (PEMFC) system while simultaneously minimizing hydrogen consumption, parasitic losses, and thermal degradation.

**Calibration and Tuning** is the closed-loop mapping of these optimal operating set-points to specific driving conditions (urban, highway, cold-start, acceleration) using model-based, lookup-table, or adaptive control algorithms, often executed through an **Energy Management Strategy (EMS)** and a **Fuel Cell Control Unit (FCCU)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight (CO4 Mapping):**
> The student must distinguish between **steady-state optimization** (polarization curve tuning for efficiency) and **transient/dynamic optimization** (load-following under drive cycles like UDDS, HWFET, WHTC, NEDC, and WLTP).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Carburetor Tune-Up" Intuition

Imagine a PEMFC stack as a **symphony orchestra where every instrument (humidifier, compressor, cooler, hydrogen injector) must play in perfect harmony**:

- If you blow **too much air** (excess λ), the membrane dries out → voltage sags and the membrane cracks.
- If you blow **too little air**, oxygen starvation occurs → reverse voltage and carbon corrosion destroy the catalyst.
- If you feed **cold hydrogen**, the reaction kinetics slow down → polarization losses shoot up.
- If you feed **over-humidified hydrogen**, the cathode floods → mass-transport limit collapses.

**Optimization = finding the "Goldilocks zone"** for *every* driving instant. During acceleration (high current demand), the system must pre-purge, raise air-flow rapidly, and lower humidity. During idling (low current), it must coast in a high-efficiency low-air mode to save compressor parasitic power.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Metrics (KTU Board Data):**
> - Faraday Constant: **F = 96,485 C/mol**
> - Universal Gas Constant: **R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)**
> - Standard Hydrogen Higher Heating Value (HHV): **ΔH° = −285.83 kJ/mol**
> - Standard Hydrogen Lower Heating Value (LHV): **ΔH° = −241.82 kJ/mol**
> - Theoretical Open Circuit Voltage (HHV): **E°_th = 1.481 V**
> - Theoretical Open Circuit Voltage (LHV): **E°_th = 1.253 V**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Optimization Parameter Universe

A PEMFC stack has **five (5) primary control knobs** that the FCCU manipulates in real time:

1. **Air Stoichiometry Ratio (λ_air)** — typically **1.5 – 3.0**
2. **Hydrogen Stoichiometry Ratio (λ_H2)** — typically **1.1 – 1.5** (dead-ended mode often preferred)
3. **Stack Operating Temperature (T_stack)** — typically **60 – 80 °C**
4. **Cathode Inlet Pressure (P_cath)** — typically **1.0 – 3.0 bar (absolute)**
5. **Membrane Relative Humidity (RH)** — typically **50% – 100%** at both anode and cathode

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Optimization Lever

- **λ_air ↑ →** Higher O₂ partial pressure → reduces concentration loss (η_conc), but ↑ parasitic power of the compressor (η_compressor).
- **T_stack ↑ →** Faster reaction kinetics (lower η_act via Arrhenius), but ↓ membrane hydration and accelerates catalyst degradation.
- **P_cath ↑ →** Higher Nernst voltage (E_Nernst ↑ with P_O2), but compressor work ↑ cubically.
- **RH ↑ →** Higher proton conductivity (lower η_ohmic), but cathode flooding risk ↑.
- **λ_H2 ↓ (toward dead-end)** → Improved H₂ utilization, but risk of H₂ starvation during transients.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Parameter | LaTeX Form | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Nernst Voltage** | $E_{Nernst} = E^\circ + \dfrac{RT}{2F}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_{H_2}\sqrt{P_{O_2}}}{P_{H_2O}}\right)$ | Predicts reversible cell voltage |
| 2 | **Tafel Activation Overpotential** | $\eta_{act} = \dfrac{RT}{\alpha n F}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{j}{j_0}\right)$ | Models kinetic losses at low current |
| 3 | **Ohmic Overpotential** | $\eta_{ohm} = j \cdot R_{ASR}$ | Captures membrane + contact resistance |
| 4 | **Concentration Overpotential** | $\eta_{conc} = \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln\!\left(1 - \dfrac{j}{j_L}\right)$ | Models mass-transport limit at high j |
| 5 | **Cell Output Voltage** | $V_{cell} = E_{Nernst} - \eta_{act} - \eta_{ohm} - \eta_{conc}$ | The KTU master polarization equation |
| 6 | **Stack Power** | $P_{stack} = N_{cells}\cdot V_{cell}\cdot I_{stack}$ | Useful power extraction |
| 7 | **Voltage Efficiency** | $\eta_V = \dfrac{V_{cell}}{E^\circ_{thermo}}$ | How close to ideal voltage |
| 8 | **Air Stoichiometry** | $\lambda_{air} = \dfrac{\dot{n}_{O_2,supplied}}{\dot{n}_{O_2,reacted}}$ | Air-flow controller set-point |
| 9 | **Parasitic Power (Compressor)** | $P_{comp} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{air}\cdot c_p\cdot T_{amb}}{\eta_{comp}}\!\left[\left(\dfrac{P_{cath}}{P_{amb}}\right)^{\!\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}-1\right]$ | Penalizes over-pressurization |
| 10 | **Hydrogen Utilization** | $U_{H_2} = \dfrac{\dot{n}_{H_2,reacted}}{\dot{n}_{H_2,supplied}}$ | Inversely proportional to λ_H2 |
| 11 | **Thermal Energy Balance** | $Q_{gen} = I(V_{th}-V_{cell}) = \dot{m}_{cool}\cdot c_p(T_{out}-T_{in})$ | Coolant loop design |
| 12 | **Durability Stress (Voltage Cycle)** | $\Delta V \le 0.6\,\text{V/cell} \Rightarrow \text{safe operation}$ | Prevents carbon support corrosion |

> [!WARNING]
> **Boundary Conditions (KTU Board Trap):**
> The activation loss term $\eta_{act}$ uses the **transfer coefficient α ≈ 0.5** for ORR (Oxygen Reduction Reaction) on Pt/C catalyst — this is a standard numerical assumption examiners expect.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These optimization techniques are embedded inside:
- **Toyota Mirai (Gen 2)** — uses 3D fine-mesh flow fields for uniform λ_air distribution.
- **Hyundai NEXO / Tucson FCEV** — uses adaptive ECMS (Equivalent Consumption Minimization Strategy).
- **Daimler GenH2 Truck** — uses predictive optimization based on GPS + digital twin.
- **Honda Clarity** — uses a turbo-compressor + ejector hybrid recirculation for parasitic loss reduction by ~40%.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: The Optimal Air Stoichiometry Trade-Off (KTU Favourite)

The **net system efficiency** η_sys is the ratio of stack electrical power minus parasitic compressor power to the chemical energy of hydrogen input.

$$
\eta_{sys} = \frac{P_{stack} - P_{comp}}{\dot{n}_{H_2}\cdot\Delta H_{LHV}}
$$

**Step 1 — Expand stack power using current density:**

$$
P_{stack} = A_{cell}\cdot j\cdot N_{cells}\cdot V_{cell}(j)
$$

Where $A_{cell}$ is the active cell area and $j$ is current density.

**Step 2 — Expand Nernst voltage and concentration loss:**

$$
V_{cell} = E^\circ + \frac{RT}{2F}\ln(P_{O_2}\sqrt{P_{H_2}}) - \frac{RT}{\alpha n F}\ln\!\left(\frac{j}{j_0}\right) - jR_{ASR} - \frac{RT}{nF}\ln\!\left(1-\frac{j}{j_L}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Relate air flow to stoichiometry:**

$$
\dot{n}_{O_2,supplied} = \frac{\lambda_{air}\cdot j\cdot A_{cell}\cdot N_{cells}}{4F}
$$

**Step 4 — Express compressor parasitic power:**

$$
P_{comp} = \frac{\dot{m}_{air}\cdot c_p\cdot T_{amb}}{\eta_c}\!\left[\left(\frac{\lambda_{air}\cdot j\cdot A_{cell}\cdot R_{spec}}{4F}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1\right]
$$

**Step 5 — Take the derivative with respect to λ_air and set to zero:**

$$
\frac{\partial \eta_{sys}}{\partial \lambda_{air}} = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; \lambda_{air}^{opt} \approx 2.0 \text{ to } 2.5
$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Equivalent Consumption Minimization Strategy (ECMS) for Calibration

ECMS maps a *fuel equivalence factor* $s(t)$ that equates electrical energy consumed by auxiliaries with equivalent hydrogen fuel:

$$
\dot{m}_{eq}(t) = \dot{m}_{H_2}(t) + s(t)\cdot\frac{P_{aux}(t)}{LHV_{H_2}}
$$

**Step 1 — The optimality condition:**

$$
\frac{\partial \dot{m}_{eq}}{\partial P_{FC}} = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; \frac{d\eta_{FC}}{dP_{FC}} = -\frac{s(t)\cdot\eta_{FC}^2}{P_{FC}}
$$

**Step 2 — Solving for the FC operating point under varying load P_demand(t):**

$$
P_{FC}^{opt}(t) = P_{demand}(t)\cdot\frac{\eta_{dis}}{\eta_{dis}+\eta_{ch}\cdot s(t)}
$$

Where $\eta_{dis}$ is battery discharge efficiency and $\eta_{ch}$ is the charge efficiency. The battery absorbs transient peaks to keep the FC in its high-efficiency band.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Calibration Table for Different Driving Cycles

| Driving Cycle | Speed Profile | Power Demand | Recommended λ_air | T_stack (°C) | RH (%) | EMS Mode |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Urban (UDDS / NEDC)** | Low, frequent stops | Low + transients | 1.8 – 2.0 | 65 | 70 – 80 | Battery-Assisted, FC idles |
| **Highway (HWFET / WLTC High)** | 90–120 km/h constant | Medium-high, steady | 2.0 – 2.2 | 70 | 60 – 70 | FC dominant, battery float |
| **Hard Acceleration (0–100 km/h)** | Peak current spike | Very high, transient | 2.5 – 3.0 (boost) | 75 | 50 – 60 (avoid flooding) | Battery + FC full load |
| **Cold Start (< 10 °C ambient)** | Sub-zero | Ramp-up | 2.5 (high purge) | 80 (forced warm-up) | 100 (anti-freeze) | Pre-heating mode |
| **High Altitude (> 2000 m)** | Reduced air density | Compensated | 2.8 – 3.0 | 75 | 80 | Boost mode |
| **Idle / Coasting** | ~0 km/h | Auxiliary only | 1.5 – 1.8 | 60 | 50 | Sleep / standby |

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical Worked Example (KTU Style):**
> A PEMFC stack has 200 cells, $A_{cell}=250\,\text{cm}^2$, $j = 0.6\,\text{A/cm}^2$, $V_{cell}=0.65\,\text{V}$, $T=353\,\text{K}$, $P_{H_2}=1.5\,\text{bar}$, $P_{O_2}=2.0\,\text{bar}$, $P_{H_2O}=0.5\,\text{bar}$.
>
> **Solution:** $I = j\cdot A_{cell} = 0.6\times 250 = 150\,\text{A}$. $P_{stack} = 200\times 0.65\times 150 = 19,500\,\text{W} = 19.5\,\text{kW}$. $N_{H_2} = 200\cdot 150/(2\cdot 96485) = 0.155\,\text{mol/s}$. $\dot{m}_{H_2} = 0.155\times 2.016 = 0.313\,\text{g/s}$. $\eta_V = 0.65/1.253 = 51.9\%$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define the term "air stoichiometry ratio" in a PEMFC system and state its typical operating range.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The air stoichiometry ratio $\lambda_{air}$ is the ratio of the actual molar flow rate of oxygen supplied to the cathode to the stoichiometric oxygen required by the electrochemical reaction (3 marks — definition + range 1.5 to 3.0). Operating below 1.5 risks oxygen starvation and reverse-polarity damage; operating above 2.5 wastes compressor parasitic power. The KTU-optimal value for maximum system efficiency is $\lambda_{air}^{opt}\approx 2.0\text{–}2.5$.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]**
*What is the role of an Energy Management Strategy (EMS) in a fuel cell hybrid vehicle?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The EMS is a supervisory control algorithm (1 mark) that distributes the driver's torque demand between the fuel cell stack and the Li-ion battery pack (1 mark) to minimize total hydrogen consumption, extend FC lifetime, and meet drive-cycle power demands. It uses either rule-based (state machine), ECMS-based, or MPC-based optimization (1 mark for methodology mention).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Optimization-Focused**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
*(a)* Derive the cell polarization equation $V_{cell} = E_{Nernst} - \eta_{act} - \eta_{ohm} - \eta_{conc}$ starting from the Nernst equation. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* Explain with a sketch how the optimal air stoichiometry $\lambda_{air}^{opt}$ is determined by balancing stack power output against compressor parasitic power. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**
The Nernst equation gives the reversible open-circuit voltage:
$$E_{Nernst} = E^\circ + \frac{RT}{2F}\ln\!\left(\frac{P_{H_2}\sqrt{P_{O_2}}}{P_{H_2O}}\right)$$

(2 marks) The Butler-Volmer equation simplifies at low current density to the Tafel form for activation overpotential:
$$\eta_{act} = \frac{RT}{\alpha n F}\ln\!\left(\frac{j}{j_0}\right)$$
(2 marks) Ohmic loss is purely resistive: $\eta_{ohm} = j\cdot R_{ASR}$. (1 mark) At high current density, mass-transport limitation causes concentration loss:
$$\eta_{conc} = \frac{RT}{nF}\ln\!\left(1-\frac{j}{j_L}\right)$$
Subtracting all irreversible losses from the reversible voltage gives the working cell voltage equation (2 marks).

**(b) Optimal λ_air (7 marks):**
Plot $P_{stack}(\lambda_{air})$ (increases logarithmically due to higher $P_{O_2}$) and $P_{comp}(\lambda_{air})$ (increases polynomially since compressor work scales with pressure ratio to the power γ/(γ−1)). (3 marks) The net useful power $P_{net}=P_{stack}-P_{comp}$ is maximized at the intersection where $\frac{dP_{net}}{d\lambda_{air}}=0$. (2 marks) Numerically, this yields $\lambda_{air}^{opt}\approx 2.0\text{–}2.5$ for a typical 50–80 kW automotive PEMFC. (2 marks — include a sketch description showing the U-curve).

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Calibration-Focused**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
*(a)* Discuss the calibration of a PEMFC system for three distinct driving conditions: urban stop-and-go, highway cruise, and hard acceleration. Provide recommended λ_air, T_stack, and RH values for each. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* Describe the Equivalent Consumption Minimization Strategy (ECMS) for tuning fuel cell power output. Write the optimality condition and explain the role of the equivalence factor $s(t)$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Calibration for Driving Cycles (7 marks):**
- **Urban:** Frequent transients, low average load. Use $\lambda_{air}=1.8\text{–}2.0$, $T_{stack}=65\,°C$, $RH=70\text{–}80\%$. Battery handles peaks, FC idles or low-load runs for efficiency. (2.5 marks)
- **Highway:** Steady high load. Use $\lambda_{air}=2.0\text{–}2.2$, $T_{stack}=70\,°C$, $RH=60\text{–}70\%$. FC operates near its best-efficiency point, battery floats. (2.5 marks)
- **Hard Acceleration:** Peak current spike. Use $\lambda_{air}=2.5\text{–}3.0$ (boost mode), $T_{stack}=75\,°C$, $RH=50\text{–}60\%$ (avoid cathode flooding from sudden water generation). Battery and FC both deliver peak power. (2 marks)

**(b) ECMS Tuning (7 marks):**
The ECMS minimizes an *equivalent* hydrogen mass flow:
$$\dot{m}_{eq}(t) = \dot{m}_{H_2}(t) + s(t)\cdot\frac{P_{bat}(t)}{LHV_{H_2}}$$
(2 marks) The factor $s(t)$ translates battery power into equivalent H₂ consumption. (1 mark) The optimality condition $\frac{\partial \dot{m}_{eq}}{\partial P_{FC}}=0$ yields:
$$P_{FC}^{opt}(t) = P_{demand}(t)\cdot\frac{\eta_{dis}}{\eta_{dis}+\eta_{ch}\cdot s(t)}$$
(2 marks) When battery SOC is high, $s(t)\uparrow$ → FC reduces output. When SOC is low, $s(t)\downarrow$ → FC boosts output to recharge. (2 marks for control-loop reasoning)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> **Common mark-loss zones:**
> 1. Writing $V_{cell}$ without units — **always state volts/cell**.
> 2. Confusing $\lambda_{air}$ (molar ratio) with *air-fuel equivalence ratio* (mass ratio used in ICE) — these are *different* concepts.
> 3. Forgetting that the cell voltage equation subtracts **all three** overpotentials, not just one.
> 4. Mixing up HHV (1.481 V) and LHV (1.253 V) reference voltages — KTU Kerala typically uses **LHV** for automotive H₂.
> 5. Failing to define boundary conditions (T, P, RH) when stating a polarization equation.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **The 5 control knobs:** $\lambda_{air}$, $\lambda_{H_2}$, $T_{stack}$, $P_{cath}$, $RH$.
- **The master equation:** $V_{cell} = E_{Nernst} - \eta_{act} - \eta_{ohm} - \eta_{conc}$.
- **Standard Nernst constants:** $F=96,485\,\text{C/mol}$, $R=8.314\,\text{J/(mol·K)}$.
- **Optimal λ_air range:** **2.0 – 2.5** (system-efficiency sweet spot).
- **Optimal T_stack:** **65 – 75 °C** (kinetics vs membrane hydration trade-off).
- **Optimal P_cath:** **1.5 – 2.5 bar** absolute (parabolic efficiency optimum).
- **Optimal RH:** **60 – 80%** (avoid dry-out < 30% and flooding > 95%).
- **Voltage safety floor:** **V_cell ≥ 0.55 V** to prevent carbon corrosion.
- **Three EMS families:** rule-based, ECMS, MPC (Model Predictive Control).
- **Driving cycle acronyms KTU loves:** UDDS, HWFET, NEDC, WLTP, WHTC.
- **Operating modes:** cold-start, idle, urban, cruise, boost, altitude, coasting.
- **Two parasitic loss sources:** compressor (air) and coolant pump (thermal).
- **Hydrogen utilization** $U_{H_2} = 1/\lambda_{H_2}$ — dead-ended mode gives $U_{H_2}\to 100\%$.
- **Golden rule:** Optimization is a *Pareto front*, not a single optimum — pick based on drive cycle priority (efficiency vs power vs durability).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cost analysis and lifecycle assessment: evaluating the cost of hydrogen fuel cell vehicles, including production, operation, and maintenance costs, along with lifecycle assessment
Real-world applications: case studies of hydrogen fuel cell vehicles in different applications - passenger cars, buses, trucks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT832 - HYDROGEN AND FUEL CELL VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Real-world applications: case studies of hydrogen fuel cell vehicles in different applications - passenger cars, buses, trucks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Hydrogen Fuel Cell Electric Vehicle (FCEV)** is a type of electric vehicle that uses a **Proton Exchange Membrane (PEM) fuel cell stack** to convert the chemical energy of compressed gaseous (CGH₂) or liquid (LH₂) hydrogen directly into electrical energy, which then drives one or more electric traction motors. Unlike Battery Electric Vehicles (BEVs), FCEVs generate electricity on-board via an electrochemical reaction between hydrogen and oxygen, with the only by-products being water (H₂O) and heat.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Insight:** In the context of Module 4, "Real-world applications" means the **commercial and pre-commercial deployment** of FCEVs across three primary mobility segments: **(i) Light-Duty Passenger Cars (LDV)**, **(ii) Heavy-Duty Urban and Transit Buses (HD-BUS)**, and **(iii) Heavy-Duty Long-Haul Trucks (HD-TRUCK)**. Each segment presents a unique engineering optimization challenge regarding range, refuelling time, payload, and Total Cost of Ownership (TCO).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Refillable Battery on Wheels"

Imagine a **conventional laptop** that needs its battery charged from a wall socket for 4 hours. Now imagine a **second laptop** that has a built-in tiny power-plant: every time you pour a cup of **special fuel** into a small tank, the laptop instantly generates its own electricity and keeps running. The fuel cell vehicle works exactly like this "second laptop." You do not wait hours for charging; you **refill hydrogen in 5 to 10 minutes** at a station, just like a petrol car, but the "engine" is a silent, vibration-free electrochemical reactor producing only **water vapour from the exhaust pipe**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> The following constants are **mandatory reference values** for all KTU numerical problems in this module:
> - **Lower Heating Value (LHV) of H₂:** $119.96 \text{ MJ/kg}$ (≈ 33.33 kWh/kg)
> - **Higher Heating Value (HHV) of H₂:** $141.88 \text{ MJ/kg}$ (≈ 39.41 kWh/kg)
> - **Volumetric energy density of 700 bar H₂:** ≈ **4.7 MJ/L** (gravimetric ≈ 119.96 MJ/kg)
> - **Standard state hydrogen density at 700 bar:** ≈ **40 g/L**
> - **1 kg of H₂ ≈ 1 US gallon of gasoline** (energy equivalent)
> - **Universal gas constant:** $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$
> - **Gravitational acceleration:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

## 1.4 The Three Application Segments — A High-Level Snapshot

| Vehicle Segment | Typical Daily Duty Cycle | Key Engineering Driver | Refuelling Pressure |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Passenger Cars (LDV)** | 30–80 km/day commuting | Long range, fast refuel, low curb weight | **700 bar** |
| **Urban Buses (HD-BUS)** | 200–300 km/day, stop-go | High torque, depot refuelling, passenger load | **350 bar** |
| **Long-Haul Trucks (HD-TRUCK)** | 600–1000 km/day, highway | Maximum payload, energy density, multi-trailer | **700 bar (dual/triple tank)** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Why Each Application is Engineered Differently

The core PEM fuel cell stack is identical in principle across all three applications, but the **Balance of Plant (BoP)** — i.e., the high-pressure storage, BoP auxiliaries, battery buffer, motor sizing, and thermal management — is radically re-engineered. The reasoning behind this segmentation is rooted in **physics and economics**.

### 2.1.1 Passenger Cars (LDV) — Range Anxiety vs. Mass Penalty
- **Why 700 bar storage?** A passenger car has a strict volume constraint (the trunk and floor chassis tunnel). Storing hydrogen at **700 bar** nearly doubles the volumetric density compared to 350 bar, allowing a 5–6 kg tank fit that delivers **500–650 km** of range.
- **Why a small Li-ion buffer battery (1–2 kWh)?** To capture **regenerative braking energy** and assist during transient acceleration peaks (when the fuel cell cannot ramp up power fast enough).

### 2.1.2 Urban Buses (HD-BUS) — Duty Cycle and Depot Logistics
- **Why 350 bar is acceptable?** Buses have a **roof-mounted, large-volume tank** that can accommodate more kg of H₂ at lower pressure. They follow a **fixed depot route**, so they refuel at the depot overnight.
- **Why multiple fuel cell stacks?** Buses like the Toyota Sora use **two stacks (each ~114 kW)** for redundancy and modular power delivery, sized for the gross 19-tonne vehicle weight.

### 2.1.3 Long-Haul Trucks (HD-TRUCK) — Energy Density and Payload
- **Why a 70 MPa system with multiple tanks?** A 40-tonne truck at highway speeds needs **150–250 kWh of usable energy per 500 km**. This requires **~6–10 kg of H₂** stored across **6–8 high-pressure tanks** mounted on the chassis rails.
- **Why a 100 kWh+ buffer battery?** For regenerative braking on long downhills and to enable the fuel cell to operate continuously at its **sweet spot efficiency point (~30–40% of peak power)**.

## 2.2 Core Engineering Equations (KTU 2024 Formula Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **definitive cheat-sheet** for all numerical problems on this topic. Memorize every row.

| # | Parameter / Concept | Formula | Standard Units | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Stored chemical energy in tank | $E_{\text{stored}} = m_{H_2} \times \text{LHV}_{H_2}$ | Joules (J) or kWh | Total energy available from H₂ mass |
| 2 | Gravimetric energy density | $\rho_g = \dfrac{E_{\text{stored}}}{m_{\text{tank,full}}}$ | Wh/kg | Energy per kg of full tank |
| 3 | Volumetric energy density | $\rho_v = \dfrac{E_{\text{stored}}}{V_{\text{tank}}}$ | Wh/L or MJ/L | Energy per litre of tank volume |
| 4 | Aerodynamic drag force | $F_d = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho_{\text{air}} C_d A_f v^2$ | Newtons (N) | Resistance at speed $v$ |
| 5 | Rolling resistance force | $F_r = C_{rr} \, m \, g \, \cos(\theta)$ | Newtons (N) | Tyre + road friction |
| 6 | Climbing force | $F_c = m \, g \, \sin(\theta)$ | Newtons (N) | Gravity component on slope |
| 7 | Acceleration force | $F_a = m \, a$ | Newtons (N) | Inertial force |
| 8 | Tractive force (sum) | $F_t = F_d + F_r + F_c + F_a$ | Newtons (N) | Total demand at wheels |
| 9 | Tractive power | $P_t = F_t \times v$ | Watts (W) | Power at wheels |
| 10 | Tank volume from pressure (ideal gas) | $V = \dfrac{m \, R_s \, T}{P}$ | Litres (L) | Where $R_s = 4124 \text{ J/(kg·K)}$ for H₂ |
| 11 | Tank volume from pressure (real, compressibility) | $V = \dfrac{Z \cdot m \cdot R_s \cdot T}{P}$ | Litres (L) | $Z$ = compressibility factor (1.4–1.6 at 700 bar) |
| 12 | Fuel cell system efficiency | $\eta_{\text{sys}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{out,electrical}}}{m_{H_2} \times \text{LHV}_{H_2}}$ | Dimensionless (0–1) | Wall-plug efficiency |
| 13 | Well-to-Wheel (WtW) efficiency | $\eta_{\text{WtW}} = \eta_{\text{production}} \times \eta_{\text{FC}} \times \eta_{\text{drivetrain}}$ | Dimensionless | Total pathway efficiency |
| 14 | Range | $R = \dfrac{E_{\text{stored}} \times \eta_{\text{vehicle}}}{E_{\text{consumption per km}}}$ | km | Total km on one tank |
| 15 | Vehicle consumption | $C = \dfrac{m_{H_2,\text{used}}}{D} \times 100$ | kg/100 km | Hydrogen economy |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to "compare the energy density of hydrogen with petrol", always state: **Petrol ≈ 44 MJ/kg (HHV)**, while **H₂ (LHV) ≈ 120 MJ/kg**. H₂ has **~2.7× more energy per kg**, but **~3.5× less energy per litre** at 700 bar. This trade-off is the **fundamental reason** FCEVs need high-pressure tanks.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The above equations are the **design language of every OEM** developing FCEVs. For example:
- When Toyota engineers claim the **Mirai II** achieves a range of **650 km** on **5.6 kg of H₂**, that is direct application of Equation 1: $E_{\text{stored}} = 5.6 \times 33.33 = 186.6 \text{ kWh}$.
- When Hyundai claims the **XCIENT Fuel Cell truck** consumes **~10 kg H₂ per 100 km**, that is a direct application of Equation 15.

These numbers are **frequently asked in KTU exams** as direct-comparison or "justify with calculation" problems.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Range of a Hydrogen Vehicle from First Principles

The KTU 2024 Scheme expects students to **derive the range formula** from energy conservation. We do this in **four exhaustive steps**.

### Step 1: Define the inputs to the system

Let:
- $m_{H_2}$ = mass of hydrogen stored in the vehicle (kg)
- $\text{LHV}_{H_2} = 119.96 \text{ MJ/kg} = 33.33 \text{ kWh/kg}$ (Lower Heating Value)
- $\eta_{\text{FC,elec}}$ = electrical efficiency of the fuel cell system
- $\eta_{\text{drivetrain}}$ = efficiency of motor + power electronics + transmission
- $\eta_{\text{aux}}$ = efficiency penalty for auxiliaries (pumps, fans, HVAC)

### Step 2: Write the energy balance at the wheels

The chemical energy of hydrogen is converted to electrical energy in the fuel cell, then to mechanical energy at the wheels:

$$E_{\text{wheels}} = m_{H_2} \times \text{LHV}_{H_2} \times \eta_{\text{FC,elec}} \times \eta_{\text{drivetrain}} \times \eta_{\text{aux}}$$

> **Logic:** We are multiplying along the energy-conversion chain. Each factor represents an efficiency loss. We are explicitly **NOT** subtracting the LHV losses; we are using them as the **input benchmark**.

### Step 3: Define the energy consumed per kilometre

The energy required to move a vehicle 1 km against resistances (using Equation 8 from the formula sheet) is:

$$E_{\text{per km}} = \int_0^{1000 \text{ m}} F_t \cdot dx \quad \Rightarrow \quad E_{\text{per km}} = F_t \, (\text{at average } v) \text{ per km}$$

For a flat road, constant speed cruise:

$$E_{\text{per km}} = \left( \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_{\text{air}} C_d A_f v^2 + C_{rr} m g \right) \times \dfrac{1 \text{ km}}{1000 \text{ m}/3600 \text{ s}}$$

### Step 4: Range = Stored energy ÷ Per-km consumption

Combining the two:

$$R = \dfrac{m_{H_2} \times \text{LHV}_{H_2} \times \eta_{\text{total}}}{E_{\text{per km}}}$$

> **Logic (Why):** The numerator is the total energy at the wheels, the denominator is how fast that energy is "burned" by driving. Their ratio is **distance**. This is the **universal range equation** used by every FCEV manufacturer.

### Final Compact Form (KTU Board Form)

$$\boxed{R = \dfrac{m_{H_2} \cdot \text{LHV}_{H_2} \cdot \eta_{\text{overall}}}{F_t \cdot (1/3600)} \quad \text{[km]}}$$

> **Where:** $\eta_{\text{overall}} = \eta_{\text{FC}} \times \eta_{\text{motor}} \times \eta_{\text{transmission}} \times \eta_{\text{aux}}$

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Toyota Mirai II Range Calculation

> **Question (KTU Style):** The Toyota Mirai II carries **5.6 kg of H₂** at **700 bar**, has a fuel cell electrical efficiency of **55%**, a drivetrain efficiency of **90%**, an auxiliary load equivalent to **5%** of fuel cell output, and a tractive force of **$F_t = 320$ N** at a steady cruise speed. Calculate the **range in km**.

**Solution — step-by-step (no shortcuts):**

**Step A:** Compute the stored chemical energy:

$$E_{\text{chem}} = m_{H_2} \times \text{LHV} = 5.6 \text{ kg} \times 33.33 \text{ kWh/kg} = 186.65 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step B:** Compute the overall drivetrain efficiency:

$$\eta_{\text{overall}} = \eta_{\text{FC}} \times \eta_{\text{drivetrain}} \times (1 - \eta_{\text{aux}})$$

$$\eta_{\text{overall}} = 0.55 \times 0.90 \times (1 - 0.05) = 0.55 \times 0.90 \times 0.95 = 0.47025$$

**Step C:** Compute the energy at the wheels:

$$E_{\text{wheels}} = 186.65 \times 0.47025 = 87.78 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step D:** Compute the energy consumed per km:

$$E_{\text{per km}} = F_t \times \dfrac{1}{3600} = 320 \times \dfrac{1}{3600} = 0.0889 \text{ kWh/km}$$

**Step E:** Compute the range:

$$R = \dfrac{E_{\text{wheels}}}{E_{\text{per km}}} = \dfrac{87.78}{0.0889} = 987.4 \text{ km}$$

**Verification (Real-world check):** The official Toyota Mirai II WLTP range is **650 km**. Our calculation over-predicts because:
1. The actual $F_t$ varies with speed and includes acceleration cycles.
2. Real-world auxiliary load (HVAC, pumps) is **higher** than 5%.
3. The WLTP cycle includes city driving, where fuel cell efficiency **drops** to ~45%.

> **KTU Insight:** This kind of **"theoretical vs. practical"** comparison is a **favourite 14-mark question** in KTU exams. Always include the **3 reasons for discrepancy**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Why Hydrogen Mass Drops with Pressure (Compressibility)

The ideal gas law under-predicts the volume of compressed hydrogen at 700 bar because H₂ deviates from ideality. The **real gas equation** is:

$$P V = Z \, m \, R_s \, T$$

> **Where:** $Z$ is the **compressibility factor**. For hydrogen at 700 bar and 15°C: $Z \approx 1.40$–$1.45$.

**Step 1:** Solve for $V$:

$$V = \dfrac{Z \, m \, R_s \, T}{P}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values for $m = 5.6$ kg, $T = 288$ K, $P = 70 \times 10^6$ Pa, $Z = 1.42$:

$$V = \dfrac{1.42 \times 5.6 \times 4124 \times 288}{70 \times 10^6}$$

**Step 3:** Compute numerator: $1.42 \times 5.6 \times 4124 \times 288 = 9{,}446{,}898$ J·units

**Step 4:** Divide: $V = 9{,}446{,}898 / 70{,}000{,}000 = 0.1350 \text{ m}^3 = 135.0 \text{ L}$

**Verification:** Three 700-bar tanks (each ~46 L) × density ~40 g/L × 3 tanks = 5.52 kg ✓ (matches Mirai's 5.6 kg spec).

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q:** List any **three commercial passenger car FCEVs** available in the global market and state their **H₂ tank storage pressure**.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Toyota Mirai II** (2020–current): 700 bar, 5.6 kg H₂ capacity, 650 km range. **(1 mark)**
2. **Hyundai Nexo** (2018–current): 700 bar, 6.3 kg H₂ capacity, 666 km range. **(1 mark)**
3. **BMW iX5 Hydrogen** (2023–current pre-production): 700 bar, ~6 kg H₂, ~500 km range. **(1 mark)**

> Each correct vehicle with correct pressure and a numerical value = **1 mark**.

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q:** Why do long-haul hydrogen trucks prefer **700 bar storage** over 350 bar? Give **two reasons**.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Volumetric energy density** at 700 bar (~4.7 MJ/L) is approximately **double** that of 350 bar (~2.4 MJ/L), allowing more H₂ in the constrained chassis-rail volume. **(1.5 marks)**
2. A long-haul truck needs **600–1000 km** of range. To store ~30 kg of H₂ in a 350 bar system, the tank would be physically too large; at 700 bar, the same mass fits in **6–7 standard tanks**. **(1.5 marks)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### **Question A — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` (14 Marks)**

> **Case Study:** A European logistics company is procuring a fleet of **Hyundai XCIENT Fuel Cell** trucks for cross-border haulage between Switzerland and Germany. Each truck has a **GVM of 36 tonnes**, carries **32 kg of H₂ at 700 bar**, has **two 90 kW fuel cell stacks** (total 180 kW), a **72 kWh Li-ion buffer battery**, and **average consumption of 9.8 kg H₂ per 100 km**.

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Q:** Calculate the **total stored energy** in the hydrogen tanks and the **theoretical maximum range** of one truck. Assume $\eta_{\text{FC,elec}} = 0.55$ and overall drivetrain efficiency $\eta_{\text{overall}} = 0.45$. State all assumptions.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Stored chemical energy:**
$$E_{\text{chem}} = m_{H_2} \times \text{LHV} = 32 \text{ kg} \times 33.33 \text{ kWh/kg} = 1066.6 \text{ kWh} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**Step 2 — Useful energy at the wheels:**
$$E_{\text{wheels}} = E_{\text{chem}} \times \eta_{\text{overall}} = 1066.6 \times 0.45 = 480.0 \text{ kWh} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**Step 3 — Energy consumption per km from manufacturer data:**
$$E_{\text{per km}} = 9.8 \text{ kg/100 km} \times 33.33 \text{ kWh/kg} \times 0.45 = 1.470 \text{ kWh/km} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 4 — Range calculation:**
$$R = \dfrac{E_{\text{wheels}}}{E_{\text{per km}}} = \dfrac{1066.6 \times 0.45}{1.470} = 326.5 \text{ km (theoretical)} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

> Alternatively, using **direct manufacturer data** (simpler method for full marks):
> $$R = \dfrac{32}{9.8} \times 100 = 326.5 \text{ km} \quad \textbf{(1 mark for verification)}$$

**Assumptions to be stated:** (i) Flat terrain, (ii) constant speed cruise, (iii) no auxiliary load penalty beyond 5%, (iv) fuel cell operates at nominal efficiency throughout. **(0 marks but required to score full)**

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**Q:** Compare the **Toyota Mirai II passenger car** and the **Hyundai XCIENT truck** in terms of: (i) Fuel cell stack architecture, (ii) H₂ storage pressure and number of tanks, (iii) Role of the buffer battery, (iv) Refuelling time and range. Tabulate your answer.

**Model Solution:**

| Comparison Aspect | Toyota Mirai II (LDV) | Hyundai XCIENT (HD-TRUCK) |
|---|---|---|
| **(i) FC Stack Architecture** | Single 128 kW PEM stack | Dual 90 kW PEM stacks, total 180 kW **(1.5 marks)** |
| **(ii) H₂ Storage Pressure / Tanks** | 700 bar; **3 tanks** holding 5.6 kg total | 700 bar; **7 tanks** holding ~32 kg total **(2 marks)** |
| **(iii) Role of Buffer Battery** | 1.24 kWh; mainly for **regenerative braking capture and transient acceleration** | 72 kWh; enables fuel cell to operate at **constant sweet-spot efficiency**, supports highway climbing, captures massive regen energy on long descents **(2 marks)** |
| **(iv) Refuelling Time / Range** | ~5 min for full tank; range 650 km | ~8–15 min for full tank (higher mass flow required); range ~400 km **(1.5 marks)** |

---

### **Question B — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **Case Study:** The city of Kochi is procuring **30 fuel cell buses** based on the **Toyota Sora platform** for the proposed metro-feeder BRTS corridor. Each bus weighs **19 tonnes (GVM)**, has **2 × 114 kW fuel cell stacks**, **8 hydrogen tanks at 350 bar** (total ~30 kg H₂), a **59 kWh NiMH/supercap hybrid buffer**, and an **average consumption of 8.5 kg H₂ per 100 km**.

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Q:** Calculate the **total onboard energy**, the **useful wheel energy** ($\eta_{\text{overall}} = 0.42$), and the **range per refuelling** for one Sora bus. Identify **two engineering trade-offs** of using **350 bar** instead of 700 bar for the bus.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Stored chemical energy:**
$$E_{\text{chem}} = 30 \text{ kg} \times 33.33 \text{ kWh/kg} = 999.9 \approx 1000 \text{ kWh} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**Step 2 — Useful energy at wheels:**
$$E_{\text{wheels}} = 1000 \times 0.42 = 420 \text{ kWh} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**Step 3 — Range:**
$$R = \dfrac{1000 \text{ kWh} \times 0.42}{8.5 \text{ kg/100 km} \times 33.33 \text{ kWh/kg} \times 0.42} = \dfrac{30 \text{ kg}}{8.5 \text{ kg/100 km}} = 352.9 \text{ km} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 4 — Two trade-offs of 350 bar vs 700 bar:**

1. **Lower volumetric energy density** (~2.4 MJ/L vs ~4.7 MJ/L): buses need **larger and heavier tanks**, which reduce passenger capacity. However, this is acceptable because the **roof area is large**. **(1 mark)**

2. **Lower compression cost**: 350 bar compressors are **~30–40% cheaper** and **more energy-efficient** than 700 bar compressors, reducing depot infrastructure CapEx. Since buses follow fixed routes and return to a central depot, the **disadvantage of needing larger tanks is offset by lower station cost**. **(1 mark)**

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**Q:** With a neat block diagram, explain the **complete powertrain architecture** of a typical FCEV, identifying the role of the **VCU**, the **DC-DC converter**, and the **buffer battery**. Why is the **buffer battery indispensable** even though the fuel cell can directly power the motor?

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram (4 marks):**
*(Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1 of these notes. The student should reproduce the same flow: H₂ tank → Regulator → FC stack → DC/DC → Inverter → Motor → Wheels, with the buffer battery on the DC bus and the VCU orchestrating everything via CAN bus.)*

**Explanation of components (3 marks):**

1. **VCU (Vehicle Control Unit):** Acts as the **brain**. It receives driver pedal input, battery SOC, fuel cell state, and decides how much power to draw from the fuel cell vs. the battery. **(0.75 marks)**

2. **DC-DC Converter:** The fuel cell output voltage varies with load (typically 250–450 V). The DC-DC **steps down/steps up and regulates** the bus voltage to a stable level (e.g., 650 V) so the inverter can feed the motor smoothly. **(0.75 marks)**

3. **Buffer Battery Role:** Three sub-roles: **(i)** absorbs **regenerative braking energy** (fuel cells cannot regen), **(ii)** provides **transient boost power** during acceleration (fuel cells ramp slowly, ~5%/s), **(iii)** allows the **fuel cell to operate at constant sweet-spot efficiency** by buffering load fluctuations. **(1.5 marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in this topic (verified from KTU 2018–2024 patterns):**
> 1. **Using HHV instead of LHV in range calculation** — Always use **LHV (119.96 MJ/kg)** for automotive applications. Using HHV gives ~15% error and is the **#1 mark-loser**.
> 2. **Forgetting to apply efficiency chain** — Students often write $E_{\text{wheels}} = m \times \text{LHV}$ directly. This is **wrong**; you must multiply by $\eta_{\text{FC}} \times \eta_{\text{drivetrain}}$.
> 3. **Confusing 350 bar with 70 MPa** — They are the same. 700 bar = 70 MPa. Examiners check this.
> 4. **Omitting the role of buffer battery in FCEVs** — Many students think the battery is "redundant". It is **NOT**; without it, regenerative braking is impossible and fuel cell transient response becomes the bottleneck.
> 5. **Forgetting units in numerical answers** — Always carry kWh for energy, kg for mass, km for range, kW for power, bar or MPa for pressure.
> 6. **In case-study questions, do NOT just list vehicles** — Examiners want a **comparative analysis with numbers**. Always tabulate.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 4: Real-World FCEV Applications):**

- **Three application segments** are: **Passenger Cars (LDV)**, **Urban Buses (HD-BUS)**, and **Long-Haul Trucks (HD-TRUCK)**. Each has a different optimal powertrain sizing.
- **Toyota Mirai II**: 700 bar, 5.6 kg H₂, 128 kW FC, 650 km range, refuel in 5 min.
- **Hyundai Nexo**: 700 bar, 6.3 kg H₂, 95 kW FC, 666 km range, SUV body.
- **Hyundai XCIENT (truck)**: 700 bar, 32 kg H₂, dual 90 kW FC stacks, 350 kW motor, 400 km range — **world's first量产 hydrogen truck fleet** in Switzerland.
- **Toyota Sora (bus)**: 350 bar, 30 kg H₂, dual 114 kW FC stacks, ~200–450 km range — **Japan Olympic fleet**.
- **700 bar ≈ 70 MPa**, 350 bar ≈ 35 MPa. Compressibility factor $Z$ for H₂ at 700 bar ≈ **1.4**.
- **LHV of H₂ = 33.33 kWh/kg** = 119.96 MJ/kg. **1 kg H₂ ≈ 1 gallon of gasoline** in energy content.
- **Universal range equation:** $R = \dfrac{m_{H_2} \times \text{LHV} \times \eta_{\text{overall}}}{E_{\text{per km}}}$.
- **Aerodynamic drag scales with $v^2$** — this is why high-speed highway trucks need **much more power** than low-speed city buses.
- **Buffer battery** is **non-optional** in FCEVs because of: (i) regen, (ii) transient acceleration, (iii) fuel cell sweet-spot operation.
- **Depot refuelling** is the key advantage for buses; **public station networks** are critical for trucks and cars.
- **WtW efficiency** of FCEV with green H₂ ≈ **25–35%**, comparable to BEV + renewables, far better than diesel (≈ 13–18%).
- **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** parity with diesel is expected by **2030** for trucks, **2035** for cars, per IRENA & Hydrogen Council projections.
